Always justify your material choice by **process + property + cost** triplet, not by property alone. This single habit can lift your score from "Good" to "Outstanding" in the KTU valuation scale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sand Casting - Typical Characteristics of a Sand Cast Part
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sand Casting - Typical Characteristics of a Sand Cast Part

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sand Casting** is a versatile, expendable-mold casting process in which molten metal is poured into a single-use sand mold (typically green sand or chemically bonded sand) shaped by a pattern, allowed to solidify, and then broken away to release the part. The **typical characteristics of a sand cast part** refer to the standardized geometric, dimensional, and process-related design attributes that a sand cast component must possess to ensure: (a) successful extraction of the pattern from the sand mold, (b) sound metallurgical structure, (c) economical post-casting operations, and (d) functional conformity with the design intent.

These characteristics are not arbitrary — they are codified by **KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 DFMA pedagogy** under the umbrella of **Design for Casting (DFC)** principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPET755 – Module 2):** The student must be able to identify and apply at least **eight (8) typical characteristics** of a sand cast part while making original design decisions in university examination problems. These characteristics are tied directly to **CO2**: *Apply DFMA principles for cast and forged components.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Jelly Mold" Analogy

Imagine you are trying to pour liquid chocolate into a shaped ice-cube tray. If you press your fingers straight down into the tray and try to pull them out, the chocolate gets dented or torn. But if you tilt your fingers slightly (giving them a "draft"), they slide out cleanly. The same logic governs every sand cast part:

- The **pattern** (your finger) must slide out of the **mold cavity** (the sand) without eroding its walls.
- Sharp internal corners concentrate heat and cause **hot tears** (cracks), so we round them — just like the rounded internal corners of a real-world plastic bucket that don't tear under stress.
- A very thin chocolate shell solidifies too fast and cracks; a very thick shell takes forever to cool. The same applies to **minimum wall thickness** in castings.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Eight Canonical Characteristics of a Sand Cast Part:**
> 1. Draft Angle (Taper)
> 2. Fillet & Corner Radii
> 3. Minimum Wall Thickness
> 4. Uniform Wall Thickness
> 5. Machining Allowance
> 6. Shrinkage Allowance
> 7. Dimensional Tolerance & Surface Finish
> 8. Parting Line, Cores, and Section Transitions (Ribs/Bosses)

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Typical Sand Casting Range |
|---|---|
| Linear Shrinkage Allowance | **0.5 % to 2 %** (alloy-dependent) |
| Draft Angle (external) | **1° to 2°** (small parts), **5° to 10°** (deep cavities) |
| Minimum Fillet Radius | **3 mm to 25 mm** |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | **3 mm (small)** to **12 mm (large parts)** |
| Machining Allowance | **1.5 mm to 6 mm per side** |
| Achievable Tolerance | **± 0.8 mm to ± 3 mm** (as-cast) |
| Surface Finish | **6.3 μm to 50 μm Ra** |
| Maximum Practical Part Mass | **Up to 200 tonnes** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Characteristic

Each characteristic exists because of a **specific manufacturing problem** it solves. Memorizing without understanding is the number-one reason KTU students lose marks on numerical problems.

#### A. Draft Angle (Taper)
- **Why?** To allow the pattern to be withdrawn from the sand mold without damaging mold walls. Sand has low cohesive strength compared to metal dies.
- **How?** Every vertical face perpendicular to the parting plane is tilted by an angle $\alpha$. External surfaces (away from mold cavity) and internal surfaces (into mold cavity) both need draft, but in opposite senses.
- **Rule of thumb:** $\alpha = 1° \text{ to } 2°$ for shallow pockets; **5° to 10°** for deep pockets (depth > 50 mm).

#### B. Fillet and Corner Radii
- **Why?** Sharp internal corners cause **stress concentration** in the casting during solidification → hot tears, shrinkage cracks.
- **How?** Internal corners are rounded with fillet radius $r$. External corners are also rounded (with smaller radius $R$) to reduce pattern wear.
- **Rule of thumb:** $r \geq 0.5 \cdot t$, where $t$ is the nominal wall thickness.

#### C. Minimum Wall Thickness
- **Why?** Walls that are too thin (a) don't fill completely (misrun), (b) cool too fast causing brittle microstructure. Walls that are too thick cause (a) shrinkage cavities, (b) longer cycle time.
- **How?** A minimum value is set based on casting size and alloy fluidity. For **ferrous castings**, it is larger than non-ferrous because of lower fluidity.

#### D. Uniform Wall Thickness
- **Why?** Non-uniform sections cool at different rates → internal stresses and warpage.
- **How?** Designers add cores, ribs, or chill pads to balance thermal mass. **No section should be more than 2× the thickness of adjacent sections** without mitigation.

#### E. Machining Allowance
- **Why?** As-cast surfaces are rough and dimensionally inaccurate; subsequent machining (milling, drilling) is required.
- **How?** Extra material is added on surfaces to be machined. Allowance per side: **1.5 mm (small) to 6 mm (large)**.

#### F. Shrinkage Allowance
- **Why?** Metals contract as they solidify and cool from solidus to room temperature.
- **How?** The pattern is made **larger** than the final casting by a linear shrinkage percentage $s$.

$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + s)$$

#### G. Dimensional Tolerance & Surface Finish
- **Why?** Sand grains give a characteristic texture (Ra 6.3–50 μm). Geometric imperfections are inherent.
- **How?** Tolerance grade is selected per **ISO 8062-3 (CT 9–CT 13 for sand casting)**. Tighter tolerances = higher cost.

#### H. Parting Line, Cores, Ribs & Bosses
- **Why?** The parting line defines mold halves. Cores create internal cavities. Ribs stiffen without increasing thickness. Bosses provide mounting points.
- **How?** Parting line chosen to minimize undercuts, simplify mold, and reduce flash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These characteristics are not just academic — they are embedded in industrial CAD packages like **Siemens NX Casting Toolkit, SolidWorks DFMXpress, and MAGMA** which automatically flag violations during design reviews. Every characteristic directly affects **Cost-of-Quality (CoQ)**: violating any one rule typically increases rejection rates by 15–30%.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table. KTU valuation keys allot partial credit for correctly identifying and applying the right formula even if the arithmetic slips.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Equation | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shrinkage Allowance | $L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + s)$ | $s = 0.005 \text{ to } 0.020$ | mm |
| 2 | Casting Dimension | $L_{\text{casting}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{pattern}}}{1 + s}$ | — | mm |
| 3 | Draft Displacement | $d = h \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | $\alpha = 1° \text{ to } 10°$ | mm |
| 4 | Machining Allowance (per side) | $MA = \dfrac{D_{\text{pattern}} - D_{\text{final}}}{2}$ | $1.5 \text{ to } 6$ | mm |
| 5 | Minimum Wall Thickness | $t_{\min} = f(\text{size}, \text{alloy})$ | $3 \text{ to } 12$ | mm |
| 6 | Fillet Radius Rule | $r \geq \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$ | $3 \text{ to } 25$ | mm |
| 7 | Section Thickness Ratio | $\dfrac{t_{\max}}{t_{\min}} \leq 2$ (no mitigation) | — | — |
| 8 | Volumetric Shrinkage | $\Delta V \approx 3 \cdot s \cdot V$ (linear approx.) | — | mm³ |
| 9 | Tolerance (ISO 8062-3) | $CT = f(L_{\text{nom}})$ | CT 9 – CT 13 | mm |
| 10 | Pattern Surface Finish Allowance | $R_a \approx 0.6 \cdot d_{g}^{0.5}$ ($d_g$ = sand grain dia) | — | μm |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Notation Safety:** In all written answers, **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` for absolute value — use `\lvert x \rvert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid markdown table rendering errors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of the Three Most-Tested Formulas

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of Pattern Dimension (Shrinkage Compensation)

Consider a casting whose final required dimension is $L_c$. The metal, upon cooling from the solidus temperature $T_s$ to room temperature $T_{\text{room}}$, contracts linearly by a fractional amount $s$ called the **linear shrinkage coefficient**.

The linear strain due to cooling is:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_c - L_0}{L_0} = -s$$

where $L_0$ is the dimension at the moment of solidification (effectively the mold cavity dimension) and $L_c$ is the final room-temperature dimension.

Rearranging:

$$L_0 = \dfrac{L_c}{1 - s}$$

But because $s \ll 1$ (typically $s \leq 0.02$), engineers approximate the pattern dimension as:

$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_c \cdot (1 + s)$$

This is the **standard KTU textbook formula**.

**Worked Example — KTU Style:**
A sand cast gear blank must have an outer diameter of **200 mm** at room temperature. Gray cast iron has $s = 1.0\%$. Find pattern diameter.

$$L_{\text{pattern}} = 200 \cdot (1 + 0.010) = 200 \cdot 1.010 = 202 \text{ mm}$$

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Draft Displacement

Consider a vertical mold wall of height $h$, inclined at a draft angle $\alpha$ from vertical. The horizontal shift at the top relative to the bottom is:

$$d = h \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Multi-step numerical evaluation:**

$$d = h \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 100 \cdot \tan(1.5°)$$

$$d = 100 \cdot 0.02619 = 2.619 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significant Figures Rule (KTU 2024):** Carry at least 3 decimal places through the calculation; round to **2 decimal places** in the final answer for marks.

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Machining Allowance

If a finished hole must be $\phi = 50$ mm after drilling, and the as-cast hole will be $\phi_{\text{cast}} = 52$ mm, then the **machining allowance per side** is:

$$MA = \dfrac{\phi_{\text{cast}} - \phi_{\text{final}}}{2} = \dfrac{52 - 50}{2} = 1.0 \text{ mm per side}$$

This represents the radial material removed during the drilling/reaming operation.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Draft & Corner Radii

The following **decision matrix** is a high-yield tool that KTU examiners look for in 14-mark answers.

| Part Size | Wall Thickness $t$ | Recommended Draft $\alpha$ | Min. Fillet Radius $r$ | Min. Wall Thickness $t_{\min}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small (< 150 mm) | 3–6 mm | 1° – 1.5° | 3 mm | 3 mm |
| Medium (150–600 mm) | 6–12 mm | 1° – 2° | 6 mm | 6 mm |
| Large (600–1500 mm) | 12–25 mm | 2° – 3° | 10 mm | 10 mm |
| Very Large (> 1500 mm) | > 25 mm | 3° – 5° | 15–25 mm | 12–15 mm |
| Deep Pockets (h > 50 mm) | any | **add 0.5° per 100 mm of depth** | as above | as above |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Problem — Combined Characteristics

> **Problem Statement:** A sand cast aluminum housing has a length of **300 mm**, height **120 mm**, and the deepest vertical wall pocket extends **80 mm** into the mold. Aluminum shrinkage $s = 1.3\%$. Draft required = $1.5°$ on all verticals. Final machined length must be 298 mm after facing.

**Step 1 — Pattern Length (before machining):**
$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + s) = 300 \cdot 1.013 = 303.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Total Machining Allowance Distributed:**
The casting must be **300 mm** as-cast. The facing operation removes material from both ends. If finished length is **298 mm**, total material removed = **2 mm**, so per side:
$$MA = \dfrac{300 - 298}{2} = 1.0 \text{ mm per side}$$

**Step 3 — Draft Displacement at Top of Deep Pocket:**
$$d = h \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 80 \cdot \tan(1.5°) = 80 \cdot 0.02619 = 2.0952 \text{ mm}$$

This is the horizontal offset between the top and bottom of the deepest pocket — the patternmaker uses this to machine the pattern taper.

**Step 4 — Volumetric Shrinkage Compensation (informational):**
$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \approx 3s = 3 \cdot 0.013 = 0.039 = 3.9\%$$
The riser system must supply at least **3.9 % extra molten volume** to compensate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer should explicitly list:** (i) Pattern length = 303.9 mm, (ii) MA per side = 1.0 mm, (iii) Draft displacement = 2.10 mm, (iv) Riser volumetric reserve = 3.9 % minimum. KTU evaluators award 1.5 marks per correct sub-step.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Typical Characteristics Taxonomy

The following diagram maps the **eight canonical characteristics** of a sand cast part to their design functions. It uses only alphanumeric node IDs and plain uppercase labels per Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SCP["SAND CAST PART TYPICAL CHARACTERISTICS"]
    SCP --> GF["GEOMETRIC FEATURES GROUP"]
    SCP --> DF["DIMENSIONAL FEATURES GROUP"]
    SCP --> PF["PROCESS FEATURES GROUP"]
    GF --> DA["DRAFT ANGLE 1 TO 10 DEG"]
    GF --> FR["FILLET AND CORNER RADII 3 TO 25 MM"]
    GF --> MW["MINIMUM WALL THICKNESS 3 TO 12 MM"]
    GF --> UW["UNIFORM WALL THICKNESS RATIO LESS THAN 2"]
    DF --> SA["SHRINKAGE ALLOWANCE 0.5 TO 2 PERCENT"]
    DF --> MA["MACHINING ALLOWANCE 1.5 TO 6 MM PER SIDE"]
    DF --> TL["DIMENSIONAL TOLERANCE CT 9 TO CT 13"]
    DF --> SF["SURFACE FINISH RA 6.3 TO 50 MICRON"]
    PF --> PL["PARTING LINE LOCATION"]
    PF --> CR["CORE DESIGN FOR INTERNAL CAVITIES"]
    PF --> RB["RIBS AND BOSSES FOR STIFFENING"]
    PF --> TS["THERMAL SECTION BALANCING"]
    DA --> WHY1["SOLVES PATTERN WITHDRAWAL PROBLEM"]
    FR --> WHY2["SOLVES HOT TEAR AND STRESS CONCENTRATION"]
    MW --> WHY3["SOLVES MISRUN AND SHRINKAGE DEFECTS"]
    SA --> WHY4["SOLVES THERMAL CONTRACTION DEFECT"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Verification Flow

This diagram shows how each characteristic flows through a **DFMA (Design for Manufacturing and Assembly)** verification cycle in a CAD environment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT 3D CAD MODEL"] --> B["CHARACTERISTIC CHECKER MODULE"]
    B --> C1["CHECK 1 DRAFT ANGLE VALIDATION"]
    B --> C2["CHECK 2 FILLET RADIUS VALIDATION"]
    B --> C3["CHECK 3 WALL THICKNESS VALIDATION"]
    B --> C4["CHECK 4 SHRINKAGE COMPENSATION"]
    B --> C5["CHECK 5 MACHINING ALLOWANCE"]
    B --> C6["CHECK 6 TOLERANCE FEASIBILITY"]
    B --> C7["CHECK 7 PARTING LINE STRATEGY"]
    B --> C8["CHECK 8 CORE WITHDRAWAL PATH"]
    C1 --> D{"ALL PASS"}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    D -->|YES| E["DESIGN APPROVED FOR SAND CASTING"]
    D -->|NO| F["GENERATE DFM VIOLATION REPORT"]
    F --> G["ITERATE DESIGN GEOMETRY"]
    G --> A
```

### 4.3 Schematic Concept — Draft Angle Geometry

The following ASCII schematic illustrates how draft modifies a vertical wall. The **H** is the depth (height) of the cavity, $\alpha$ is the draft angle, and **d** is the horizontal displacement at the top.

```
            +---------+          <-- Top of cavity (open at parting plane)
            |        /
            |       / 
         d  |      / tan(alpha) = d / H
            |     /
            |    /
            |   /
            |  /
            | /
            |/____________ 
            +------------+     <-- Bottom of cavity
                    H
```

For $H = 80 \text{ mm}$ and $\alpha = 1.5°$:

$$d = 80 \cdot \tan(1.5°) = 2.10 \text{ mm (as derived in Section 3.3)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Confirmed:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved words like `end` or `subgraph`), and labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric characters (no markdown formatting inside quotes).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define "Draft Angle" in the context of sand casting. State its typical values for (a) shallow pockets and (b) deep pockets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Draft angle is the intentional taper provided on all vertical surfaces of a pattern that are perpendicular to the parting plane, to enable clean withdrawal of the pattern from the sand mold without damaging mold walls.
- Shallow pockets (depth ≤ 50 mm): **1° to 2°**
- Deep pockets (depth > 50 mm): **5° to 10°** (with additional **0.5° per 100 mm of depth** as a corrective rule).

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for shallow range, 1 mark for deep range.)*

---

**Q2. Why is uniform wall thickness a critical design characteristic in sand cast parts? What is the recommended thickness ratio between adjacent sections?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Non-uniform wall thickness causes differential cooling rates in adjacent sections, leading to (a) internal residual stresses, (b) warpage, and (c) shrinkage cavities in the thicker section (since it remains molten longer while the thin section solidifies first and cannot feed liquid metal).
**Recommended ratio:**
$$\dfrac{t_{\text{max}}}{t_{\text{min}}} \leq 2$$
If this ratio is exceeded, mitigation is required through ribs, chills, or cores.

*(Award: 2 marks for explanation of problem, 1 mark for ratio.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern, May 2024 Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the typical characteristics of a sand cast part. Discuss at least six characteristics with their typical values and the manufacturing problem each one solves. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The typical characteristics of a sand cast part are standardized design attributes codified under Design for Manufacturing (DFM) for casting. The six most important are:

**1. Draft Angle (1 mark):**
Taper on vertical surfaces, typically **1°–2°** for shallow features and **5°–10°** for deep pockets. Solves the **pattern withdrawal problem** — without draft, the pattern erodes sand walls during extraction.

**2. Fillet and Corner Radii (1 mark):**
Internal corners are rounded with fillet radius $r \geq 0.5 t$ (typically **3–25 mm**). Solves **hot tears and stress concentration** at sharp re-entrant corners during solidification.

**3. Minimum Wall Thickness (1 mark):**
Ranges from **3 mm (small parts)** to **12 mm (large parts)** depending on alloy fluidity. Solves **misrun** (thin sections not filling) and **shrinkage cavities** (thick sections cooling too slowly).

**4. Machining Allowance (1 mark):**
Extra material provided on surfaces to be post-machined, typically **1.5–6 mm per side**. Solves the problem of **rough as-cast surface finish** (Ra 6.3–50 μm).

**5. Shrinkage Allowance (1 mark):**
Pattern is oversized by linear shrinkage factor $s$ (**0.5%–2%** depending on alloy). Solves **thermal contraction** of the metal during cooling from solidus to room temperature.

**6. Parting Line and Core Design (2 marks):**
The parting line must be chosen to minimize undercuts and simplify the mold. Cores (made of sand bonded with resin) are used for internal cavities. Solves the **geometry-vs.-moldability conflict** — features that cannot be drawn from a two-part mold require cores, increasing cost.

---

**(b) A sand cast iron part has a specified finished dimension of 250 mm. The casting must subsequently be machined on both ends to achieve a final length of 248 mm. Gray cast iron shrinkage is 1%. The deepest vertical pocket in the pattern has a depth of 100 mm and a draft of 2°. Calculate: (i) Pattern length, (ii) Total machining allowance distributed on both ends, (iii) Draft displacement at the top of the pocket. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Finished casting dimension $L_c = 250 \text{ mm}$, finished post-machining dimension $= 248 \text{ mm}$, shrinkage $s = 1\% = 0.01$, pocket depth $H = 100 \text{ mm}$, draft angle $\alpha = 2°$.

**(i) Pattern Length (2 marks):**
$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_c \cdot (1 + s) = 250 \cdot (1 + 0.01) = 250 \cdot 1.01 = 252.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Total Machining Allowance (3 marks):**
Total material to be removed from both ends:
$$\Delta L = L_c - L_{\text{final}} = 250 - 248 = 2 \text{ mm}$$
Per side:
$$MA = \dfrac{\Delta L}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.0 \text{ mm per side}$$
Total machining allowance (both sides combined) $= 2 \text{ mm}$. This 1 mm/side is added to the casting dimension **before** pattern making but is **not** part of shrinkage compensation — it is a separate post-casting operation.

**(iii) Draft Displacement (2 marks):**
$$d = H \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 100 \cdot \tan(2°) = 100 \cdot 0.03492 = 3.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Pattern Length | **252.5 mm** |
| Machining Allowance per side | **1.0 mm** |
| Draft Displacement | **3.49 mm** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Discuss the design guidelines for sand cast parts with respect to: (i) Draft (ii) Fillets and corners (iii) Wall thickness uniformity (iv) Parting line location. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Draft (2 marks):**
Draft is a slight taper on all vertical faces perpendicular to the parting plane. Without it, the pattern cannot be withdrawn without damaging the mold cavity. Typical values are **1°–2°** for small parts and **5°–10°** for deep pockets. **External surfaces** (away from cavity) taper outward; **internal surfaces** (into cavity) taper inward, both widening toward the parting line.

**(ii) Fillets and Corners (2 marks):**
Sharp internal corners must be replaced by fillets of radius $r \geq 0.5 t$ (where $t$ is wall thickness) to prevent **hot tears** and **stress concentration**. Sharp external corners are also slightly rounded (smaller radius $R \approx 0.2 t$) to reduce pattern wear and minimize edge-chipping on the casting. Typical fillet radii range from **3 mm (small) to 25 mm (large castings)**.

**(iii) Wall Thickness Uniformity (2 marks):**
Uniform wall thickness prevents differential cooling. Rule: $t_{\max} / t_{\min} \leq 2$. When this ratio is exceeded, designers use (a) **cores** to hollow out thick sections, (b) **ribs** to stiffen without adding mass, (c) **chills** to accelerate cooling in thick areas, or (d) **insulating sleeves** to slow thin areas.

**(iv) Parting Line Location (1 mark):**
The parting line separates the cope (upper) and drag (lower) mold halves. It should be chosen to:
- Maximize features in one half (preferably the drag) for sand-fill reliability.
- Avoid horizontal surfaces in the cope (which would sag).
- Minimize the number of cores and undercuts.
- Be located on a non-critical or machined surface (so flash is removed).

---

**(b) A cylindrical sand cast steel part has a length of 300 mm and diameter 80 mm. Steel shrinkage is 2%. The depth of the deepest mold cavity is 150 mm and the draft required is 1.5°. Calculate: (i) Final casting length after shrinkage, (ii) Pattern length required, (iii) Horizontal displacement at the top of the cavity due to draft. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Casting length $L_c = 300 \text{ mm}$, diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm}$ (not used in calculations but stated for context), shrinkage $s = 2\% = 0.02$, cavity depth $H = 150 \text{ mm}$, draft $\alpha = 1.5°$.

**(i) Final Casting Length (2 marks):**
This is the dimension after the part has cooled to room temperature from the solidus. In the pattern-making process, the **pattern is oversized** by the shrinkage factor. After cooling, the casting contracts to its **nominal** specified length:

$$L_{\text{casting, final}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{pattern}}}{1 + s}$$

Since we are told the casting length is 300 mm (the specified dimension), the final casting length is **300 mm** by definition. However, if the **300 mm refers to the pattern** (a common wording ambiguity), then:

$$L_{\text{final}} = \dfrac{300}{1 + 0.02} = \dfrac{300}{1.02} = 294.12 \text{ mm}$$

*For standard KTU interpretation:* 300 mm is the desired casting dimension, so the final casting length is **300 mm** (1 mark for stating the definition; 1 mark for clarity).

**(ii) Pattern Length Required (2 marks):**
$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + s) = 300 \cdot 1.02 = 306 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Horizontal Displacement Due to Draft (3 marks):**
$$d = H \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 150 \cdot \tan(1.5°)$$
$$d = 150 \cdot 0.02619 = 3.928 \text{ mm}$$

This means the patternmaker must taper the side wall of the pattern such that at the top of the deepest pocket, the wall is shifted outward by **3.93 mm** relative to the bottom — to allow clean pattern withdrawal.

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Final Casting Length | **300 mm** (or 294.12 mm if 300 mm was pattern) |
| Pattern Length | **306 mm** |
| Draft Displacement | **3.93 mm** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Dec 2023 / May 2024 Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add shrinkage BEFORE machining allowance.** The correct sequence is: nominal dimension → add shrinkage → add machining allowance. Many students invert the order and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using degree mode in calculator** while computing $\tan(\alpha)$. Always verify: $\tan(2°)$ ≠ 2.00. The correct value is **0.0349**.
> 3. **Confusing "draft displacement" with "draft angle."** The displacement is a **linear dimension in mm**; the angle is in **degrees**. KTU examiners often test both in the same sub-question.
> 4. **Not specifying units** in tabular answers. KTU 2024 scheme mandates SI units throughout.
> 5. **Omitting the "Why" behind each characteristic.** A characteristic stated without its manufacturing rationale gets only **half marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sand Casting Typical Characteristics**

- **Draft Angle:** $d = h \cdot \tan(\alpha)$, with $\alpha = 1°–10°$ depending on depth.
- **Shrinkage:** $L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + s)$, with $s \in [0.005, 0.020]$.
- **Machining Allowance:** Added **after** shrinkage; ranges 1.5–6 mm/side.
- **Fillet Radius:** $r \geq 0.5 t$, typically 3–25 mm.
- **Min Wall Thickness:** 3 mm (small) → 12 mm (large); alloy-dependent.
- **Wall Thickness Ratio:** $t_{\max}/t_{\min} \leq 2$ (no mitigation).
- **Tolerance:** ISO 8062-3, CT 9–CT 13.
- **Surface Finish:** Ra 6.3–50 μm.
- **Parting Line:** On a non-critical surface; prefer drag side for accuracy.
- **Cores:** Required for internal cavities; increase cost ~20–30%.
- **Order of Pattern Operations:** Nominal dimension → + Shrinkage → + Machining Allowance.
- **DFM Output:** Violations of these rules typically increase rejection rates by **15–30%**.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Sand Casting: Typical Characteristics of a Sand Cast Part.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations for Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations for Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Design for Sand Casting (DFC):**
> *Design for Sand Casting* is a systematic, rules-based methodology within Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA) that prescribes the geometric, dimensional, and metallurgical modifications a designer must incorporate into a part so that it can be produced reliably, economically, and defect-free through a sand-mold casting process. The recommendations cover draft angles, fillets, radii, parting-line selection, shrinkage, machining allowance, core design, ribbing, and wall-thickness control.

In the context of the **2024 NEP-aligned KTU syllabus**, this topic is treated as a *gate-keeper* module — every cast component, whether a turbocharger housing, a gear blank, or a machine-tool bedplate, must first pass through these design rules before detailed drawing is approved.

## 1.2 The Casting Process — A Quick Refresher

Sand casting is the **most widely used casting process** in the foundry industry because:
1. Sand molds are **inexpensive** (green-sand molds are reusable).
2. It accommodates **almost any metal** (ferrous and non-ferrous).
3. It can produce **very large parts** (up to several tons).
4. It tolerates **complex internal cavities** through the use of expendable sand cores.

The basic process flow is:
*Pattern → Mold Preparation → Core Placement (if any) → Pouring → Solidification → Shakeout → Cleaning → Inspection → Machining.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Design Rules Matter:** A casting defect is **10× cheaper to prevent at the design stage** than to repair in the foundry. A poorly drafted part causes mold rupture; a sharp internal corner causes a hot tear; a non-uniform wall causes a shrinkage cavity. Hence, every line on a casting drawing is a *design decision that affects yield, cost, and quality.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of sand casting as **baking a cake in a cardboard mould**:

- The **mould (sand mould)** is the cardboard — fragile, must be removed easily without damage.
- The **cake (molten metal)** shrinks as it cools — so the mould must be slightly **bigger** than the final cake.
- The **icing (machined surface)** can only be applied flat — so the cake must be made **slightly larger** in those zones so the icing can be scraped smooth.
- If two sides of the cake are joined at a **sharp 90° corner**, it will crack while cooling. Round corners (fillets) allow even cooling — like rounded corners on a chocolate bar that don't snap.
- If the cake is very thick on one side and very thin on the other, the thin side will **freeze first** and pull away from the thick side, leaving a **void** (shrinkage porosity). Keep the thickness uniform — like the wall of a drinking glass.

**The pattern (the wooden/metal replica of the part) is therefore NOT a copy of the drawing. It is a deliberately distorted copy that bakes in the foundry's quirks.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric — Pattern Allowance Categories (KTU Recommended):**
> - **Shrinkage allowance** → 0.5 % to 2.5 % depending on metal
> - **Draft (taper) allowance** → 1° to 3° on vertical faces
> - **Machining allowance** → 1.5 mm to 8 mm based on size
> - **Shake / Rapping allowance** → 0.5 mm to 2 mm on flat surfaces
> - **Distortion allowance** → applied on long, slender sections
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Sand-Casting Design

Every sand-cast part must be evaluated against seven interrelated design pillars. Mastering these is the *single most important* step to scoring in the KTU examination on this topic.

### Pillar 1 — Draft (Taper) Allowance

**Why it exists:** When a pattern is withdrawn from the sand mould, vertical walls generate friction. Without taper, the mould walls will tear.

**Rule of Thumb (KTU):**
- **External surfaces:** 1° to 3° draft
- **Internal surfaces (holes/pockets):** 1° to 4° draft (often more, because sand is weaker in tension)
- **Deep pockets > 50 mm depth:** increase draft up to **5°**

### Pillar 2 — Fillet and Corner Radii

**Why it exists:** Sharp internal corners are stress concentrators during solidification → **hot tears**. Sharp external corners cause mould-edge crumbling.

| Corner Type | Recommended Radius |
|---|---|
| External corner (convex mould) | Small, R₁ ≈ (1/10 to 1/5) × wall thickness |
| Internal corner (concave mould) | Large, R₂ ≈ (1/3 to 1/2) × wall thickness |
| **R₂ must be ≥ 2 × R₁** | This is the golden rule for corner radii |

### Pillar 3 — Shrinkage Allowance

**Why it exists:** Metals contract on cooling. The mould cavity must therefore be **larger** than the finished part.

**Standard Shrinkage Values (KTU Reference Table):**

| Metal | Linear Shrinkage (%) |
|---|---|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | 1.0 |
| Cast Iron (White) | 2.0 |
| Carbon Steel | 2.0 |
| Stainless Steel | 2.5 |
| Aluminum Alloys | 1.5 |
| Copper Alloys (Brass, Bronze) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Magnesium Alloys | 1.5 |
| Zinc Alloys | 1.0 – 1.5 |

### Pillar 4 — Machining Allowance

**Why it exists:** Casting surfaces are rough (typically **Ra 12.5 to 25 µm**). Areas designated for mating or sliding must be machined smooth.

| Casting Size (Length) | Typical Machining Allowance |
|---|---|
| < 300 mm | 2 – 3 mm |
| 300 – 600 mm | 3 – 5 mm |
| 600 – 1500 mm | 5 – 8 mm |
| > 1500 mm | 8 – 12 mm |

### Pillar 5 — Parting Line Selection

The parting line is the **horizontal plane** where the upper and lower mould halves meet. The KTU-recommended strategy is:

- Choose the **largest cross-section** as the parting line to minimize mould height.
- Place it through a **flat, simple contour** of the part.
- Avoid placing the parting line across a **machined surface** (impossible to clean up).
- Use a **straight parting line** wherever possible (lower tooling cost than a stepped one).

### Pillar 6 — Uniform Wall Thickness

**Why it exists:** A thicker section cools **slower** than a thin one, creating a **hot spot** that causes shrinkage porosity.

**Rule of Thumb (KTU):**
- Maintain wall thickness within a **± 25 % range** where possible.
- When transitions are necessary, use a **tapered/gradual transition** (slope 1:4 to 1:6) instead of a step.
- Add **ribs** instead of thickening walls to gain stiffness.
- Minimum wall thickness for gray cast iron ≈ **4–6 mm** (small castings).

### Pillar 7 — Core Design

**Why it exists:** Internal cavities (bores, ports) are formed by sand cores that must be supported against buoyancy and metallostatic pressure.

**Key KTU Design Rules for Cores:**
- Provide **adequate core prints** (the seats in the mould that hold the core).
- **Core print length** ≈ 1.5 × core diameter (or width).
- Provide **vents** (small holes, ~3 mm dia.) in the core to allow gas escape.
- Use **chaplets** (small metal supports) to hold horizontal cores.
- **Avoid unsupported thin cores** (they break under metal pressure).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following five formulas. Every KTU numerical question on sand casting is built from these.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pattern Dimension (Shrinkage)** | $D_{pattern} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ | $S$ = linear shrinkage fraction (e.g. 0.01 for 1 %) |
| 2 | **Draft Allowance (Linear)** | $T = h \times \tan(\alpha)$ | $h$ = depth of vertical face (mm), $\alpha$ = draft angle (°) |
| 3 | **Draft Allowance (Patternmaker's Rule)** | Add $\frac{1°}{25 \text{ mm}}$ of height | "One degree per inch" simplified metric rule |
| 4 | **Minimum Fillet Radius (Internal)** | $R_{int} \geq 0.4 \times t$ | $t$ = wall thickness |
| 5 | **Minimum Wall Thickness (Gray Iron)** | $t_{min} = 0.03 \times L + 4$ (mm) | $L$ = largest casting dimension (mm); empirical |

> **Key Symbol Conventions Used in KTU Valuation:**
> - $D_{pattern}$ — Dimension on the wooden/metal pattern
> - $D_{part}$ — Dimension on the finished engineering drawing
> - $S$ — Free linear shrinkage (NOT total shrinkage)
> - $\alpha$ — Draft angle measured from the vertical parting line
> - $h$ — Vertical depth of the surface being drafted
> - $T$ — Linear material added to compensate for draft

## 2.3 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering

- **Automotive industry:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings, brake drums — all sand-cast. Draft and shrinkage errors cause entire batches to be scrapped.
- **Heavy engineering:** Turbines, pump casings, valve bodies — wall-thickness uniformity determines pressure-vessel certification.
- **Cost impact:** A well-designed casting reduces **machining time by 30–60 %**, lowers scrap rate, and eliminates **post-casting weld repairs** that weaken the part.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Shrinkage Allowance Calculation (Classic KTU 14-mark Style)

> **Problem Statement (KTU-type):**
> A gray cast iron gear blank has an outer diameter of 320 mm as specified on the engineering drawing. The free linear shrinkage of the alloy is 1.0 %. Determine:
> (a) The diameter to which the pattern must be made.
> (b) The diameter of the mould cavity at the time of pouring.
> (c) If the gear has 24 teeth, the pitch circle diameter on the pattern.
>
> Assume the pattern is made of wood with negligible shrinkage of its own.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The relationship between the *final part dimension* and the *pattern dimension* for a uniform-shrinkage alloy is:

$$D_{pattern} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$$

where $S$ is the *free linear shrinkage* expressed as a fraction (not a percentage).

**Step 2 — Convert the percentage to a fraction.**

$$S = 1.0 \% = \frac{1.0}{100} = 0.010$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pattern diameter.**

$$D_{pattern} = 320 \times (1 + 0.010) = 320 \times 1.010$$

$$D_{pattern} = 323.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Justify why mould cavity diameter = pattern diameter.**

When green-sand moulding is used, the mould cavity is formed by *ramming sand around the pattern and then withdrawing the pattern*. The cavity is therefore a *faithful negative copy* of the pattern at the moment of pouring. Hence:

$$D_{mould} = D_{pattern} = 323.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the pitch circle diameter (PCD) of the teeth on the pattern.**

Assuming the PCD is a direct linear feature (i.e., a small circle in the same plane as the bore), the same 1 % rule applies:

$$D_{PCD,pattern} = D_{PCD,part} \times (1 + 0.010) = D_{PCD,part} \times 1.010$$

If the engineering PCD is, for example, 280 mm:

$$D_{PCD,pattern} = 280 \times 1.010 = 282.80 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Answer

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Pattern outer diameter | **323.20 mm** |
| Mould cavity diameter at pouring | **323.20 mm** |
| Pattern PCD (for 280 mm part PCD) | **282.80 mm** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Draft Allowance Calculation

> **Problem Statement:**
> A vertical face of a sand-cast steel flange is 150 mm deep. The designer specifies a draft angle of 2°. Calculate the additional material to be added at the parting line end of the pattern, and the dimensions of the pattern face.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

For a vertical face inclined at a draft angle $\alpha$ from the parting line vertical, the *linear taper* added at the parting-line end is:

$$T = h \times \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$h = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad \alpha = 2°$$

$$T = 150 \times \tan(2°)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the trigonometric term.**

$$\tan(2°) = 0.03492 \text{ (from standard trigonometric table, to 5 decimal places)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the linear taper.**

$$T = 150 \times 0.03492 = 5.238 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result geometrically.**

The pattern face is therefore a *trapezoid*, not a rectangle. At the parting-line end, the pattern is **5.24 mm wider** than at the deeper end. This is *additional material* that will be machined off if that face is a machined surface, or retained if it is a non-machined "as-cast" surface.

### Final Answer

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Linear taper (T) | **5.24 mm** |
| Pattern face at parting line | Original + 5.24 mm |
| Pattern face at deep end | Original (unchanged) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Combined Allowance (Examination Favourite)

> **Problem Statement (Common 14-mark format):**
> A rectangular sand-cast plate of dimensions 400 mm × 250 mm × 30 mm is to be made from aluminum alloy (S = 1.5 %). The vertical sides are 30 mm deep. A draft angle of 1.5° is applied to all four sides. The top surface is to be machined with 3 mm allowance. Compute the pattern dimensions.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute shrinkage-corrected dimensions (Step-by-step).**

$$L_{pattern} = 400 \times (1 + 0.015) = 400 \times 1.015 = 406.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$W_{pattern} = 250 \times (1 + 0.015) = 250 \times 1.015 = 253.75 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{pattern} = 30 \times (1 + 0.015) = 30 \times 1.015 = 30.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Add machining allowance to the top face only.**

$$t_{pattern,machined} = 30.45 + 3.00 = 33.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute draft allowance on the 30 mm deep vertical sides.**

$$T = h \times \tan(\alpha) = 30 \times \tan(1.5°)$$

$$\tan(1.5°) = 0.02619$$

$$T = 30 \times 0.02619 = 0.786 \text{ mm} \approx 0.79 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Adjust length and width to include draft.**

Because the draft makes the pattern wider at the parting line, the parting-line dimensions are:

$$L_{parting} = 406.00 + 2 \times 0.79 = 407.58 \text{ mm}$$

$$W_{parting} = 253.75 + 2 \times 0.79 = 255.33 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Answer — Pattern Specification

| Pattern Feature | Value (mm) |
|---|---|
| Length at parting line | **407.58** |
| Width at parting line | **255.33** |
| Thickness (top machined) | **33.45** |
| Taper per side (linear) | **0.79** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Casting Defects vs Design Remedy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is a *favourite KTU short-answer question* in Parts A and B. Memorize it.**

| Defect | Root Cause | Design-Level Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot tear** (crack in casting) | Sharp internal corner | Add fillet, $R_{int} \geq 0.4 t$ |
| **Shrinkage cavity / porosity** | Non-uniform wall thickness | Equalize wall thickness, add ribs |
| **Mould rupture / sand drop** | Insufficient draft, deep pocket | Increase draft angle, use core |
| **Mismatched parting line** | Poor parting-line choice | Choose largest flat section |
| **Cold shut** (incomplete fusion) | Thin sections, low pour temp. | Increase min. wall thickness |
| **Misrun** (incomplete fill) | Thin/long flow path | Redesign flow path, add gates |
| **Flash / burrs at parting line** | Loose clamping, soft sand | Use deeper flask, harder ramming |
| **Blowholes** (gas pockets) | Inadequate core venting | Add vents, use drier sand |
| **Warping** | Non-uniform cooling, long thin shape | Use chills, design symmetric section |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Seven-Pillar Design-Decision Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram maps the **decision sequence** a designer must follow when designing a sand-cast part, from the *initial 3D model* to the *final pattern drawing*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Part 3D Model] --> B[Step 1: Identify Critical Surfaces]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Wall Thickness Uniformity Rule]
    C --> D[Step 3: Add Fillet and Corner Radii]
    D --> E[Step 4: Select Parting Line]
    E --> F[Step 5: Add Draft Angle to Vertical Faces]
    F --> G[Step 6: Design Cores and Core Prints]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute Pattern Dimensions]
    H --> I[Step 8: Add Machining Allowance to Mating Surfaces]
    I --> J[Step 9: Validate Pattern against DFM Checklist]
    J --> K{Defect Risk?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Revise Design and Loop Back to Step 2]
    K -- No --> M[Step 10: Approve Final Pattern Drawing]
    M --> N[End: Release to Foundry]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Steps 1–3 control **solidification behaviour** (hot tears, shrinkage).
> - Steps 4–6 control **mould integrity** (mould rupture, sand drop).
> - Steps 7–9 control **dimensional accuracy** (shrinkage, machining stock).
> - Step 10 is the **design freeze** — the pattern is now released for tooling.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Sand-Cast Part

This diagram shows the *functional layers* that exist in a typical sand-cast component and how each design rule protects a specific layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C[L1: Outer Skin Layer]
        C1[Rough As-Cast Surface]
        C2[Draft Tapered Walls]
    end
    subgraph D[L2: Machined Functional Surface]
        D1[Machined Mating Face]
        D2[Bored Hole for Bearing]
    end
    subgraph E[L3: Internal Bulk Material]
        E1[Uniform Wall Thickness]
        E2[Internal Ribs and Bosses]
    end
    subgraph F[L4: Internal Cavity and Cores]
        F1[Sand Core Cavity]
        F2[Core Vents and Chaplets]
    end
    subgraph G[L5: Corner Geometry Layer]
        G1[Fillet Radii]
        G2[Chamfers]
    end
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
```

> **Mapping the design rules to the layers:**
> - **L1 → Draft and mouldability** rules
> - **L2 → Machining allowance** rules
> - **L3 → Wall-thickness uniformity** rules
> - **L4 → Core design** rules
> - **L5 → Fillet/radii** rules

## 4.3 The Pattern vs. Part vs. Mould — Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    part1[Finished Part Dimension D_part] --> formula[Apply Shrinkage S]
    formula --> pat1[Pattern Dimension D_pattern]
    pat1 --> mould[Mould Cavity D_mould at Pouring]
    mould --> pour[Pour Molten Metal at T_pour]
    pour --> cool[Solidify and Contract]
    cool --> part1
    pat1 --> draft[Apply Draft T to Vertical Faces]
    draft --> pat2[Final Pattern Geometry]
    pat2 --> mach[Machining Allowance on Functional Faces]
    mach --> mat[Final Pattern Drawing]
```

> This loop captures the *fundamental cyclical relationship* between the part, the pattern, and the mould — and how the various allowances are layered onto the basic part dimension.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define draft allowance in sand casting. Why is a higher draft angle recommended for internal surfaces (e.g., holes, pockets) than for external surfaces?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Draft allowance** is the taper provided on the vertical faces of a pattern so that it can be withdrawn from the sand mould without damaging the mould walls. It is expressed as an angle (typically 1° to 3°) measured from the vertical parting line, or as a linear taper per unit height.

> A **higher draft on internal surfaces** is required because, when the pattern is withdrawn, the sand at an internal corner is under **tensile stress** (it is being pulled outward as the pattern rises). Sand is **10× weaker in tension than in compression**. If the draft is too small, the sand corner *tears* and falls into the mould cavity, producing a "sand wash" defect. External surfaces, by contrast, are in **compression** during withdrawal and tolerate a smaller draft.

> *Examiner's note: Award full marks only if both the definition AND the tensile-vs-compressive explanation are present.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**List any six design recommendations for sand casting that a mechanical design engineer must follow.**

**Model Answer (Any six of the following):**

1. Provide **draft** on all vertical faces (1°–3° external, higher internal).
2. Use **generous fillet radii** at internal corners ($R \geq 0.4 t$); avoid sharp corners.
3. Maintain **uniform wall thickness**; use ribs for stiffness.
4. Add a **shrinkage allowance** of 0.5 %–2.5 % to pattern dimensions.
5. Add a **machining allowance** of 2–8 mm on functional mating faces.
6. Choose the **parting line** through the largest cross-section, on a flat surface.
7. Use **sand cores with adequate prints and vents** for internal cavities.
8. Provide **gradual thickness transitions** (1:4 to 1:6 taper) where changes are unavoidable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** A gray cast iron pulley has a hub diameter of 120 mm, a rim outer diameter of 480 mm, and a thickness of 65 mm as per the engineering drawing. The free linear shrinkage of the alloy is **1.0 %**, and the pattern is to be made from seasoned wood (negligible shrinkage). Calculate the pattern dimensions for the hub, rim, and thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the *seven design recommendations* a mechanical designer must follow to produce a defect-free sand casting. Illustrate the importance of **wall thickness uniformity** with reference to the **shrinkage cavity** defect. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Hub diameter $D_{hub} = 120$ mm
- Rim outer diameter $D_{rim} = 480$ mm
- Thickness $t = 65$ mm
- Shrinkage $S = 1.0 \% = 0.010$

**Step 1 — Apply the shrinkage formula to each dimension:**

$$D_{hub,pattern} = 120 \times (1 + 0.010) = 121.20 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{rim,pattern} = 480 \times (1 + 0.010) = 484.80 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{pattern} = 65 \times (1 + 0.010) = 65.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate the result for the answer:**

| Feature | Part Dimension (mm) | Pattern Dimension (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Hub diameter | 120.00 | **121.20** |
| Rim outer diameter | 480.00 | **484.80** |
| Thickness | 65.00 | **65.65** |

*Note to student: Full marks are awarded for showing (i) the formula, (ii) the numerical substitution, AND (iii) the final tabulated result. A direct answer without the formula scores at most 4 of 7 marks.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**The seven design recommendations:**

1. **Draft (taper) allowance** — 1° to 3° on vertical walls for pattern withdrawal.
2. **Fillet and corner radii** — eliminate sharp internal corners that act as stress raisers.
3. **Shrinkage allowance** — compensate for thermal contraction of the metal.
4. **Machining allowance** — provide extra stock on surfaces that will be machined.
5. **Parting-line selection** — place at the largest, flattest cross-section.
6. **Wall-thickness uniformity** — maintain constant or gradually changing thickness.
7. **Core design** — adequate core prints, vents, and chaplets.

**Wall thickness and shrinkage cavity:**

When a casting has a **thick section** adjacent to a **thin section**, the thin section solidifies first and is therefore "frozen rigid". As the thick section continues to solidify, it contracts but is now mechanically constrained by the frozen thin section. The shrinkage volume has nowhere to go, and a **void** (shrinkage cavity or *centerline shrinkage*) forms in the thick section.

**Design-level remedy:**
- Equalize wall thickness (vary ≤ ± 25 %).
- Add **chills** (metal inserts that accelerate local cooling).
- Add **risers** (reservoirs of molten metal that feed the shrinkage).
- Use **ribs** for stiffness rather than thickening the wall.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A vertical face on a sand-cast aluminum housing is 200 mm deep. The pattern is to have an external draft angle of **2°**. Compute: (i) the linear taper, (ii) the additional material per side, and (iii) the parting-line width if the finished part width is 80 mm. Use aluminum shrinkage of **1.5 %**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **recommended corner geometry** (external vs. internal) in a sand-cast part. Why is it essential that the **internal fillet radius be at least twice the external fillet radius**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Vertical face depth $h = 200$ mm
- Draft angle $\alpha = 2°$
- Part width $W_{part} = 80$ mm
- Aluminum shrinkage $S = 1.5 \% = 0.015$

**Step 1 — Compute linear taper (T).**

$$T = h \times \tan(\alpha) = 200 \times \tan(2°)$$

$$\tan(2°) = 0.03492$$

$$T = 200 \times 0.03492 = 6.984 \text{ mm} \approx 6.98 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute additional material per side.**

Since the draft taper is applied symmetrically on both sides of the vertical face:

$$T_{per\_side} = \frac{6.98}{2} = 3.49 \text{ mm per side}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parting-line width (pattern width at parting line).**

First, apply shrinkage to the part width:

$$W_{pattern,neutral} = 80 \times (1 + 0.015) = 81.20 \text{ mm}$$

Then add the full taper (since the part has *two* vertical faces contributing to width):

$$W_{parting} = 81.20 + 2 \times T_{per\_side} = 81.20 + 6.98 = 88.18 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Tabulated Answer

| Quantity | Value (mm) |
|---|---|
| Linear taper (T) | **6.98** |
| Material per side | **3.49** |
| Shrinkage-corrected part width | **81.20** |
| Parting-line pattern width | **88.18** |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**Corner Geometry — Standard KTU Diagram (described in words):**

For a casting wall meeting at a corner, two radii must be specified:

- **External corner (outside of the casting):** Small radius $R_1$. This corner is *convex* as seen by the sand mould and is easy to form — only a small fillet is needed to prevent sand-edge crumbling.
- **Internal corner (inside of the casting):** Large radius $R_2$. This corner is *concave* and is a *hot spot* during solidification. The radius must be large enough that the metal in the corner can feed from the riser and that the thermal gradient remains smooth.

**The Golden Rule (KTU 2024):**

$$R_2 \geq 2 \times R_1$$

A typical practical relationship is:

$$R_1 = 0.2 \times t, \quad R_2 = 0.4 \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = 2 R_1$$

**Why must $R_2 \geq 2 R_1$?**

1. **Hot-tear prevention:** Sharp internal corners act as stress concentrators during solidification. The thermal stress $\sigma$ at a notch tip is amplified by the *stress concentration factor* $K_t$, which is highest when the radius is smallest. A larger $R_2$ reduces $K_t$.
2. **Feeding path:** The internal corner is the *last* region to feed liquid metal from the riser. A larger radius creates a smoother liquid-metal pathway, preventing microporosity.
3. **Machinability:** A larger $R_2$ leaves more material around a drilled or milled corner, giving the cutting tool a stronger "back-up" surface and preventing tool breakage.
4. **Uniform cooling:** A larger $R_2$ means the corner is *thicker* in a controlled way, but it is *thinner than the adjoining wall* by a controlled ratio — preventing the corner from becoming a hot spot.

> *Examiner's note: Award the full 7 marks only if the student explains at least THREE of the four reasons above with a clear physical mechanism.*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Sand-Casting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % to a fraction.** Students often write $D_{pattern} = 320 \times 1.0 = 320$ instead of $320 \times 1.01$. *Always write the conversion step explicitly.*
> 2. **Confusing mould-cavity dimension with pattern dimension.** At the moment of pouring, $D_{mould} = D_{pattern}$, NOT $D_{part}$. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 3. **Applying draft at the wrong end.** Draft makes the pattern *wider at the parting line* and *narrower at the deep end* — not the other way around. A common conceptual error.
> 4. **Forgetting that draft is per side, not total.** When asked for parting-line width, students often add the full taper to one side only, producing a dimension that is *off by one taper (≈ 3–7 mm)*.
> 5. **Using linear shrinkage in a volumetric formula.** Shrinkage is a *linear* quantity; volume shrinkage is approximately 3× the linear shrinkage for small values, but the *pattern dimension* formula always uses the linear value.
> 6. **Omitting the formula before substituting.** The KTU 2024 marking scheme awards **1 mark** for writing the formula, **2 marks** for substitution, and **4 marks** for the final numerical answer in a 7-mark problem. Skip the formula → lose 1 mark minimum.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Summary**

- [x] **Sand casting** is the most flexible casting process but is defect-prone if design rules are ignored.
- [x] **Draft angle** = taper on vertical faces; 1°–3° external, higher internal; **sand is weak in tension.**
- [x] **Shrinkage allowance** = $D_{pattern} = D_{part}(1 + S)$; typical $S$ values: **Al 1.5 %, CI 1.0 %, Steel 2.0 %**.
- [x] **Machining allowance** = 2–8 mm depending on casting size, applied only on functional faces.
- [x] **Fillet radii** rule: $R_{int} \geq 2 R_{ext}$; both should be a fraction of the wall thickness $t$.
- [x] **Wall thickness uniformity** is the *most important* single rule; use ribs instead of thickening walls.
- [x] **Parting line** goes through the **largest flat section** of the part.
- [x] **Cores** need prints, vents, and chaplets; avoid thin unsupported cores.
- [x] **Defect-remedy matrix**: hot tear → fillet; shrinkage cavity → uniform walls; sand drop → more draft.
- [x] **Always show the formula, the substitution, AND the numerical answer** in any KTU 14-mark question.
- [x] **Master the five formulas** in §2.2 — they cover ~80 % of all KTU numerical questions on this topic.
- [x] **Use a 1:4 to 1:6 taper** when transitioning between two different wall thicknesses.
- [x] **Draft is added at the parting-line end** of the pattern, never at the deep end.
- [x] **Pattern shrinkage is linear, not volumetric** — do not cube or square the factor.
- [x] **Distinguish between free shrinkage and total shrinkage** — patterns use the *free* value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investment Casting - Design Considerations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax Process):** A precision metal-forming process in which a disposable *wax pattern* is invested (encased) in a ceramic shell, the wax is melted out to leave a refractory cavity, and molten metal is poured into this cavity to produce a near-net-shape component. Under KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPET755), it is classified as a **primary net-shape / near-net-shape forming process** capable of producing complex geometries with high dimensional accuracy (**±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm**) and excellent surface finish (**Ra 1.6 – 6.3 µm**).

The process is governed by the *disposable pattern* principle — every pattern is destroyed to retrieve a single casting, distinguishing it from permanent-mould processes.

### 1.2 Process Variants (KTU Syllabus Sub-classification)

| Variant | Pattern Material | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Lost-wax (Classical)** | Injectable wax | Jewelry, dental, aerospace turbine blades |
| **Frozen Mercury** | Mercury (frozen) | Antique fine art castings |
| **Plastic/PVC patterns** | Polystyrene | Large industrial components |
| **Ceramic-shell (Mod–Shel)** | Wax + ceramic slurry | Aerospace & defence (most common) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chocolate Bunny"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine a solid chocolate bunny is your *pattern*. You dip it repeatedly in liquid ice-cream coating and freeze it — this becomes the **ceramic shell**. Now you let the chocolate melt and pour out — this is **de-waxing**. You are left with a perfect hollow ice-cream shell. Pour hot milk into it, let it set, break the shell — and you have a perfect milk bunny. This is exactly how investment casting works: **wax pattern → ceramic shell → wax melts out → metal fills in → shell is broken off**.

### 1.4 Why "Design Considerations" are Critical in KTU Module 2

Investment casting is *expensive per piece* (high tooling & ceramic cost) but *cheap per feature* (no draft, no parting line constraints, no machining). Hence, **DFM decisions made at the design stage** decide whether the part is castable, economical, and dimensionally sound. KTU Module 2 specifically targets the **twelve golden design rules** the designer must respect.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twelve Design Rules for Investment Casting

Investment casting imposes fewer geometric restrictions than sand casting, but each rule below must be respected to avoid defects (cracks, misruns, ceramic inclusions, shell spalling).

#### Rule 1 — Uniform Wall Thickness
Thick sections cool last and act as **hot spots**, causing shrinkage porosity. A uniform section ensures directional solidification.

- **Target:** Largest thickness (Tmax) ≤ 2 × smallest thickness (Tmin)
- **Action:** If local thickening is unavoidable, insert a **chill** or **pad** to extract heat.

#### Rule 2 — Generous Radii & Fillets
Sharp internal corners cause **stress concentration** AND act as **ceramic-shell crack initiators** during de-waxing.

- **Recommendation:** Internal corner radius (R) ≥ 1.5 × wall thickness (t) and never below **1.5 mm**.
- **External corner radius** should equal wall thickness (t).

#### Rule 3 — Draft / Taper
Because the ceramic shell is *flexible*, draft requirements are far less severe than sand casting.

- **Recommended draft:** **0° 15' (≈0.25°) to 1°** per side on external walls.
- **Internal walls (cores):** **0° 30' to 1°** per side (need slightly more for shell rigidity).

#### Rule 4 — Parting Line Minimisation
Investment casting can have **non-planar, multiple, or curved parting lines** because the wax pattern is injected into a metal die and the ceramic shell is built in sections.

- Place parting line on the **largest cross-section** of the part.
- Hide it on a non-critical cosmetic surface.

#### Rule 5 — Section Thickness Transitions
Abrupt changes create **hot spots and fillet cracks**. Use a **tapered transition** of slope **3:1 (run:rise) or flatter**.

#### Rule 6 — Hollow Sections & Cores
Investment casting can produce **complex internal cavities** using:
1. **Integral wax cores** (most common)
2. **Ceramic cores** (for high-temp alloys, e.g., turbine blades)
3. **Sand cores** (large castings)

- Small holes (< 6 mm dia.) should be **cored out** rather than drilled post-casting to save cost.

#### Rule 7 — Undercuts
- **External undercuts:** Avoid — require multiple die halves (high die cost).
- **Internal undercuts:** Use **collapsible ceramic cores** or pre-formed wax inserts.

#### Rule 8 — Tolerances
Linear tolerance is governed by KTU/ISO 8062 (CT-grade):

| CT Grade | Tolerance (mm per 100 mm) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| CT 6 | ± 0.2 | Precision aerospace |
| **CT 7** | **± 0.3** | **General engineering (most common)** |
| CT 8 | ± 0.4 | Non-critical |
| CT 9 | ± 0.5 | Large castings |

#### Rule 9 — Surface Finish
As-cast finish: **Ra 1.6 – 6.3 µm** (3.2 µm typical). No further polishing needed for functional surfaces.

#### Rule 10 — Inserts & Metallic Cores
Pre-positioned **ferrous / non-ferrous inserts** can be cast-in-place (e.g., threaded bushes, carbide wear pads). The insert must be:
- Pre-heated to **200 – 400 °C** before assembly to avoid thermal shock.
- Held in the wax pattern by **Locating Features (LOF)**.

#### Rule 11 — Identification Marks
Cast directly on the part as raised (positive) or recessed (negative) lettering. KTU design rule: lettering depth ≥ **1.5 mm**, width ≥ **3 mm** to avoid filling defects.

#### Rule 12 — Gating, Risering & Feeders
- Risers are **optional** in investment casting because of directional solidification from ceramic shell.
- Gating follows **bottom-gate (side-gate) principle** to avoid metal splashing and gas entrapment.
- Feeders (risers) sized using **Chvorinov's rule** when needed.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-exam favourites. Memorise units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{total} = S_{wax} + S_{ceramic} + S_{metal}$ | Total linear shrinkage | 1.5 % – 3.0 % |
| 2 | $L_{die} = L_{final} \times (1 + S_{total})$ | Die (master) dimension | — |
| 3 | $L_{wax} = L_{final} \times (1 + S_{ceramic} + S_{metal})$ | Wax-pattern dimension | — |
| 4 | $T_{max} \leq 2 \times T_{min}$ | Wall thickness uniformity | — |
| 5 | $R_{int} \geq 1.5 \, t$ | Internal fillet radius | t = wall thickness |
| 6 | $\text{Draft} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{L}\right)$ | Draft angle | 0.25° – 1° |
| 7 | $t_{solidification} = K \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | Chvorinov's rule | n = 1.5 – 2 |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{vol} = 3 \times \varepsilon_{lin}$ | Volumetric = 3× linear strain | — |
| 9 | $FOS_{casting} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\sigma_{design}}$ | Casting strength factor | ≥ 2.0 |
| 10 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{tooling} + C_{wax} + n(C_{shell} + C_{metal} + C_{finish})}{n}$ | Per-piece cost | — |

> *Note: $V/A$ is the *modulus* (M) of the casting, central to riser sizing.*

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Turbine blades, combustor liners (Inconel, Ti-alloys)
- **Medical:** Orthopaedic implants (Ti-6Al-4V), dental crowns
- **Defence:** Missile nose-cones, gun components
- **Automotive:** Steering knuckle prototypes, fuel-injector bodies
- **General:** Valve bodies, pump impellers, gear blanks

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Master-Die (Pattern-Die) Dimension from Final Casting Dimension

The final casting is *smaller* than the wax pattern (because metal shrinks on cooling). The wax pattern is *larger* than the master die (because wax shrinks on cooling). The ceramic shell is *larger* than the wax pattern (because the ceramic expands during firing).

**Step 1 — Define shrinkages algebraically.**

Let:
- $L_f$ = final casting dimension (mm)
- $L_w$ = wax-pattern dimension (mm)
- $L_d$ = die (master) dimension (mm)
- $S_m$ = metal linear shrinkage (fraction)
- $S_c$ = ceramic-shell linear expansion (fraction)
- $S_w$ = wax linear shrinkage (fraction)

**Step 2 — Relate casting to wax pattern.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_f &= L_w \, (1 - S_m) \\
\Rightarrow L_w &= \dfrac{L_f}{1 - S_m} \approx L_f \, (1 + S_m) \quad \text{for small } S_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate wax pattern to die.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= L_d \, (1 - S_w) \\
\Rightarrow L_d &= \dfrac{L_w}{1 - S_w} \approx L_w \, (1 + S_w)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add ceramic expansion.**

The ceramic shell expands *after* the wax is removed. The *cavity* into which metal is poured is therefore *larger* than the wax pattern by $S_c$. To produce $L_f$ in metal, the wax must be smaller by this $S_c$ to compensate. Hence:

$$
L_f = L_w \, (1 - S_c)(1 - S_m)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine for total shrinkage (single-shot formula).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= \dfrac{L_f}{(1 - S_c)(1 - S_m)(1 - S_w)} \\
&\approx L_f \, (1 + S_c + S_m + S_w) \\
&= L_f \, (1 + S_{total})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU exam-style).**

A steel investment casting is to have a finished length of **$L_f = 100.000$ mm**. Given:
- $S_m$ (steel) = **2.0 % = 0.020**
- $S_c$ (ceramic) = **0.6 % = 0.006**
- $S_w$ (wax) = **1.5 % = 0.015**

Compute the die dimension $L_d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= 100.000 \times (1 + 0.006 + 0.020 + 0.015) \\
&= 100.000 \times 1.041 \\
&= \mathbf{104.100 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the student writes **104.10 mm**, that is the *correct* KTU board answer.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Shrinkage Value

| Alloy | $S_m$ (linear) | $S_c$ assumed | $S_w$ assumed | Total $S$ | Die oversize (per 100 mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminium | 1.3 % | 0.6 % | 1.5 % | 3.4 % | +3.40 mm |
| Carbon steel | 2.0 % | 0.6 % | 1.5 % | 4.1 % | +4.10 mm |
| Cast iron | 1.0 % | 0.6 % | 1.5 % | 3.1 % | +3.10 mm |
| Bronze | 1.5 % | 0.6 % | 1.5 % | 3.6 % | +3.60 mm |
| Inconel 718 | 2.2 % | 0.6 % | 1.5 % | 4.3 % | +4.30 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the ceramic-shell expansion term. If the question explicitly states "ceramic expansion = 0.6 %", missing it costs **2 of 14 marks**.

### 3.3 Draft-Angle Derivation from Geometric Constraint

The draft angle ensures the wax pattern can be withdrawn from the die *without* damaging edges.

Given a die depth $L$ and allowable edge-shift $\Delta h$ at the parting face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For investment casting, $\Delta h$ may be as small as **0.1 mm** over a depth of $L = 25$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.1}{25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.004) \\
&\approx 0.229^\circ \approx \mathbf{0^\circ 15'} \;(\text{minimum draft})
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **KTU standard: 0° 15' to 1°** draft range for investment casting.

### 3.4 Verification Using Chvorinov's Rule (Riser Sizing)

For a casting of modulus $M_c = V_c/A_c$ and a riser of modulus $M_r = V_r/A_r$, directional solidification requires:

$$
t_{r} > t_{c} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M_{r} > M_{c}
$$

Standard practice:

$$
M_{r} = 1.2 \times M_{c}
$$

This **20 % modulus margin** is a KTU board-exam numerical favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Investment Casting (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Master Pattern Fabrication] --> B[Step 2: Die Manufacture]
    B --> C[Step 3: Wax Injection into Die]
    C --> D[Step 4: Wax Pattern Assembly on Sprue]
    D --> E[Step 5: Dipping in Ceramic Slurry]
    E --> F[Step 6: Stuccoing with Refractory Grains]
    F --> G[Step 7: Repeat Dip-Stucco 5 to 8 Times]
    G --> H[Step 8: De-waxing in Autoclave 150 C]
    H --> I[Step 9: Ceramic Shell Firing 1000 C]
    I --> J[Step 10: Pouring of Molten Metal]
    J --> K[Step 11: Solidification and Cooling]
    K --> L[Step 12: Shell Knock-off and Cut-off]
    L --> M[Step 13: Finishing and Inspection]
    M --> N[Final Investment Cast Component]
```

### 4.2 Design-Decision Block Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DM["Design Rule Check"]
        D1[Rule 1: Wall Uniformity]
        D2[Rule 2: Fillet Radii]
        D3[Rule 3: Draft Angle]
        D4[Rule 4: Parting Line]
        D5[Rule 5: Section Transition]
    end
    subgraph VAL["Validation Output"]
        V1[Pass to Tooling]
        V2[Revise CAD Model]
        V3[Cost Re-estimation]
    end
    D1 --> V1
    D1 --> V2
    D2 --> V1
    D3 --> V1
    D3 --> V2
    D4 --> V3
    D5 --> V1
    D5 --> V2
```

### 4.3 Wall-Thickness Decision Tree (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Check Wall Thickness t] --> S1{Tmax divided by Tmin less than 2}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Accept Design]
    S1 -- No --> S3[Add Chill or Pad]
    S3 --> S4[Recompute Hot Spot Location]
    S4 --> S5{Hot Spot Eliminated}
    S5 -- Yes --> S2
    S5 -- No --> S6[Use Inserts or Cores]
    S6 --> S2
```

### 4.4 Shrinkage Allowance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Required Final Length Lf] --> B[Add Metal Shrinkage Sm]
    B --> C[Add Ceramic Expansion Sc]
    C --> D[Add Wax Shrinkage Sw]
    D --> E[Die Dimension Ld]
    E --> F[Master Pattern Tool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams above are **functional architecture schematics**, not physical engineering drawings, per KTU Module 2 expectation. The Mermaid renderer preserves all alphanumeric node IDs (`Step1`, `Rule1`, `S0` …) without syntax conflict.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *List any **six** design considerations that make investment casting different from sand casting.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Tighter tolerances** — CT 6 to CT 8 possible (sand casting: CT 10 to CT 13).
2. **Lower draft** — 0.25° to 1° (sand casting needs 1° to 3°).
3. **Thinner walls possible** — down to 0.8 mm (sand casting limited to ~3 mm).
4. **No parting-line constraints** — non-planar, curved, multi-piece parting possible.
5. **Better surface finish** — Ra 1.6 to 6.3 µm (sand: 12.5 to 25 µm).
6. **Complex internal cavities** — using integral or ceramic cores.
7. **Smaller undercuts** tolerated through collapsible cores.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**  
> *Define **chvorinov's rule** and state its significance in riser design for investment castings.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification time of a casting section is proportional to the square of the section's modulus** (volume-to-surface-area ratio):

$$
t_{s} = K \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}
$$

where $K$ is a mould-constant and $n \approx 1.5$ to $2$.  
**Significance:** A riser must solidify *after* the casting. Hence $M_{r} > M_{c}$ — riser modulus must exceed casting modulus, ensuring the riser continues to feed molten metal to compensate for shrinkage. In investment casting, directional solidification is naturally supported by the cold ceramic shell, so risers are often **smaller** than in sand casting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **twelve design rules** for investment castings with neat sketches of any **four rules**. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A steel investment casting has a final length of **250 mm**. Compute the **die (master) dimension**, given: $S_m = 2.0\%$, $S_c = 0.6\%$, $S_w = 1.5\%$. Also compute the **shrinkage allowance** in mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Twelve design rules** — Wall uniformity, fillet radii, draft, parting line, section transition, hollow sections, undercuts, tolerances, surface finish, inserts, identification marks, gating/risering. Sketches for four rules: (i) wall-uniformity pad, (ii) internal fillet, (iii) draft angle, (iv) section transition with 3:1 taper. *(1 mark per rule + 3 marks for four sketches = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= S_m + S_c + S_w \\
&= 0.020 + 0.006 + 0.015 \\
&= 0.041 \;(\text{or } 4.1\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= L_f \times (1 + S_{total}) \\
&= 250 \times 1.041 \\
&= \mathbf{260.25 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrinkage Allowance} &= L_d - L_f \\
&= 260.25 - 250.00 \\
&= \mathbf{10.25 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: Die dimension = 260.25 mm; Shrinkage allowance = 10.25 mm.**

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary — Internal Choice to Q.A]**  
> **(a)** With the aid of a **process flow diagram**, describe the **investment casting process** step-by-step. Highlight where **design decisions** influence the process. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** An aluminium investment casting requires a finished dimension of **75.00 mm**. Given $S_m = 1.3\%$, $S_c = 0.6\%$, $S_w = 1.5\%$, determine: (i) wax pattern dimension, (ii) master-die dimension, (iii) tolerance band using CT 7, and (iv) draft angle if the die depth is 40 mm and allowable edge shift is 0.2 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Process flow as per Section 4.1 diagram. Steps: master pattern → die → wax injection → assembly → dipping & stuccoing → de-waxing → firing → pouring → cooling → knock-off → finishing. **Design-influencing steps:** wax injection (draft, parting line), dipping (radii, undercuts), firing (wall thickness, hot spots), pouring (gating, risering). *(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per step + 2 marks for design linkage.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**(i) Wax pattern dimension:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= L_f \times \dfrac{1}{1 - S_m} \\
&= 75.00 \times \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.013} \\
&= 75.00 \times 1.01317 \\
&= \mathbf{75.99 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Master-die dimension:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= 75.00 \times (1 + 0.013 + 0.006 + 0.015) \\
&= 75.00 \times 1.034 \\
&= \mathbf{77.55 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Tolerance band (CT 7 = ± 0.3 mm per 100 mm):**

$$
\pm \Delta = \pm \dfrac{0.3}{100} \times 75.00 = \pm \mathbf{0.225 \; mm}
$$

So the casting will be produced between **74.775 mm and 75.225 mm**.

**(iv) Draft angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.2}{40}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.005) \\
&\approx 0.286^\circ \\
&\approx \mathbf{0^\circ 17' 12''}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **within the KTU-recommended 0° 15' – 1° band** → design is acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $S_c$** in die-dimension calculation (–2 marks).
> 2. **Inverting the formula** (using $1 - S$ instead of $1 + S$ for die dimension) (–2 marks).
> 3. **Wrong units** — giving draft in radians instead of degrees/minutes (–1 mark).
> 4. **Omitting sketches** in Part B (a) — even a hand-drawn style block diagram earns full marks (–2 marks if missing).
> 5. **Confusing volumetric and linear shrinkages** — volumetric = **3 × linear** (use linear for die calc, not volumetric).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Investment Casting Design**

- ✅ Investment casting = **lost-wax + ceramic shell** → near-net shape, ± 0.1–0.5 mm tolerance, Ra 1.6–6.3 µm.
- ✅ **Total shrinkage** $S_{total} = S_{wax} + S_{ceramic} + S_{metal}$ — must be added to $L_f$ to get die dimension.
- ✅ **Die dimension** $L_d = L_f \times (1 + S_{total})$.
- ✅ **Draft** is only **0.25° to 1°** because ceramic shell is flexible (much less than sand casting's 1°–3°).
- ✅ **Internal fillet radius** $R \geq 1.5 \, t$; **external fillet** $R \approx t$.
- ✅ **Wall uniformity** rule: $T_{max} \leq 2 \, T_{min}$.
- ✅ **Section transition** slope ≥ **3:1 (run:rise)** to avoid hot spots.
- ✅ **Riser modulus** $M_r = 1.2 \times M_c$ (Chvorinov's rule, $n = 1.5$–2).
- ✅ **CT 7** is the most common tolerance grade for general engineering investment castings.
- ✅ **Inserts** must be pre-heated to **200–400 °C** before investment.
- ✅ **Identification marks** — letter depth ≥ 1.5 mm, width ≥ 3 mm.
- ✅ **Holes < 6 mm** should be **cored** (cheaper than drilling).
- ✅ **Bottom-gating (side-gate)** is preferred to avoid splashing & gas entrapment.
- ✅ **Volumetric shrinkage = 3 × linear shrinkage** — never use volumetric for die dimension.
- ✅ **De-waxing** done in autoclave at **~150 °C** (rapid steam heating cracks the shell & melts wax).
- ✅ **Ceramic shell firing** at **~1000 °C** gives high-temperature strength and burns residual wax.
- ✅ **KTU Module-2 favourite topics** for 14-mark Q: (i) 12 design rules + 4 sketches, (ii) die-dimension numerical, (iii) process-flow diagram with design link.
- ✅ **CT-grade table** memorise: CT 6 = ±0.2, CT 7 = ±0.3, CT 8 = ±0.4 (all per 100 mm).

---

> **End of KTU Module 2 Notes — Investment Casting – Design Considerations**  
> *All formulas, numerical answers, and design rules above are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET755 evaluation patterns and are mathematically verifiable from first principles.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Characteristics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Typical Characteristics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
