<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Design for Assembly (DFA) and list any four Boothroyd guidelines for assembly design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Design for Assembly (DFA) is a systematic methodology that simplifies the product structure, reduces the number of parts, and minimizes assembly time and cost by applying empirical rules during the product design stage itself.

**Four Boothroyd Guidelines:**

1. **Minimize the number of parts** — every additional part increases handling, insertion, fastening, and inventory costs.
2. **Use self-locating features** — shoulders, chamfers, and tapers that guide the part into its correct position.
3. **Use self-fastening features** — snap-fits, heat-stakes, and integral hinges eliminate separate fasteners.
4. **Standardize fasteners** — restrict to one or two types/sizes per assembly.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain Boole's three groups of design recommendations with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

George Boole classified design rules into three strategic groups:

1. **Design for Development (Pre-manufacture)** — Decisions taken before tooling exists.
   *Example:* Modular product architecture — dividing a product into independent subassemblies so different teams can develop in parallel.

2. **Design for Production (Manufacturing)** — Rules that govern fabrication efficiency.
   *Example:* Draft angles of $1^\circ$–$3^\circ$ in sand castings to allow pattern withdrawal without damaging the mold cavity.

3. **Design for Distribution (Post-production)** — Rules for shipping, storage, and end-of-life.
   *Example:* Designing snap-fits instead of threaded fasteners to enable tool-free disassembly for recycling.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **design recommendations for injection-molded plastic parts**. Cover wall thickness, draft angles, radii, ribs, and bosses. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A product assembly has **5 functional requirements** but contains **12 parts**, with an average assembly time of **6 seconds per part**. Calculate the Boothroyd **DFA efficiency index**. Comment on the result and suggest **two design improvements**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — (a):**

Design Recommendations for Injection-Molded Plastic Parts:

**1. Uniform Wall Thickness**
- Recommended: **1.0 mm to 3.0 mm** depending on part size and resin.
- Reason: Non-uniform walls cause differential cooling → sink marks, voids, and warpage.
- Differential thickness ratio should be $\leq 1.5 : 1$.

**2. Draft Angle**
- Range: **$0.5^\circ$ to $2^\circ$** (higher for deep cavities).
- Reason: Allows part ejection from the mold without scoring or distortion.

**3. Radii (Fillets)**
- Minimum: **0.5 × nominal wall thickness**.
- Reason: Sharp corners cause stress concentration and hinder material flow.

**4. Ribs**
- Thickness: **0.5 to 0.75 × adjacent wall thickness**.
- Height: $\leq 3 \times$ wall thickness.
- Reason: Thicker ribs cause sink marks on the opposite face.

**5. Bosses**
- Outer diameter: $\leq 3 \times$ inner hole diameter.
- Use gussets at the base for rigidity.
- Reason: Prevents warping and provides self-tapping screw support.

**Model Solution — (b):**

Given: $N_m = 5$, $N_p = 12$, $t_a = 6$ s/part.

> **Step 1** — Compute total assembly time.
>
> $$T = N_p \cdot t_a = 12 \times 6 = 72 \text{ s}$$

> **Step 2** — Apply Boothroyd DFA formula.
>
> $$\eta_a = \dfrac{N_m}{N_p \cdot t_a} = \dfrac{5}{12 \times 6} = \dfrac{5}{72} = 0.0694$$

> **Step 3** — Compute Boothroyd parts-count index.
>
> $$D_i = \dfrac{N_m}{N_p} = \dfrac{5}{12} = 0.417$$

> **Step 4** — Interpretation.** The assembly uses **2.4× more parts** than functionally necessary. The efficiency is only **6.94%** of the theoretical optimum, indicating severe over-complication.

**Two Design Improvements:**

1. **Eliminate redundant fasteners** — replace separate screws + washers with integral snap-fits or molded-in hinges.
2. **Combine sub-functions into a single molded part** — use one plastic piece to perform what currently requires multiple metal pieces (e.g., a one-piece bracket-clip replaces a bracket + clip + screw).

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the **design recommendations for sand casting and forging** with respect to draft angles, fillets, parting lines, and undercuts. Present a comparative table. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A steel bracket is to be made from **3 mm thick cold-rolled steel sheet**. Determine **(i)** the minimum bend radius if $k = 1.0$, and **(ii)** the bend allowance for a **$90^\circ$ bend** using neutral axis factor $k_b = 0.33$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — (a):**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Forging (Closed-Die) |
|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | $1^\circ$–$3^\circ$ (small) | $3^\circ$–$7^\circ$ (larger) |
| Fillet radius | **Minimum 3 mm** to reduce stress | **Minimum 3 mm**, often 5–8 mm |
| Parting line | Single flat plane preferred | Symmetric about parting line |
| Undercuts | Avoided (use cores if needed) | Strictly avoided (impossible without complex dies) |
| Wall thickness | Uniform, **5–25 mm** typical | Controlled by die impression |
| Ribs | Avoid in casting | Ribs allowed but **3× wall thickness** |

**Model Solution — (b):**

> **Step 1** — Compute minimum bend radius.
>
> $$r_{min} = k \cdot t = 1.0 \times 3 = 3 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 2** — Compute the bend allowance.
>
> $$L_{bend} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (r_{min} + k_b \cdot t) \cdot \theta}{180^\circ}$$
>
> $$L_{bend} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3 + 0.33 \times 3) \times 90}{180}$$

$$L_{bend} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.99 \times 90}{180}$$

$$L_{bend} = \pi \times 3.99 \times 0.5 = 6.265 \text{ mm}$$

> **Step 3** — Final flat-pattern length.
>
> $$L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + 6.265 \text{ mm}$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the straight leg lengths.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Dec 2023 / June 2024 Batches):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always specify mm, µm, °, s in numerical answers. A correct number without unit = **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing DFM and DFA** — DFM = Manufacturing design; DFA = Assembly design. Many students interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping "why"** — Just listing a rule (e.g., "use draft angle $2^\circ$") without explaining *why* (e.g., "to allow pattern withdrawal without mold damage") costs **2 of the 3 marks** in Part A.
> 4. **Wrong formula in Boothroyd** — $\eta_a = N_m / (N_p \cdot t_a)$ is NOT $\eta_a = N_m / N_p$. The denominator includes *time*.
> 5. **No sketch in Part B** — Even a hand-drawn rough sketch with labeled features earns an **extra 1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Decimal precision** — Round to **3 decimal places** for dimensionless ratios (e.g., 0.417 not 0.42).
> 7. **Boole's groups** — Many students write only 2 groups. Always write **all three**: Development, Production, Distribution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design Recommendations (Module 3):**

- ✅ **Boole's three groups:** Development, Production, Distribution — *always* name all three.
- ✅ **DFA efficiency formula:** $\eta_a = N_m / (N_p \cdot t_a)$ — Boothroyd 1980.
- ✅ **Parts-count index:** $D_i = N_m / N_p$, ideally equals 1.
- ✅ **Draft angles:** Casting $1^\circ$–$3^\circ$ | Forging $3^\circ$–$7^\circ$ | Plastic $0.5^\circ$–$2^\circ$.
- ✅ **Wall thickness:** Must be uniform within ratio $1.5 : 1$ for plastics.
- ✅ **Rib rule:** $0.5$–$0.75 \times$ wall thickness to avoid sink marks.
- ✅ **Fillet rule:** Minimum 3 mm for castings and forgings, **never** sharp corners.
- ✅ **Sheet metal:** $r_{min} = k \cdot t$, $k \in [0.5, 2.0]$ depending on material.
- ✅ **Bend allowance formula:** $L_{bend} = \pi (r + k_b t) \theta / 180$.
- ✅ **Thread standardization:** Use ISO metric M-series (M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10, M12).
- ✅ **Surface roughness:** Specify $R_a$ in µm (e.g., $R_a = 1.6$ µm for machined surfaces).
- ✅ **Boothroyd's 3 core DFA principles:** (1) Minimize parts, (2) Self-locating features, (3) Self-fastening features.
- ✅ **Modular design** reduces development time via parallel work streams.
- ✅ **COTS** parts reduce cost and lead time — always preferred over custom.
- ✅ **Avoid deep pockets** > $4 \times$ width in machining (tool deflection risk).
- ✅ **Hole-to-edge distance** in sheet metal $\geq 1.5 \times$ hole diameter.
- ✅ **Snap-fits** preferred over screws when disassembly is needed.
- ✅ **One material per part** preferred for recyclability (DFD principle).
- ✅ **Tolerance doubling** doubles cost — never specify tighter than needed.
- ✅ **Part count ratio target:** aim for $N_p \leq 1.5 \times N_m$ in mature DFA designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining Round Holes - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machining Round Holes - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, *Machining Round Holes* refers to the systematic design philosophy and engineering methodology used to specify, dimension, and produce circular openings (holes/bores) in engineering components using subtractive manufacturing processes. Round holes are the **most frequently produced feature** in machined components and account for a disproportionately large share of total manufacturing cost.

A round hole, in KTU 2024 DFMA terminology, is defined as:
> A cylindrical void of finite depth, characterized by its **nominal diameter (D)**, **depth (h)**, **tolerance grade (IT)**, **surface finish (Ra)**, and **positional accuracy (true position)**, produced by a chip-removal or material-deformation process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Round holes are introduced as the *single most cost-influencing feature* in machined parts. The DFMA approach mandates that designers select the **fewest holes possible**, the **largest standard sizes**, and the **loosest functional tolerances** compatible with assembly function.

### 1.2 The Eight Primary Characteristics of a Machined Round Hole

Every hole specified on a drawing is governed by the following eight design parameters, collectively known as the **Hole Specification Octet**:

1. **Diameter (D)** — measured in mm
2. **Depth (h)** — measured in mm
3. **Tolerance grade** — e.g., **H7, H8, H11**
4. **Surface roughness (Ra)** — measured in µm
5. **True position tolerance** — e.g., **Ø0.2 at MMC**
6. **Hole type** — *through, blind, stepped, counter-bored, counter-sunk, tapped*
7. **Material** — determines achievable tolerance and cost
8. **Quantity (batch size)** — influences process selection

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a tailor stitching a button onto a shirt. To thread the button-hole, you use a needle that *just fits* the thread. Now imagine the factory has only ONE needle size — every shirt must use that exact thread thickness.

**Round holes in engineering work the same way:**

- A designer draws a **Ø 13.7 mm** hole because of some local stress calculation.
- The factory, however, has standard drills of **Ø 13, Ø 13.5, Ø 14 mm**.
- Result: a *non-standard size* is ordered, custom tools are set up, costs rise by **400–800%**, and lead time grows by days.

> [!NOTE]
> **The DFMA Golden Rule of Holes:** *"Use the standard size that is BIGGER than the smallest functional requirement."* This single design habit eliminates the largest single source of unnecessary machining cost.

### 1.4 Why Round Holes Dominate Manufacturing Cost

Statistical surveys of machined components reveal the following **Boothroyd cost distribution**:

- Round holes represent **≈ 25 % of all features** drawn on mechanical parts.
- They account for **≈ 50 % of all machining time** on a typical prismatic part.
- A redesign that consolidates two Ø 10 mm holes into one Ø 12 mm hole (both satisfying the same fastener clearance) can reduce hole-making cost by **30–45 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Hole Size Families (KTU Reference):** Preferred diameters are taken from the **R20 (Renard) series** — e.g., 1, 1.25, 1.6, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50 mm — extending with R40 and R80 sub-series.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DFMA Rationale for Studying Round Holes

Round holes are treated as a **separate module** in KTU DFMA because:

- They are produced by **multiple competing processes** (drilling, reaming, boring, EDM, laser, etc.).
- They require **fixturing**, **tool selection**, **chuck/collet system**, and **chip evacuation** — all of which the designer must understand to specify parts efficiently.
- Hole **tolerance and fit** (Hole-basis / Shaft-basis) directly dictate assembly cost and performance.

### 2.2 Classification of Hole-Making Processes

Holes are made by **five process families**, listed in ascending order of achievable accuracy and cost:

| Process | Typical Tolerance | Surface Roughness Ra (µm) | Relative Cost | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Punching / Blanking** | ± 0.10 mm | 1.6 – 6.3 | **1× (lowest)** | Thin sheet, large batches |
| **Drilling** | IT 10 – IT 12 | 1.6 – 6.3 | **3×** | General through / blind holes |
| **Reaming** | IT 7 – IT 8 | 0.4 – 1.6 | **6×** | Finishing after drilling |
| **Boring** | IT 6 – IT 7 | 0.4 – 1.6 | **10×** | Large diameters, single setup |
| **EDM / Laser / Water-jet** | IT 7 – IT 9 | 0.8 – 3.2 | **8–15×** | Hard / exotic materials |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Multiplier Rule:** Every escalation of one tolerance grade (e.g., H11 → H9 → H7) typically *doubles* the hole-making cost. From H7 → H6 the cost may *triple*. Designers must therefore *demand only the precision actually required.*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations form the **core computational toolkit** for round-hole analysis in DFMA.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | Cutting speed = π × diameter × spindle rpm | m/min |
| 2 | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | Feed rate = feed per rev × rpm | mm/min |
| 3 | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot f \cdot N$ | Material Removal Rate during drilling | mm³/min |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot \eta}$ | Required machining power | kW |
| 5 | $T_h = \dfrac{L_h}{f_r}$ | Hole-making time per hole | min |
| 6 | $\dfrac{h}{D}$ | Depth-to-diameter ratio (drillability limit ≈ 5) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_{hole} = k_1 \cdot \rho^{k_2}$ | Cost per hole as a power law of radius ρ (Boothroyd) | ₹ / hole |
| 8 | $n_{std} = n_{req} \cdot k_{eq}$ | Standardization index = holes redesigned ÷ holes required | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition — Drillability:** Standard twist drills can reliably produce holes only when **h/D ≤ 5** for through-holes and **h/D ≤ 3** for blind holes. Beyond this, **peck-drilling, gun-drilling, or EDM** must be used, each adding **2× to 5× cost**.

### 2.4 The Boothroyd Cost Function (Detailed)

Boothroyd, Dewhurst, and Knight (DFMA Handbook) express the cost of producing a feature as:

$$
C_{feature} = C_{material} + C_{machining}
$$

For a *round hole*, the machining cost per hole follows a **power-law** in the hole radius:

$$
C_{hole} = C_{0} \cdot \rho^{n}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = hole radius (mm)
- $C_{0}$ = base cost constant (depends on material, machine, batch)
- $n$ = process exponent (typically **1.6 to 2.0** for drilling)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This equation is used by DFMA software (e.g., *DFMA Pro*, *Boothroyd Dewhurst*) to flag a feature as **redundant** when its removal causes zero functional penalty. Designers are urged to combine two small holes into one larger one wherever possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of the Three Foundational Hole-Machining Equations

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed $V_c$

**Starting Physical Principle:** Cutting speed is the linear velocity at which the *outer cutting edge* of a rotating tool travels through the workpiece.

- Let $D$ = tool diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle rpm (rev/min).
- In one revolution, a point on the tool periphery travels a *circumferential distance* equal to the circle perimeter.

$$
\text{Distance per rev} = \pi \cdot D \quad (\text{mm})
$$

- In $N$ revolutions per minute, total distance travelled per minute:

$$
V_c = N \times \pi \cdot D \quad (\text{mm/min})
$$

- Convert to standard unit (m/min):

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad (\text{m/min})
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We divide by 1000 because 1 m = 1000 mm. This is the form used in every standard machining data table (e.g., *Machining Data Handbook*).

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Drilling

**Starting Physical Principle:** MRR equals the *volume of material removed per unit time*. For a drill, the cross-sectional area swept per revolution is the **circular area of the drill**.

- Cross-sectional area of drill:

$$
A_{drill} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \quad (\text{mm}^{2})
$$

- In one revolution, the drill advances axially by an amount equal to the feed-per-revolution $f$ (mm/rev). Therefore, *volume removed per revolution*:

$$
V_{rev} = A_{drill} \times f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot f \quad (\text{mm}^{3}/\text{rev})
$$

- Per minute (with $N$ rev/min):

$$
MRR = V_{rev} \times N = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot f \cdot N \quad (\text{mm}^{3}/\text{min})
$$

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Required Machining Power

**Starting Physical Principle:** Power equals *force times velocity* (P = F × V). In machining, the cutting force is the product of the *specific cutting energy* ($k_c$) and the *undeformed chip cross-section* (which equals MRR × time-base).

$$
P = k_c \times \dot{V}_{chip}
$$

- Convert MRR from mm³/min to m³/s (1 mm³ = 10⁻⁹ m³ and 1 min = 60 s):

$$
\dot{V}_{chip} = \dfrac{MRR}{60 \times 10^{9}} \quad (\text{m}^{3}/\text{s})
$$

- Substituting into the power equation and accounting for machine efficiency $\eta$:

$$
P = \dfrac{k_c \times MRR}{60 \times 10^{9} \times \eta} \quad (\text{Watts})
$$

- Express in kW:

$$
P_{kW} = \dfrac{k_c \times MRR}{60 \times 10^{6} \times \eta}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Cost & Time Estimation for an M10 Clearance Hole

> **Problem Statement:** A steel bracket requires **6 holes of Ø 10.5 mm (H11)** through a thickness of 20 mm. The available drill runs at $V_c$ = 25 m/min with feed $f$ = 0.15 mm/rev on a milling machine with $\eta$ = 0.80. Specific cutting energy $k_c$ = 2000 N/mm². Determine: (a) spindle rpm, (b) MRR, (c) cutting power, (d) total hole-making time for 6 holes.

#### (a) Spindle RPM

$$
D = 10.5 \text{ mm}, \quad V_c = 25 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 10.5}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{25000}{32.987} \approx 757.9 \text{ rpm}
$$

#### (b) Material Removal Rate

$$
MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot f \cdot N
$$

$$
MRR = \dfrac{\pi \times 10.5^{2}}{4} \times 0.15 \times 757.9
$$

$$
MRR = 86.59 \times 0.15 \times 757.9 \approx 9846 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

#### (c) Cutting Power

$$
P = \dfrac{k_c \times MRR}{60 \times 10^{6} \times \eta} = \dfrac{2000 \times 9846}{60 \times 10^{6} \times 0.80}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{1.9692 \times 10^{7}}{4.8 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.410 \text{ kW}
$$

#### (d) Total Hole-Making Time for 6 Holes

$$
f_r = f \times N = 0.15 \times 757.9 = 113.7 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
T_{1\,hole} = \dfrac{L_h}{f_r} = \dfrac{20}{113.7} \approx 0.176 \text{ min} = 10.55 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{6\,holes} = 6 \times 0.176 = 1.056 \text{ min} \approx 63.4 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the entire batch of 6 holes is produced in **just over one minute** of spindle engagement. However, in real production, *tool change* (≈ 15 s) and *indexing* (≈ 5 s/hole) add ≈ 45 s of *non-cutting* time — a fact that justifies the Boothroyd principle of *minimizing the number of holes*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DFMA Design Decisions for Round Holes

| Design Question | DFMA Recommendation | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Is the hole strictly necessary? | Eliminate if fastener can be replaced by clip / snap | **High** (−100 %) |
| Can hole diameter be standardized? | Snap to nearest R20 size | **Medium** (−30 to −45 %) |
| Can two small holes be merged? | Combine into one larger hole | **High** (−25 to −40 %) |
| Can hole be blind instead of through? | Yes, if possible | **Medium** (−20 %) |
| Can tolerance grade be relaxed? | From H7 → H9 if function allows | **High** (−50 %) |
| Can surface roughness be relaxed? | From Ra 0.8 → Ra 3.2 | **Low** (−10 %) |
| Can hole be made by casting / MIM? | Yes, if diameter ≥ 1 mm | **Very High** (−70 to −90 %) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Hole-Making Process Selection

The following block diagram maps the **decision tree** that a DFMA designer follows when specifying a round hole. It captures process selection logic that is impractical to render as a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Hole Required] --> B{Standard R20 Size Available?}
    B -- No --> B1[Redesign to nearest standard size]
    B1 --> C
    B -- Yes --> C{Hole Diameter D in mm?}
    C -- D less than 1 --> D1[Use EDM or Micro-drilling]
    C -- D between 1 and 50 --> D2[Use Drilling / Reaming]
    C -- D greater than 50 --> D3[Use Boring from Pilot Hole]
    D1 --> E{Tolerance IT Grade?}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E -- IT 10 to IT 12 --> E1[Drilling Only]
    E -- IT 7 to IT 9 --> E2[Drilling + Reaming]
    E -- IT 6 to IT 7 --> E3[Drilling + Boring]
    E1 --> F[Apply DFMA Cost Check]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Finalize Drawing Specification]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Hole Specification Octet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Diameter D] --> OCT[Hole Specification Octet]
    P2[Depth h] --> OCT
    P3[Tolerance Grade IT] --> OCT
    P4[Surface Roughness Ra] --> OCT
    P5[True Position] --> OCT
    P6[Hole Type] --> OCT
    P7[Material] --> OCT
    P8[Batch Size] --> OCT
    OCT --> OUT[Manufacturable, Inspectable, Cost-Effective Hole]
```

### 4.3 Process Cost Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Punching - Cost 1x] --> L2[Drilling - Cost 3x]
    L2 --> L3[Reaming - Cost 6x]
    L3 --> L4[Boring - Cost 10x]
    L4 --> L5[EDM - Cost 12x]
    L5 --> L6[Fine Boring - Cost 15x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each step upward in the diagram represents a **multiplicative cost penalty**. Designers must *demand only the accuracy required*; overspecification cascades cost exponentially.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Define the term "round hole" in the context of DFMA. List the eight primary characteristics used to specify a round hole on an engineering drawing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *round hole* is a cylindrical void of finite depth, characterized by its diameter, depth, tolerance grade, surface finish, true position, hole type, material, and batch size. The eight primary characteristics are:

1. Diameter (D)
2. Depth (h)
3. Tolerance grade (e.g., H7, H8, H11)
4. Surface roughness (Ra)
5. True position tolerance
6. Hole type (through, blind, counter-bore, etc.)
7. Workpiece material
8. Batch / production quantity

*(Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for listing the eight characteristics.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*State the "DFMA Golden Rule of Holes" and explain why standard R20 diameters are preferred over arbitrary sizes.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rule:** *"Use the standard size that is BIGGER than the smallest functional requirement."*

**Reason:** Standard R20 diameters (1, 1.25, 1.6, 2, 2.5 … 50 mm) match the *preferred numbers* stocked by tool manufacturers. Non-standard sizes require special drills, custom orders, longer lead times, and cost **400–800 % more** than standard sizes. They also cannot be reground or replaced from inventory during breakdown maintenance.

*(Award 1 mark for stating the rule; 2 marks for the cost rationale.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** *Part B carries 14 marks with module-level internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer ONE full question.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss, with a labelled sketch, the **five primary process families** used to manufacture round holes. For each, give one typical tolerance grade and one representative application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cast-iron bracket has **8 holes of Ø 12 mm** through a thickness of 15 mm. Calculate the *spindle speed*, *MRR*, and *time per hole* given: $V_c$ = 30 m/min, $f$ = 0.20 mm/rev. Comment on whether the depth-to-diameter ratio is within safe drilling limits. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

The five primary process families are:

1. **Punching / Blanking** — IT 12 to IT 13; used for thin sheet metal in mass production (e.g., electrical chassis).
2. **Drilling** — IT 10 to IT 12; the most common hole-making process, used for general through and blind holes (e.g., fastener clearance).
3. **Reaming** — IT 7 to IT 9; finishing operation after drilling for precision holes (e.g., bearing bores).
4. **Boring** — IT 6 to IT 7; enlarging existing holes on lathe / boring bar, used for large-diameter precision holes.
5. **EDM / Laser / Water-jet** — IT 7 to IT 9; non-contact processes used for hard or exotic materials (e.g., tungsten carbide dies).

*(Award 1.4 marks per process × 5 = 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

- Given: $D$ = 12 mm, $h$ = 15 mm, $V_c$ = 30 m/min, $f$ = 0.20 mm/rev.

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{30000}{\pi \times 12} = 795.8 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 12^{2}}{4} \times 0.20 \times 795.8 = 113.1 \times 159.16 = 18000 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Feed Rate and Time per Hole:**

$$
f_r = 0.20 \times 795.8 = 159.16 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
T_{hole} = \dfrac{15}{159.16} = 0.0943 \text{ min} \approx 5.66 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Depth-to-Diameter Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{h}{D} = \dfrac{15}{12} = 1.25
$$

Since 1.25 < 5 (through-hole limit), the operation is **safe** with a standard twist drill. No peck-drilling required.

*(Award 2 marks for spindle speed; 2 marks for MRR; 2 marks for time; 1 mark for the h/D comment.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd cost function** for a round hole and describe how it is used to evaluate hole-elimination and hole-consolidation decisions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A designer has specified **four Ø 8 mm clearance holes** on a steel cover plate for mounting M8 bolts. Using DFMA principles, suggest how these four holes could be redesigned to reduce manufacturing cost. Justify each suggestion quantitatively where possible. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

The Boothroyd cost function expresses the cost of producing a feature as a function of its geometric size. For a round hole of radius $\rho$:

$$
C_{hole} = C_{0} \cdot \rho^{n}
$$

where $C_{0}$ is a base cost constant (function of material, machine, batch size) and $n$ is the process exponent (≈ 1.6 to 2.0 for drilling).

**Application 1 — Hole Elimination:** If a feature has *zero functional penalty upon removal*, the function flags it as a candidate for deletion. Removing the hole saves 100 % of $C_{hole}$.

**Application 2 — Hole Consolidation:** Two Ø 8 mm holes (radius 4 mm each) cost $2 \times C_0 \times 4^n$. If functionally equivalent to one Ø 16 mm hole (radius 8 mm), the new cost is $1 \times C_0 \times 8^n$. With $n$ = 2:

$$
\text{Saving} = \dfrac{2 \times 16 - 1 \times 64}{2 \times 16} = \dfrac{-32}{32} = -100\% \text{ (an apparent doubling of cost!)}
$$

This indicates *consolidation does not always reduce cost*; the decision must be driven by **functional analysis**, not by formula alone.

*(Award 3 marks for the equation and meaning; 4 marks for the two applications.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

| # | Original Feature | Redesigned Feature | Cost / Functional Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 × Ø 8 mm clearance | 2 × Ø 10 mm clearance with elongated slots | Allows positional tolerance relaxation; tolerance grade from H8 → H11; cost saving ≈ 50 %. |
| 2 | 4 separate drilled holes | 4 holes punched in sheet-metal blank | If thickness < 3 mm, punching replaces drilling; cost multiplier **3× → 1×**. |
| 3 | Tolerance H8 | Relax to H11 | H8 holes cost ≈ 4× H11 holes; function (clearance fit) does not require H8. |
| 4 | Ra 1.6 µm | Ra 3.2 µm | Surface roughness reduction adds reaming step; elimination saves ≈ 20 %. |

**Aggregate Saving:** Combining all four redesigns typically yields **40 – 60 % cost reduction** in the hole-making sub-process.

*(Award 7 marks distributed across the four redesigns with quantitative justification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU DFMA Examinations:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in $V_c$ calculations. **Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.** KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for dimensional errors.
> 2. **Confusing Hole-Basis and Shaft-Basis Fits:** ISO fits (H7/g6) are *hole-basis* unless stated otherwise. Writing "g7/H6" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring the h/D Ratio:** Forgetting to verify drillability. Examiners deduct 1 mark if h/D > 5 is left unchecked.
> 4. **Using Non-Standard Diameters in Solutions:** Examiners award zero for answers recommending Ø 13.7 mm instead of standard Ø 14 mm.
> 5. **Forgetting Efficiency $\eta$:** Machine power calculations must include $\eta$ (typically 0.75 to 0.85). Forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Machining Round Holes (Introduction)**

- ⭐ **Round holes are the single most cost-influencing feature** in machined parts.
- ⭐ **Eight characteristics** define every hole: D, h, tolerance, Ra, position, type, material, batch.
- ⭐ **Standard R20 diameters** are mandatory wherever functional clearance allows.
- ⭐ **Five process families**: Punching, Drilling, Reaming, Boring, EDM — with cost multipliers **1×, 3×, 6×, 10×, 12×**.
- ⭐ **Drillability limit:** $h/D \leq 5$ for through, $\leq 3$ for blind holes.
- ⭐ **Cost equation:** $C_{hole} = C_{0} \cdot \rho^{n}$, with $n \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0$.
- ⭐ **Cutting speed:** $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 1000$ (m/min).
- ⭐ **MRR:** $MRR = \pi D^{2}/4 \times f \times N$ (mm³/min).
- ⭐ **Power:** $P = k_c \cdot MRR / (60 \times 10^{6} \times \eta)$ (kW).
- ⭐ **DFMA Golden Rule:** *"Biggest standard size that satisfies function."*
- ⭐ **Loosest functional tolerance** is always preferred (H11 unless function demands H7).
- ⭐ **Hole consolidation** is desirable but must be *functionally validated* before cost reduction is claimed.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suitable Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suitable Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, "Suitable Materials" refers to the systematic selection of engineering materials based on their compatibility with chosen manufacturing processes, assembly methods, functional performance requirements, cost constraints, and environmental considerations. The selection is governed by the principle that **no material is universally superior**; rather, the optimal material is one that satisfies a balanced trade-off between **manufacturability, assemblability, mechanical performance, durability, and life-cycle cost** for a specific product application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Material selection in DFMA is a multi-criteria decision-making process that integrates *property indices* (e.g., specific stiffness $E/\rho$, specific strength $\sigma_y/\rho$), *process capability windows*, and *design constraints* to converge on the most economically and functionally viable material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are building a **bridge for bicycles** versus a **bridge for heavy trucks**. Both are "bridges," but the material you choose differs drastically:

- For the **bicycle bridge**: You might choose lightweight aluminum or engineered wood (low weight, easy to fabricate, low cost).
- For the **truck bridge**: You would select high-strength steel or pre-stressed concrete (high load-bearing capacity, durability, but heavier and harder to process).

This analogy illustrates that **material selection is not about finding the "best" material in absolute terms** but about finding the **most suitable material** for a given *function*, *manufacturing route*, *assembly strategy*, and *budget*. In DFMA, an additional constraint is that the material must be *easy to manufacture* and *easy to assemble*. For example, a material requiring expensive vacuum furnaces or clean-room environments will inflate costs even if its mechanical properties are excellent.

### 1.3 Key Selection Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Specific Stiffness ($E/\rho$)** — *measured in MPa·cm³/g* — Resistance to elastic deformation per unit mass.
- **Specific Strength ($\sigma_y/\rho$)** — *measured in kN·m/kg* — Load-bearing capacity per unit mass.
- **Machinability Rating** — *percentage relative to free-cutting brass (100%)* — Ease of material removal.
- **Recyclability Index** — *qualitative score 1–5* — Environmental sustainability factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, students must be able to justify material selection using both **quantitative material indices** (Ashby's methodology) and **qualitative manufacturing constraints** (DFM/DFA compatibility).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Engineering Materials

Engineering materials used in design are broadly classified into four families:

1. **Metals and Alloys** — Ferrous (steels, cast irons) and Non-ferrous (aluminum, copper, titanium, magnesium).
2. **Polymers** — Thermoplastics (PE, PP, ABS, PA), Thermosets (epoxy, phenolic), Elastomers.
3. **Ceramics and Glasses** — Alumina, zirconia, silicon carbide, silica glass.
4. **Composites** — Fiber-reinforced polymers (CFRP, GFRP), metal-matrix composites (MMCs), particulate composites.

### 2.2 Selection Criteria Framework (The Five Pillars)

Material suitability is evaluated along five interlocking pillars:

- **Pillar 1 — Functional Performance:** Mechanical (strength, stiffness, toughness, fatigue), thermal (conductivity, expansion), electrical (resistivity), chemical (corrosion resistance).
- **Pillar 2 — Manufacturability:** Compatibility with primary and secondary processes (casting, forging, machining, forming, molding). Expressed via *process capability maps*.
- **Pillar 3 — Assemblability:** Compatibility with joining methods (welding, fastening, adhesive bonding, snap-fits).
- **Pillar 4 — Cost & Availability:** Raw material cost per kg, processing cost, lead time, supply chain reliability.
- **Pillar 5 — Sustainability:** Recyclability, embodied energy, end-of-life disposal, regulatory compliance (RoHS, REACH).

### 2.3 Why and How Behind the Selection Logic

**Why does manufacturability matter so strongly?**

Because a material that requires exotic tooling, controlled atmospheres, or post-process heat treatment adds **hidden manufacturing cost** that can exceed raw material cost by **5× to 10×**. The DFM principle states: *Design the part so that the material can be processed using the lowest-cost, highest-volume route available.*

**How is assemblability linked to material choice?**

Material properties directly dictate joining strategy:
- **Weldable steels** → Low-carbon grades (e.g., AISI 1018, 1020) preferred over high-carbon (cracking risk).
- **Aluminum alloys** → 6xxx series (e.g., 6061) preferred for weldability over 2xxx or 7xxx (heat-affected zone cracking).
- **Polymers** → Snap-fits work best with thermoplastics having 5–15% elongation (e.g., ABS, PA66).
- **Dissimilar joints** → Require careful galvanic corrosion analysis when joining dissimilar metals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Material Index / Property | Formula / Definition | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Stiffness | $E / \rho$ | MPa·cm³/g | Lightweight stiff structures (aerospace) |
| 2 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | kN·m/kg | Lightweight load-bearing parts |
| 3 | Specific Toughness | $K_{IC} / \rho$ | MPa·m^0.5·cm³/g | Damage-tolerant design |
| 4 | Springback Index | $\sigma_y / E$ | dimensionless | Sheet metal forming |
| 5 | Castability Rating | $C = f(\text{fluidity, shrinkage, T}_\text{melt})$ | qualitative | Casting process selection |
| 6 | Machinability Index | $M = \frac{V_{cut}}{V_{ref}} \times 100\%$ | % | Subtractive manufacturing |
| 7 | Forming Limit | $\text{FLD}_0$ | % strain | Sheet metal stamping |
| 8 | Thermal Distortion | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | mm | Dimensional accuracy |
| 9 | Galvanic Series Position | $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | V | Dissimilar metal joining |
| 10 | Cost-Per-Unit-Performance | $C_{eff} = \frac{\text{Cost}}{(\sigma_y / \rho)}$ | ₹·kg/kN·m | Economic optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **units** alongside any material index. Marks are frequently lost when students write $E/\rho$ without specifying MPa or GPa for numerator and g/cm³ or kg/m³ for denominator.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Material selection charts (Ashby plots) are used across industries:
- **Automotive:** Selecting high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels for crash-resistant body panels to balance formability and energy absorption.
- **Aerospace:** CFRP over aluminum for wing skins — superior specific stiffness reduces fuel burn by 15–20%.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Magnesium alloys for laptop chassis — best combination of EMI shielding, stiffness, and die-castability.
- **Medical Devices:** Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) for implants — biocompatibility + specific strength + corrosion resistance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Material Index for a Lightweight Ties (Axial Load)

**Problem Statement:** A tensile tie (a structural member under pure axial tension) must support an axial load $F$ without exceeding a maximum allowable elongation $\delta_{max}$, while minimizing mass $m$. Derive the material index that governs the optimal material choice.

**Given:**
- Applied axial load: $F$
- Maximum allowable elongation: $\delta_{max}$
- Tie geometry: length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$
- Material properties: Young's modulus $E$, density $\rho$, yield strength $\sigma_y$

**Objective:** Minimize mass $m = \rho A L$ subject to two constraints.

**Step 1 — Write the stiffness constraint.**

Elongation under axial load is given by:

$$
\delta = \frac{F L}{A E}
$$

The constraint $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$ gives:

$$
A \geq \frac{F L}{E \, \delta_{max}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the strength (failure) constraint.**

The stress in the tie must not exceed the yield strength (with a safety factor $N$ applied):

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{N}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A \geq \frac{N F}{\sigma_y}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the larger required area into the mass equation.**

The mass is:

$$
m = \rho A L \geq \rho L \cdot \max\left(\frac{F L}{E \, \delta_{max}},\ \frac{N F}{\sigma_y}\right)
$$

To minimize mass, we choose $A$ as the *minimum* area satisfying both constraints, i.e., $A = \max$ of the two lower bounds. For **stiffness-dominated** design (the $\delta_{max}$ requirement is more stringent):

$$
m_{stiff} = \rho L \cdot \frac{F L}{E \, \delta_{max}} = \frac{F L^2}{\delta_{max}} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E}
$$

The mass is minimized by maximizing $E/\rho$ (specific stiffness).

For **strength-dominated** design (the yield constraint is more stringent):

$$
m_{strong} = \rho L \cdot \frac{N F}{\sigma_y} = N F L \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}
$$

The mass is minimized by maximizing $\sigma_y / \rho$ (specific strength).

**Step 4 — Combined objective function.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material Index (stiffness)} &= \frac{E}{\rho} \\[6pt]
\text{Material Index (strength)} &= \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \\[6pt]
\text{Combined Index} &= \min\left(\frac{\rho}{E},\ \frac{N \rho}{\sigma_y}\right) \quad \text{(invert and minimize)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The most suitable material for a lightweight tie is the one that **maximizes $E/\rho$** when stiffness is critical, or **maximizes $\sigma_y/\rho$** when strength is critical — independently of the absolute values of $E$ or $\sigma_y$ alone.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Material vs. Manufacturing Process Compatibility

The following matrix maps each major material family against common manufacturing processes. Cells marked **★★★** indicate excellent compatibility, **★★** indicate workable with care, and **★** indicate poor or uneconomical compatibility.

| Material Family | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Injection Molding | CNC Machining | Sheet Forming | Forging | Additive Mfg. |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cast Iron | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Carbon Steel | ★★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Aluminum Alloys | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| Stainless Steel | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Copper Alloys | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Titanium Alloys | ★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Magnesium Alloys | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ |
| Thermoplastics | ★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ✗ | ★★★ |
| Thermosets | ★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★ |
| CFRP Composites | ★ | ✗ | ★★ | ★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★ |

### 3.3 Cost-Driven Derivation: Cost-Per-Unit-Strength

For economic comparison across materials competing for the same application, define:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{\text{Unit Cost (₹/kg)}}{\sigma_y / \rho \ (\text{kN·m/kg})}
$$

**Example calculation:**

| Material | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Yield $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | Cost (₹/kg) | $\sigma_y/\rho$ (kN·m/kg) | $C_{eff}$ (₹/kN·m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 7850 | 250 | 70 | 31.85 | 2.20 |
| Al 6061-T6 | 2700 | 240 | 250 | 88.89 | 2.81 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4430 | 880 | 4500 | 198.65 | 22.65 |
| GFRP | 1800 | 200 | 800 | 111.11 | 7.20 |

Despite its higher strength, titanium's $C_{eff}$ is **10× higher** than mild steel — explaining why Ti is reserved for aerospace/medical and not used in mass-market automotive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ashby-Style Material Selection Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the logical architecture of the Ashby material selection methodology, the de-facto standard used in KTU-aligned DFMA coursework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: TRANSLATE DESIGN REQUIREMENTS"] --> B["Identify Function e.g. Tie, Beam, Panel, Spring"]
    B --> C["Identify Constraints e.g. Load, Stiffness, Temperature, Geometry"]
    C --> D["Identify Objectives e.g. Minimize Mass, Minimize Cost, Maximize Life"]
    D --> E["STEP 2: SCREEN USING PROPERTY LIMITS"]
    E --> F["Eliminate Materials Failing Hard Constraints e.g. T_melt < 500C"]
    F --> G["STEP 3: RANK REMAINDER USING MATERIAL INDICES"]
    G --> H["Compute Index e.g. E/rho, sigma_y/rho, K_IC/rho"]
    H --> I["Plot on Ashby Chart Log E vs Log rho"]
    I --> J["STEP 4: APPLY MANUFACTURING FILTERS"]
    J --> K["Check Compatibility with Casting, Forming, Molding, Machining"]
    K --> L["STEP 5: INCORPORATE COST AND SUSTAINABILITY"]
    L --> M["Refine Ranking Using Rs per kg and Recyclability"]
    M --> N["STEP 6: FINAL CANDIDATE MATERIALS"]
    N --> O["Document Justification and Trade-off Matrix"]
```

### 4.2 Material Selection Decision Tree for DFMA Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Component Function and Load Case Defined"] --> load{"Primary Load Type?"}
    load -->|"Axial/Tension"| s1["Maximize sigma_y/rho"]
    load -->|"Bending"| s2["Maximize E^0.5/rho"]
    load -->|"Torsion"| s3["Maximize G^0.5/rho"]
    load -->|"Impact"| s4["Maximize K_IC/rho"]
    s1 --> proc1{"Process Route?"}
    s2 --> proc1
    s3 --> proc1
    s4 --> proc1
    proc1 -->|"High Volume Casting"| p1["Cast Iron, Al-Si, Mg Alloys"]
    proc1 -->|"Sheet Stamping"| p2["HSLA Steel, Al 5xxx, DP Steel"]
    proc1 -->|"Injection Molding"| p3["PP, PA66, ABS, PC"]
    proc1 -->|"Machining from Bar"| p4["Brass, Al 6061, Free-cut Steel"]
    proc1 -->|"Additive Mfg"| p5["Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, IN718"]
    p1 --> out["Optimal Material with DFM Justification"]
    p2 --> out
    p3 --> out
    p4 --> out
    p5 --> out
```

### 4.3 Material Family Property Comparison Chart (Visual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    BlockA["METALS: High E, High sigma_y, Moderate rho, Good Ductility, Recyclable"]
    BlockB["POLYMERS: Low E, Moderate sigma_y, Very Low rho, High Formability, Limited Temp"]
    BlockC["CERAMICS: Very High E, High sigma_y, Moderate rho, Brittle, High T_resistance"]
    BlockD["COMPOSITES: Tunable E and sigma_y, Low rho, Anisotropic, Process-Specific"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **block diagrams above use Mermaid syntax** in compliance with KTU digital note standards. The Block-Beta syntax (introduced in Mermaid v10) is used to display material-family property profiles side-by-side for comparative study.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "Material Index" as used in DFMA. With a suitable example, explain how the material index $E/\rho$ governs the selection of materials for a lightweight stiff beam.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **material index** is a combination of material properties that, when maximized or minimized, leads to the optimal material for a given design objective and constraint set. It decouples material choice from geometry by expressing performance purely as a function of intrinsic properties.

For a lightweight **stiff beam** of fixed length and stiffness, the mass $m = \rho A L$ must be minimized. From the bending stiffness relation $\delta = F L^3 / (3 E I)$, we obtain $I \propto F L^3 / (E \delta_{max})$. Since $I \propto A^2$ for a beam cross-section, the mass scales as $m \propto (\rho / E^{0.5})$. Therefore, the material index to maximize is $E^{0.5}/\rho$, and for a uniform axial tie it is $E/\rho$.

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for derivation/example.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*List any three criteria used for material selection in DFMA and briefly justify why each is important.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Manufacturability (1 mark):** The material must be compatible with the chosen manufacturing process. For example, magnesium alloys are excellent for die casting but poor for welding — so the process dictates material choice.
2. **Assemblability (1 mark):** The material must support the intended joining method. Thermoplastics enable snap-fit assembly, eliminating fasteners and reducing assembly cost.
3. **Cost-Per-Unit-Performance (1 mark):** Absolute cost is misleading; the cost-per-unit-strength or cost-per-unit-stiffness provides a fairer comparison across material families.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Ashby methodology of material selection**. With the help of a labeled schematic, describe each step from design translation to final material ranking. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **tie-rod in a suspension system** must carry an axial tensile load of **50 kN** with a maximum allowable elongation of **0.5 mm** over a length of **500 mm**. Compare the suitability of **mild steel (E = 200 GPa, ρ = 7850 kg/m³)** and **Al 6061-T6 (E = 70 GPa, ρ = 2700 kg/m³)** based on the specific stiffness index. Conclude with a justified recommendation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Ashby Methodology (7 Marks):**

The Ashby methodology, developed by Prof. Michael F. Ashby, is a systematic, chart-based approach to material selection. It consists of **six sequential steps:**

**Step 1 — Translation of Design Requirements (1 Mark):**
Convert the design problem into three categories:
- **Function:** What does the component do? (e.g., carry load, store energy, insulate)
- **Constraints:** Must-meet limits (e.g., load capacity, temperature, geometry)
- **Objectives:** Maximize or minimize (e.g., mass, cost, volume, energy)

**Step 2 — Screening Using Property Limits (1 Mark):**
Eliminate materials that violate hard constraints. For example, if operating temperature exceeds 300 °C, all thermoplastics are excluded. This dramatically reduces the candidate pool.

**Step 3 — Ranking by Material Indices (2 Marks):**
For each objective-constraint combination, derive the *material index* (as derived in Section 3.1 above). Plot candidates on **Ashby charts** (log–log plots of one property vs another) and identify which materials lie on or near the optimal index line.

**Step 4 — Manufacturing Process Filtering (1 Mark):**
Overlay the Ashby chart with a **process capability map** showing which processes can shape each material economically. This narrows the choice to those materials that can actually be manufactured at the required scale and tolerance.

**Step 5 — Cost and Sustainability Overlay (1 Mark):**
Apply cost indices (₹/kg) and environmental indices (embodied energy, recyclability) to the shortlist. This may flip the ranking if a slightly inferior mechanical material is dramatically cheaper or greener.

**Step 6 — Final Documentation and Trade-off Matrix (1 Mark):**
Document the final selection with a **trade-off matrix** listing strengths, weaknesses, risks, and design margin for each finalist material.

---

**Part (b) — Specific Stiffness Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $F = 50{,}000$ N
- $\delta_{max} = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 500$ mm $= 0.5$ m
- Mild Steel: $E_{st} = 200$ GPa, $\rho_{st} = 7850$ kg/m³
- Al 6061-T6: $E_{al} = 70$ GPa, $\rho_{al} = 2700$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Compute the required cross-sectional area from stiffness:**

$$
A_{required} = \frac{F L}{E \, \delta_{max}}
$$

For **Mild Steel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.5}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{25{,}000}{100 \times 10^6} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 250\ \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Al 6061-T6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{al} &= \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.5}{70 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{25{,}000}{35 \times 10^6} \\
&= 7.143 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 714.3\ \text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass for each candidate (length L = 0.5 m):**

For **Mild Steel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{st} &= \rho_{st} \cdot A_{st} \cdot L \\
&= 7850 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.981\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Al 6061-T6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{al} &= \rho_{al} \cdot A_{al} \cdot L \\
&= 2700 \times 7.143 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.964\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the specific stiffness indices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{st} &= \frac{200 \times 10^9}{7850} = 25.48 \times 10^6\ \text{N·m/kg} \\[6pt]
\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{al} &= \frac{70 \times 10^9}{2700} = 25.93 \times 10^6\ \text{N·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**

Aluminum 6061-T6 has a marginally higher $E/\rho$ ratio (≈ 1.8% higher) and yields nearly identical mass (~0.964 kg vs ~0.981 kg) when designed for identical stiffness. However, **mild steel is recommended** for a suspension tie-rod because:
- Suspension tie-rods are **strength-dominated** (cyclic fatigue loading), where $\sigma_y/\rho$ for steel is far superior.
- Steel offers **better fatigue resistance, impact toughness, and wear resistance.**
- Manufacturing (machining, forging) of steel is more universally available than aluminum in standard automotive workshops.

The aluminum variant would only be chosen if **weight reduction outweighs fatigue life** — e.g., in Formula 1 or aerospace suspension arms.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **material properties in selecting a manufacturing process** for an engineering component. Use at least **three examples** to illustrate your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **gear blank to be produced in batches of 50,000 units/year**, evaluate the suitability of **(i) Sand casting of cast iron, (ii) Powder metallurgy of brass, and (iii) Injection molding of PA66 (nylon)**. Justify your selection using DFMA principles and tabulate your comparison across at least four criteria. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Material–Process Linkage (7 Marks):**

Manufacturing process selection is governed by material properties. Three illustrative examples:

**Example 1 — Cast Iron and Casting (2 Marks):**
Cast iron has a **narrow freezing range** and **high fluidity** when molten, making it ideal for sand casting intricate shapes. However, its **low ductility** (≈ 1–2% elongation) makes it unsuitable for forging. The graphite flakes in gray cast iron also act as **stress concentrators**, so it is rarely welded — machining and adhesive bonding are preferred.

**Example 2 — Aluminum Alloys and Sheet Forming (2 Marks):**
Aluminum alloys (especially 5xxx and 6xxx series) exhibit **good ductility** (20–30% elongation) and a **low strain-hardening exponent**, enabling deep-drawing and stretch-forming operations. Their **low density** reduces the forming force required. However, their **high thermal expansion coefficient** (~23 × 10⁻⁶/°C) demands careful tooling design for tight tolerances.

**Example 3 — Thermoplastics and Injection Molding (3 Marks):**
Thermoplastics like PP, ABS, and PA66 have **sharp melting transitions** and **low viscosities above $T_m$**, making them perfect for injection molding into complex geometries. Their **elastic recovery** (1–3%) affects dimensional accuracy. However, they cannot be forged, machined only with sharp tools due to **low thermal conductivity**, and must be dried before processing (especially PA66, which absorbs moisture).

**Synthesis:** Each material "naturally" fits certain processes due to its intrinsic properties — *flowability, melting point, ductility, hardness, thermal conductivity, and reactivity*. DFMA dictates that designers align the material-process pair to **minimize secondary operations** (machining, heat treatment, surface finishing).

---

**Part (b) — Gear Blank Material/Process Selection (7 Marks):**

**Tabulated Comparison:**

| Criterion | Sand Cast Iron | Powder Metallurgy Brass | Injection Molded PA66 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Rate** (parts/hr) | 10–30 | 100–500 | 500–2000 |
| **Tensile Strength** (MPa) | 150–300 | 200–350 | 60–85 |
| **Dimensional Tolerance** (mm) | ±0.5 to ±1.0 | ±0.05 to ±0.15 | ±0.05 to ±0.20 |
| **Surface Finish** (Ra μm) | 6.3–12.5 | 1.6–3.2 | 0.8–1.6 |
| **Tooling Cost** (₹ lakhs) | 0.5–2 | 10–50 | 20–80 |
| **Batch Suitability** (50,000/yr) | Low rate, OK | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Machinability Post-Process** | Required (gears need teeth) | Minimal (near-net shape) | Minimal |
| **Weight** (gear blank) | Heavy | Heavy | 1/5th of metal |
| **Noise Dampening** | Poor | Poor | Good (polymer) |

**Justification (3 Marks):**

For a gear blank at **50,000 units/year**:
- **Sand casting of cast iron** is unsuitable because the production rate is too low and dimensional tolerance of cast gears is poor — extensive post-casting machining would be required to cut gear teeth, negating cost savings.
- **Powder metallurgy of brass** is a strong candidate: it produces near-net-shape parts with good tolerances, minimal machining (only hobbing/shaping of teeth), and is economical at this volume. PM brass also has self-lubricating properties beneficial for gears.
- **Injection-molded PA66 (nylon)** is the best choice if the application permits plastic gears — extremely high production rate, no post-machining for many gear designs (modulus can be molded), quiet operation, and self-lubrication. However, it is unsuitable if the gear carries high torque or operates above 150 °C.

**Final Recommendation (1 Mark):** For **moderate-load, high-volume gear applications** (e.g., consumer appliances, toys, printers), **injection-molded PA66** is preferred. For **higher-load gears** in power transmission, **powder-metallurgy brass** is recommended. Sand-cast iron is **not recommended** for this batch size.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in "Suitable Materials" Questions:**
> 1. **Units Omission (–1 mark):** Writing $E/\rho = 25.48$ without specifying N·m/kg or MPa·cm³/g. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Density with Mass (–1 mark):** The material index uses *density*, not *mass*. A heavy but stiff material can still have a high $E/\rho$.
> 3. **Ignoring Manufacturing Constraints (–2 marks):** Recommending titanium for a low-cost consumer product without addressing its 10× higher cost. Always perform the cost overlay.
> 4. **Forgetting Safety Factor (–1 mark):** In strength calculations, never use $\sigma_y$ directly; apply a safety factor of $N = 1.5$ to $3.0$ depending on loading uncertainty.
> 5. **Writing "best material" instead of "most suitable material" (–1 mark):** KTU examiners penalize absolute statements. Always frame selection as context-dependent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Material selection is multi-objective**: there is no "best" material universally — only *most suitable* for a given function, process, cost, and environment.
- **Ashby methodology** is the gold-standard framework: *Translate → Screen → Rank with indices → Filter by process → Overlay cost → Document.*
- **Material indices** $E/\rho$ and $\sigma_y/\rho$ govern stiffness- and strength-dominated lightweight design respectively.
- **Manufacturability** = material must be processable by the chosen route at acceptable cost and tolerance.
- **Assemblability** = material must support the intended joining method (welding, snap-fit, adhesive, fastener).
- **Cost-per-unit-performance** ($C_{eff}$) is a more honest comparator than raw material price.
- **Sustainability** (recyclability, embodied energy) is now a KTU-graded criterion under the 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus.
- **Common exam traps:** missing units, forgetting safety factors, ignoring galvanic corrosion in dissimilar joints, recommending titanium for low-cost parts.
- **Key families to remember:** Metals (steel, Al, Ti, Mg, Cu), Polymers (PP, ABS, PA, PC), Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiC), Composites (CFRP, GFRP).
- **Process compatibility matrix** is high-yield for both Part A and Part B questions.
- **Final tip:** Always justify your selection with at least **three of the five pillars** (function, manufacturability, assemblability, cost, sustainability) to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Recommendations** in the context of Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA) constitute a codified set of process-specific geometric, dimensional, and tolerance guidelines that a product designer must follow to ensure a component can be produced economically, with consistent quality, and with minimum post-processing requirements. These recommendations act as a *bridge* between conceptual design and shop-floor manufacturability, translating functional requirements into manufacturable geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Design recommendations are empirical and analytical rules-of-thumb derived from accumulated manufacturing knowledge, covering casting, forging, machining, welding, sheet-metal forming, and plastics processing. They govern features such as **draft angles, fillet radii, wall thickness uniformity, parting line selection, undercut avoidance, and tolerance allocation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Adaptor" View

Imagine you are an architect designing a building. Before laying the first brick, you must know whether the bricks are clay, concrete, or glass. Each material has its own grammar — bricks need mortar gaps, glass needs expansion joints, concrete needs curing time. **Design Recommendations are exactly that grammar for engineers.** They tell the designer *what shape, what angle, what thickness* a feature must have so the chosen manufacturing process "speaks" the same language as the design.

| Manufacturing Process | "Language" of the Process | Typical Design Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting | Molten metal flow & solidification | Draft angle ≥ 1°–3° |
| Closed-die Forging | Plastic deformation under dies | Web thickness ≥ 3–5 mm |
| Turning/Milling | Cutting tool geometry & access | Internal corner radius ≥ tool nose radius |
| Resistance Welding | Electrode contact & current flow | Flange width ≥ 2× thickness |
| Sheet Metal Bending | Neutral axis shift & bend springback | Min. bend radius = 1× thickness |
| Injection Molding | Polymer flow & cooling shrinkage | Draft angle ≥ 0.5°–1° per side |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these rules non-negotiable?** Every manufacturing process has *intrinsic physical limitations* — minimum curvature dictated by tool geometry, minimum draft dictated by demoulding forces, minimum wall thickness dictated by flow dynamics. Ignoring them guarantees scrap, rework, or expensive tooling modifications.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Design Recommendations

1. **Geometric Recommendations** — shape, angles, radii, symmetry.
2. **Dimensional Recommendations** — thicknesses, lengths, hole spacings, edge distances.
3. **Tolerance & Specification Recommendations** — surface roughness, dimensional tolerances, datums.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU board-exam pitfall: students memorize rules but cannot justify *why* a rule exists. Always link the rule back to its **physical origin** (e.g., draft angle → demoulding friction; fillet radius → stress concentration + tool geometry).

---

## 1.4 Scope of Module 3

This module covers recommendations for the **six primary shaping processes**:

- Design for **Casting** (sand, die, investment)
- Design for **Forging** (open-die & closed-die)
- Design for **Machining** (turning, milling, drilling, grinding)
- Design for **Welding** (arc, resistance, spot)
- Design for **Sheet-Metal Working** (bending, deep drawing, piercing)
- Design for **Plastics** (injection molding, blow molding)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Recommendation Framework

Every manufacturing process imposes a *design envelope* — a permissible region of geometry. The role of a DFMA engineer is to **map the functional design into this envelope with minimum compromise**.

---

### 2.1.1 Design for Casting

**A. Wall Thickness Uniformity**

Molten metal solidifies from the mould wall inward. If sections have unequal thickness, the thinner section freezes first while the thicker one is still liquid → **hot tears, shrinkage voids, or warping** result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** Maintain uniform wall thickness wherever possible. Local thick sections should be cored out. Maximum variation in adjacent sections ≤ **2:1** to **3:1** ratio.

**B. Draft Angle (Taper)**

Required on faces perpendicular to the parting line so the casting can be ejected without damaging mould surfaces.

$$
\theta_d = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta L}{H}\right)
$$

where $\Delta L$ is the taper allowance and $H$ is the height in the mould.

Typical values:
- Sand casting: $\theta_d = 1°\text{ to } 3°$
- Die casting: $\theta_d = 0.5°\text{ to } 1.5°$
- Investment casting: $\theta_d \approx 0°$ to $1°$

**C. Fillet and Fillet Radii**

Sharp internal corners act as **stress raisers** and create hot-spot zones in castings. External corners are filleted to ease mould-making.

$$
r_{int} \geq 1.5 \cdot t \quad \text{(where } t = \text{wall thickness)}
$$

**D. Parting Line & Cores**

- Parting line should lie in a **single plane** when possible (no stepped parting).
- Use **draft on both sides** of the parting line.
- Cores increase cost → minimize internal undercuts. If unavoidable, consider **side cores** or **loose pieces**.

---

### 2.1.2 Design for Forging

Forging involves plastic deformation of hot (or cold) metal between dies. Material flows under compressive stress.

**A. Draft Angle**

Required for die withdrawal. Typical values:
- Open-die forging: $3°$ to $7°$
- Closed-die forging: $5°$ to $10°$ (depending on depth)

**B. Web Thickness & Rib Design**

Webs must be thick enough to fill but thin enough to avoid excessive material waste and die chill.

$$
t_{web,min} \approx 3 \cdot t_{rib}
$$

**C. Rib-to-Rib Spacing**

To allow metal flow between ribs:

$$
s \geq 2 \cdot t_{rib} + 3 \cdot r_{fillet}
$$

**D. Fillet Radii**

$$
r_{fillet} \geq 1.5 \cdot t \quad \text{and} \quad r_{fillet} \leq 0.15 \cdot t_{web}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-die forging** uses simpler tools (flat dies, V-dies); **Closed-die forging** uses cavity dies and produces near-net shapes. Design rules are stricter for closed-die forging due to flash formation.

---

### 2.1.3 Design for Machining

**A. Corner Radii (Internal)**

Internal corner radius must be **at least equal to the tool nose radius** to avoid scallops and unreadable geometry.

$$
r_{internal} \geq r_{tool\,nose}
$$

Standard tool nose radii: **0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.6 mm**.

**B. Depth of Cut / Shoulder Accessibility**

Tool clearance dictates minimum reachable shoulder height:

$$
h_{shoulder} \leq L_{tool} \cdot \tan(\theta_{approach})
$$

**C. Hole Diameters**

- Standard drill sizes preferred.
- Avoid blind holes deeper than $4 \times$ diameter (chip evacuation).
- For tapped holes, provide run-out (chamfer + clearance) of $0.5 \times$ pitch.

**D. Tolerance vs. Process Capability**

| Process | Typical Tolerance (IT Grade) | Surface Roughness $R_a$ (μm) |
|---|---|---|
| Turning | IT 8 – IT 11 | 0.8 – 6.3 |
| Milling | IT 10 – IT 12 | 1.6 – 12.5 |
| Grinding | IT 5 – IT 7 | 0.2 – 1.6 |
| Broaching | IT 6 – IT 8 | 0.4 – 3.2 |
| EDM | IT 6 – IT 8 | 0.4 – 1.6 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "What tolerance should be specified?", always respond with the *widest tolerance that the function allows*. Tighter tolerances dramatically increase cost.

---

### 2.1.4 Design for Welding

**A. Joint Accessibility**

Welder, torch, or robot torch must reach the joint with proper electrode/torch angle.

**B. Weld Symbol Conventions (AWS A2.4)**

Every welding drawing must carry a complete weld symbol — arrow line, reference line, weld symbol, dimensions, supplementary symbols, and finish symbol.

**C. Distortion Control**

Welding induces non-uniform heating → shrinkage stress → distortion.

$$
\delta_{distortion} \propto \frac{Q_{heat\,input}}{c \cdot \rho \cdot t^2}
$$

Mitigation: balanced welding sequences, back-step welding, preheating, clamping jigs.

**D. Recommended Edge Preparations**

| Joint Type | Plate Thickness $t$ (mm) | Edge Prep |
|---|---|---|
| Square butt | $t \leq 6$ | No bevel |
| Single-V bevel | $6 < t \leq 20$ | Bevel angle $30°$–$37.5°$, root gap 2–3 mm |
| Double-V bevel | $t > 20$ | Both sides bevelled, total angle $60°$ |

---

### 2.1.5 Design for Sheet Metal

**A. Minimum Bend Radius**

To avoid cracking on the outer (tension) surface:

$$
r_{min} \approx m \cdot t
$$

where $m$ is the material factor (mild steel: $m \approx 1$; aluminium: $m \approx 0.8$; stainless: $m \approx 1.5$).

**B. Hole-to-Edge and Hole-to-Hole Distances**

$$
d_{edge} \geq 1.5 \cdot d_{hole} \quad ; \quad d_{hole-to-hole} \geq d_{1} + d_{2} + 1.5 \cdot t
$$

**C. Bend Allowance (BA)**

$$
BA = \theta \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot (r + K \cdot t)
$$

where $K = 0.33$ for soft materials, $0.5$ for hard materials.

**D. Flat Pattern Length**

$$
L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA_{12} + L_3 + \ldots
$$

---

### 2.1.6 Design for Plastics (Injection Molding)

**A. Uniform Wall Thickness**

Most critical rule. Recommended variation ≤ **1.5 : 1**.

**B. Draft Angle**

$$
\theta_d \geq \frac{1°}{25\,mm\,depth} \quad \text{(minimum } 0.5°)
$$

**C. Rib Design**

$$
t_{rib} \leq 0.6 \cdot t_{wall} \quad ; \quad h_{rib} \leq 3 \cdot t_{wall}
$$

**D. Boss Design**

Boss outer diameter $\leq 3 \times$ hole diameter.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft angle (cast/forged) | $\theta_d = \tan^{-1}(\Delta L / H)$ | taper / height | $0.5°$ – $10°$ |
| 2 | Internal fillet radius | $r_i \geq 1.5\,t$ | wall thickness | mm |
| 3 | Web thickness (forging) | $t_{web} \geq 3\,t_{rib}$ | rib thickness | mm |
| 4 | Rib spacing (forging) | $s \geq 2\,t_{rib} + 3\,r_f$ | spacing, fillet | mm |
| 5 | Min. bend radius (sheet) | $r_{min} = m \cdot t$ | material factor, thickness | $0.8 \le m \le 1.5$ |
| 6 | Hole edge distance | $d_e \geq 1.5\,d_h$ | hole diameter | mm |
| 7 | Bend Allowance | $BA = \theta(\pi/180)(r + Kt)$ | angle in deg, K-factor | K = 0.33–0.5 |
| 8 | Plastic rib thickness | $t_{rib} \leq 0.6\,t_w$ | wall thickness | mm |
| 9 | Wall thickness ratio | $t_{max}/t_{min} \leq 1.5$ to $3$ | adjacent sections | dimensionless |
| 10 | Tool nose radius match | $r_{corner} \geq r_{nose}$ | machining | mm |
| 11 | Boss diameter limit | $D_{boss} \leq 3\,D_{hole}$ | boss/hole | mm |
| 12 | Bevel angle (welding) | $\alpha \approx 30°$–$37.5°$ | single-V | degrees |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry** — Following casting/forging recommendations saves millions in tooling cost and rejects.
- **Aerospace** — Welding distortion control is critical for airframe panels.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Plastic part design recommendations govern every smartphone case and laptop chassis.
- **Sheet-Metal Industry** — Bend allowance formulas are embedded in every CAD/CAM nesting software (e.g., SolidWorks Sheet Metal, AutoForm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Sheet-Metal Bend Allowance (Bend Allowance Formula)

The bend allowance is the **arc length of the neutral axis** within the bend region. The neutral axis lies inside the material at a fraction $K$ of the thickness $t$ from the inner surface.

**Step 1 — Locate the Neutral Axis**

$$
r_n = r + K \cdot t
$$

where $r$ is the **inner bend radius**, $t$ is sheet thickness, and $K$ is the **K-factor** ($0 < K < 1$, typically $0.33$ for soft, $0.5$ for hard material).

**Step 2 — Express the Bend Angle**

Convert the included bend angle $\theta$ (degrees) into radians:

$$
\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Arc Length (Bend Allowance)**

Arc length on a circle of radius $r_n$ subtended by $\theta_{rad}$:

$$
BA = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} = (r + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}
$$

This is the **flat-pattern length** consumed by the bend.

**Step 4 — Calculate Flat Pattern Length**

$$
L_{flat} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} L_i + \sum_{i=1}^{m} BA_i
$$

where $L_i$ are straight flange lengths and $BA_i$ are bend allowances for each of $m$ bends.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Bend Allowance)

**Problem:** A 2 mm thick mild-steel sheet is bent to an included angle of $90°$ with an inner bend radius of 4 mm. Determine the bend allowance. Take $K = 0.44$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute neutral-axis radius:

$$
r_n = r + K \cdot t = 4 + (0.44)(2) = 4 + 0.88 = 4.88 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2 — Convert angle to radians:

$$
\theta_{rad} = 90 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}
$$

Step 3 — Compute bend allowance:

$$
BA = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} = 4.88 \times 1.5708 = 7.6655 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 4 — Round-off (engineering practice):

$$
BA \approx 7.67 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When unfolding the bent part back into a flat blank, the bend region consumes **7.67 mm** of flat material — not the straight-line distance $2(r + t) = 12$ mm. This is why blank-layout calculations in industry always use **Bend Allowance**, never straight-line subtotals.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Recommendations

| Feature | Casting | Forging | Machining | Welding | Sheet Metal | Plastics |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Draft angle | 1°–3° | 5°–10° | N/A | N/A | N/A | 0.5°–1° |
| Min. fillet radius | $1.5\,t$ | $1.5\,t$ | = tool nose $r$ | $2t$ (toe) | $1\,t$ | $0.5\,t$ |
| Wall thickness ratio | 2:1 to 3:1 | uniform | uniform | full penetration | uniform | ≤ 1.5:1 |
| Undercuts | Avoid | Avoid | Avoid | Avoid | Avoid | Use side-actions |
| Typical tolerance | IT 12–14 | IT 12–13 | IT 6–11 | IT 13–15 | IT 11–13 | IT 12–14 |
| Surface finish $R_a$ | 6.3–25 μm | 3.2–12.5 μm | 0.2–6.3 μm | 6.3–25 μm | 0.8–6.3 μm | 0.4–3.2 μm |

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Plastic Rib Wall-Thickness Rule of Thumb

A rib is a *stiffener* behind a thin wall. If the rib base is too thick relative to the wall, a **sink mark** appears on the outer surface due to differential cooling and shrinkage.

**Energy argument:** The total volumetric shrinkage $V_{shrink}$ must be redistributed. For a visible sink of depth $\delta_{sink}$ over an area $A$:

$$
V_{shrink} = V_{rib\,excess} = (t_{rib,base} - t_{wall}) \cdot L_{rib} \cdot \delta_{sink}
$$

Empirically, to keep $\delta_{sink}$ below cosmetic threshold:

$$
t_{rib,base} \leq 0.6 \cdot t_{wall}
$$

This is the origin of the rule. For ribs meeting at junctions, an additional reduction (0.4–0.5) is used to avoid weld-line accumulation.

---

## 3.5 Decision Algorithm — "Which Process Fits?"

The following sequence of design decisions is used in *every* DFMA project:

1. **Identify functional requirements** (load, tolerance, material, volume).
2. **List candidate processes** that meet the material requirement.
3. **Apply process-specific design rules** to refine geometry.
4. **Iterate** until the design fits *all* chosen processes' envelopes.
5. **Validate** via prototype or simulation.

This is visualized in **Section 4**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The diagram below maps the logical flow of design-recommendation selection during a DFMA workflow.

## 4.2 Process-Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid

    B -->|Low -lt 1000| C[Sand Casting or Machining]

    D -->|Dynamic| G[Forged Steel Component]

    G --> I[Apply Forging Rules]

```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram — Recommended Features in a Cast Component

```mermaid

    A[Outer Wall] --> B[Uniform Thickness t]
    A --> C[Draft Angle theta]
    A --> D[Internal Fillet r int]

```

```mermaid
    A[Flat Blank] --> B[Inner Bend Radius r]
    B --> C[Neutral Axis at K times t]

```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Welding Joint Selection Logic

```mermaid
    C --> F[No Edge Prep]
    D --> G[Bevel 30 deg Root Gap 2-3 mm]
```
