```mermaid
    H --> I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Why is draft angle provided on castings? Mention typical values for sand casting and die casting."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Draft angle is the intentional taper provided on surfaces **perpendicular to the parting line** so that the casting can be withdrawn from the mould without damaging the cavity surfaces and without friction-induced surface defects.

- **Sand casting:** $1°$ to $3°$
- **Die casting:** $0.5°$ to $1.5°$
- **Investment casting:** $0°$ to $1°$

*Marking key:* (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sand casting range, 1 mark for die casting range.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"What is the K-factor in sheet-metal bending? State typical values for soft and hard materials."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The K-factor is the ratio of the distance of the **neutral axis** from the inner surface of the bend to the sheet thickness $t$. It depends on material, thickness, bend radius, and tooling.

$$
K = \frac{r_n - r}{t}, \quad 0 < K < 1
$$

- **Soft materials (annealed aluminium, mild steel):** $K \approx 0.33$
- **Hard materials (stainless steel, spring steel):** $K \approx 0.5$

*Marking key:* (1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark values.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **design recommendations for sand castings**. Cover draft angles, wall thickness uniformity, fillet radii, parting line, and core design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **2 mm thick mild-steel sheet** is bent to an **included angle of $60°$** with an **inner bend radius of 6 mm**. Taking $K = 0.44$, calculate the **bend allowance** and the **flat pattern length** if the part has two flanges of lengths 50 mm and 70 mm respectively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Draft angle** — 1° to 3° for sand casting; needed to ease removal from mould and avoid surface damage.
2. **Wall thickness uniformity** — variation between adjacent sections should not exceed 2:1 to 3:1; otherwise use chills, risers, or cored-out sections to promote uniform solidification and avoid shrinkage voids.
3. **Fillet radii** — internal fillets of radius $r_i \geq 1.5\,t$ to reduce stress concentration and avoid hot tears; external fillets to ease mould-making.
4. **Parting line** — should be a single plane; avoid stepped or curved parting lines to reduce mould cost.
5. **Cores** — minimize use; if unavoidable, support adequately to avoid sag; provide adequate draft on core prints.
6. **Machining allowance** — typically 1.5–3 mm on surfaces to be post-machined.
7. **Avoid undercuts** — or design them so that they can be moulded without side cores.

*Examiner's distribution:* 1 mark each for the first six, plus 1 mark for the undercut recommendation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $t = 2$ mm, $\theta = 60°$, $r = 6$ mm, $K = 0.44$, $L_1 = 50$ mm, $L_2 = 70$ mm.

**Step 1 — Neutral-axis radius:**

$$
r_n = r + K \cdot t = 6 + (0.44)(2) = 6.88 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Bend angle in radians:**

$$
\theta_{rad} = 60 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.0472 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Bend allowance:**

$$
BA = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} = 6.88 \times 1.0472 \approx 7.205 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Flat-pattern length:**

$$
L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 + BA = 50 + 70 + 7.205 = 127.205 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{flat} \approx 127.21 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Part (b):**
> 1. Many students wrongly substitute $r$ instead of $r_n = r + Kt$ into the formula. **Marks lost: 2 of 7.**
> 2. Some students forget to convert degrees to radians and use $\theta$ directly in the arc-length formula. **Marks lost: 1 of 7.**
> 3. Do not compute flat pattern by straight-line subtraction $2(r+t)\tan(\theta/2)$ unless the question explicitly asks for *outer* flange lengths. **Marks lost: 2 of 7.**

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **design recommendations for plastic injection-molded parts**. Discuss uniform wall thickness, draft, ribs, and bosses with proper sketches / diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **closed-die forged component** has a rib of thickness $t_{rib} = 8$ mm and an internal fillet radius of 12 mm. Determine (i) the **minimum web thickness**, (ii) the **minimum spacing between two adjacent ribs**, and (iii) the **recommended boss outer diameter** for a hole of diameter 14 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Uniform wall thickness** — recommended variation $\leq 1.5 : 1$. Thicker sections cause sink marks; thinner sections cause short shots.
2. **Draft angle** — minimum $0.5°$ to $1°$ per side; deeper cavities require more draft.
3. **Rib design** — rib base thickness $\leq 0.6 \times$ nominal wall; rib height $\leq 3 \times$ wall thickness; add fillets at rib root.
4. **Boss design** — boss outer diameter $\leq 3 \times$ hole diameter; provide draft; avoid sharp internal corners.
5. **Corner radii** — internal radius $\geq 0.5\,t$ to ease flow and reduce stress.
6. **Gate location** — symmetric gates prevent weld-line weakness.
7. **Surface finish and texture** — provide adequate draft on textured surfaces (add $1°$ per $0.025$ mm of texture depth).

*Examiner's distribution:* 1 mark for each key point (1–6), 1 mark for the texture note.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $t_{rib} = 8$ mm, $r_{fillet} = 12$ mm, $d_{hole} = 14$ mm.

**(i) Minimum web thickness:**

$$
t_{web} \geq 3 \cdot t_{rib} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Minimum rib spacing:**

$$
s \geq 2 \cdot t_{rib} + 3 \cdot r_{fillet} = 2(8) + 3(12) = 16 + 36 = 52 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Boss outer diameter:**

$$
D_{boss} \leq 3 \cdot d_{hole} = 3 \times 14 = 42 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Part (b):**
> 1. Web thickness formula $t_{web} \geq 3\,t_{rib}$ is often misquoted as $t_{web} \geq 2\,t_{rib}$. **Marks lost: 1 of 7.**
> 2. Boss diameter formula uses $\leq$, not $=$. **Marks lost: 1 of 7.**
> 3. Spacing rule involves *both* rib thickness AND fillet radius — students who ignore the $3\,r_f$ term lose **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design Recommendations (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Draft angle** is needed on surfaces perpendicular to the parting line (casting, forging, molding).
- [ ] **Wall thickness uniformity** — variation ratio 1.5:1 (plastics) to 3:1 (sand casting).
- [ ] **Internal fillet radii** — $r_i \geq 1.5\,t$ for castings and forgings; equals tool-nose radius for machined parts.
- [ ] **Rib thickness** — forging web $\geq 3\,t_{rib}$; plastic rib $\leq 0.6\,t_{wall}$.
- [ ] **Boss outer diameter** $\leq 3 \times$ hole diameter.
- [ ] **Hole edge distance** in sheet metal $\geq 1.5 \times$ hole diameter.
- [ ] **Bend allowance** uses neutral-axis radius $r_n = r + K\,t$ with $K = 0.33$ (soft) to $0.5$ (hard).
- [ ] **Flat pattern length** = sum of flanges + sum of bend allowances.
- [ ] **Bend angle in radians** mandatory before applying $BA = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad}$.
- [ ] **Welding edge prep** — single-V bevel $30°$–$37.5°$ for plates 6–20 mm thick.
- [ ] **Undercuts** — always avoid if possible; if unavoidable, plan for side cores or loose pieces.
- [ ] **Tolerance specification** — always specify the *widest tolerance the function allows*; tighter tolerances increase cost exponentially.
- [ ] **Always link a design rule back to its physical origin** — this is what examiners reward.

> **End of Module 3 — Design Recommendations Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recommended Tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recommended Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, **Recommended Tolerances** refer to the standardized, pre-calculated tolerance values prescribed by international standards (primarily **ISO 286** — *ISO System of Limits and Fits*) that a designer should preferentially allocate to mating and non-mating features of a product. These recommendations balance three competing requirements simultaneously:

1. **Functional performance** (the part must perform its intended role).
2. **Manufacturing economy** (the part must be producible at minimum cost).
3. **Assemblability** (parts must fit together predictably without re-work or rejection).

The recommended tolerance system is built upon three pillars:
- **Basic Size (Nominal Size, $D$ or $d$)**: The reference size from which limits are derived.
- **Tolerance Grade (IT — International Tolerance grade)**: Defines the **magnitude (width)** of the tolerance zone. Standard grades are **IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2 ... IT18**, with IT5 to IT13 being the most commonly used in general engineering.
- **Fundamental Deviation**: Defines the **position** of the tolerance zone relative to the basic size (upper deviation for shafts, lower deviation for holes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (verbatim from syllabus spirit):**
> *Recommended tolerances are the preferred tolerance values that designers should adopt based on the type of fit, the manufacturing process, and the functional requirement, thereby reducing the infinite design choices to a manageable, standardized set.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Cup Lid" Intuition

Imagine you are a chef designing a **screw-on lid for a coffee cup**:
- If the lid is **too tight (very small tolerance)**, the customer will struggle to open it, and the plastic moulding cost will be very high.
- If the lid is **too loose (very large tolerance)**, coffee will spill in the customer's bag.
- The **"recommended tolerance"** is the Goldilocks zone — the pre-decided standard that says: *for a snap-fit of this size, use a tolerance of ±0.1 mm, achieved through injection moulding.*

In engineering, this Goldilocks zone is pre-calculated by ISO so that you (the designer) do **not** have to re-invent it for every part. You simply **look up** the recommended tolerance from a table.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Recommended Tolerances Matter in DFMA**
> - They **reduce design cycle time** (no need to compute from scratch).
> - They **standardize** across suppliers worldwide.
> - They **minimize manufacturing cost** (process capability is already matched to the grade).
> - They **ensure interchangeability** (a hallmark of mass production).

## 1.3 Two Coordinate Systems of Tolerance

A recommended tolerance is fully described by **two numbers**:

$$ \text{Tolerance Specification} = \{ \text{Grade (IT)}, \ \text{Position (Letter/Symbol)} \} $$

For example, a **50H7/g6** fit says: Hole is H7, Shaft is g6 — the system itself **automatically recommends the magnitude and position** of the tolerance zones for a basic size of 50 mm.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO 286 System — Three Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 Basic Size ($D$ for hole, $d$ for shaft)
The size from which dimensions are derived, expressed in millimetres. All tolerance zones are calculated using formulas indexed by basic size ranges called **steps** (e.g., 0–3, 3–6, 6–10, 10–18, 18–30, 30–50, 50–80, 80–120 mm ...).

### 2.1.2 Tolerance Grade (IT Grade) — Controls the WIDTH
The International Tolerance grade defines the **size of the tolerance zone**. The numerical value of one IT unit (called $i$ or $I$) is calculated as:

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(for sizes up to 500 mm)} $$

$$ I = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(same formula; conventionally } I \text{ is used for holes)} $$

Then, each IT grade is a multiple of $i$ (or $I$):

$$ \text{IT5} = 7i, \quad \text{IT6} = 10i, \quad \text{IT7} = 16i, \quad \text{IT8} = 25i, \quad \text{IT9} = 40i $$
$$ \text{IT10} = 64i, \quad \text{IT11} = 100i, \quad \text{IT12} = 160i, \quad \text{IT13} = 250i $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a cube-root formula?** Tolerance is proportional to manufacturing error, which is empirically known to grow with the **cube root of size** (this comes from error-propagation theory and surface roughness scaling).

### 2.1.3 Fundamental Deviation — Controls the POSITION
The fundamental deviation is the **deviation (upper for shafts a–h, lower for shafts j–zc; lower for holes A–H, upper for holes J–ZC)** closest to the basic size. It is given by empirical formulas in ISO 286. For example:

- **Shaft 'g' (shaft basis, clearance)**: Lower deviation $ei = -2.5 \cdot D^{0.34}$ (for $D \leq 10$ mm), with corrections for larger sizes.
- **Hole 'H'**: Lower deviation $EI = 0$ (the hole's lower limit is exactly the basic size).
- **Shaft 'h'**: Upper deviation $es = 0$ (the shaft's upper limit is exactly the basic size).

## 2.2 The Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis Recommendation

There are two standardized ways of expressing the same fit:

| System | Description | When Recommended (KTU 2024 view) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hole-Basis (H/g, H/h, H/k, H7/p6 etc.)** | The hole is always H-grade; shaft varies | **PREFERRED** for general engineering. Tools like drills, reamers, broaches are made to a standard size. Reduces tooling cost. |
| **Shaft-Basis (G/h, F/h, N7/h6 etc.)** | The shaft is always h-grade; hole varies | Used when a long shaft mates with several parts of varying fits, or when a standard bar stock is used. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** *In 90% of KTU exam questions and real engineering, the **Hole-Basis** system is assumed unless stated otherwise. Always default to H-grade holes.*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Use / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tolerance Unit (mm) | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | For $D \le 500$ mm |
| 2 | IT Grade Value | $\text{IT}n = K \cdot i$ where $K$ is grade coefficient | $K=7,10,16,25,40,64,100,160,250$ for IT5–IT13 |
| 3 | Shaft 'h' Upper Deviation | $es = 0$ | Shaft-basis reference |
| 4 | Hole 'H' Lower Deviation | $EI = 0$ | Hole-basis reference |
| 5 | Shaft Lower Deviation (h) | $ei = es - \text{IT}n$ | Since $es=0$, $ei = -\text{IT}n$ |
| 6 | Hole Upper Deviation (H) | $ES = EI + \text{IT}n$ | Since $EI=0$, $ES = +\text{IT}n$ |
| 7 | Maximum Clearance (Clearance Fit) | $C_{max} = ES - ei$ | Largest gap in assembly |
| 8 | Minimum Clearance | $C_{min} = EI - es$ | Smallest gap in assembly |
| 9 | Maximum Interference (Interference Fit) | $I_{max} = EI - es$ (negative) | Forcing in parts |
| 10 | Tolerance Stack-up (worst case) | $T_{stack} = \sum T_i$ | Linear summation of tolerances |
| 11 | Statistical Stack-up (RSS) | $T_{stack} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ | $\pm 3\sigma$ method, more realistic |
| 12 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $C_p \ge 1.33$ recommended |
| 13 | Manufacturing Cost vs IT grade | Cost $\approx k \cdot \text{IT}^{-1.5}$ (empirical) | Tighter tolerance $\Rightarrow$ exponentially higher cost |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing tolerance values inside any markdown table, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe `\vert` symbol, otherwise the markdown table will break.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The ISO recommendation system is used in:
- **CNC machining**: IT7–IT9 typically.
- **Sheet metal**: IT12–IT14.
- **Injection moulding**: IT10–IT13 (due to mould wear).
- **Die casting**: IT11–IT14.
- **Grinding**: IT5–IT6 (precision shafts).

In DFMA, the designer's job is to **select the loosest IT grade** that still meets the functional requirement, because every step tighter in IT grade can **multiply the manufacturing cost by 2–5×**.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tolerance Unit as a Function of Size

**Statement:** Prove that the tolerance unit for a basic size of $D = 50$ mm equals approximately $1.56$ microns (or $0.00156$ mm) using the ISO formula.

**Step 1 — State the governing formula**

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(in micrometres, when } D \text{ is in mm)} $$

> In SI, $i$ comes out in micrometres ($\mu$m).

**Step 2 — Substitute $D = 50$ mm**

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cube root**

$$ \sqrt[3]{50} \approx 3.6840 $$

**Step 4 — Compute each term**

$$ 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 = 1.6578 $$

$$ 0.001 \cdot 50 = 0.0500 $$

**Step 5 — Sum**

$$ i = 1.6578 + 0.0500 = 1.7078 \ \mu\text{m} $$

**Step 6 — Compare with standard table value**

The ISO table lists $i = 1.56$ $\mu$m for the 30–50 mm step and $i = 1.86$ $\mu$m for the 50–80 mm step. The formula gives a value used as a **smooth interpolation**; tables give **step-discrete** values. Both are accepted in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Limits and Fits for a 50 H7/g6 Specification

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**. Let's solve it fully.

**Given:** Fit = **50 H7/g6** (hole-basis, clearance fit)

**Step 1 — Find IT grade tolerance magnitudes**

For $D = 50$ mm, ISO 286 table gives:

$$ \text{IT7} = 25 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.025 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ \text{IT6} = 16 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.016 \ \text{mm} $$

**Step 2 — Define deviations for Hole H7**

Since the hole is **H** grade, the **lower deviation is zero**:

$$ EI = 0 $$

The **upper deviation** is the IT7 value:

$$ ES = EI + \text{IT7} = 0 + 0.025 = +0.025 \ \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Hole limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= D + ES = 50 + 0.025 = 50.025 \ \text{mm} \\
H_{min} &= D + EI = 50 + 0 = 50.000 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define deviations for Shaft g6**

For shaft **g**, the **upper deviation $es$ is negative** (it lies below the basic size). From ISO 286 tables for the 40–50 mm step:

$$ es = -0.012 \ \text{mm} $$

The **lower deviation** is:

$$ ei = es - \text{IT6} = -0.012 - 0.016 = -0.028 \ \text{mm} $$

**Step 5 — Shaft limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= d + es = 50 - 0.012 = 49.988 \ \text{mm} \\
S_{min} &= d + ei = 50 - 0.028 = 49.972 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Clearance Range**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 50.025 - 49.972 = 0.053 \ \text{mm} \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 50.000 - 49.988 = 0.012 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify Fit Type**

Since $C_{min} = +0.012 > 0$ and $C_{max} = +0.053 > 0$, this is a **clearance fit** — the hole is always bigger than the shaft. It corresponds to **Loose Running Fit**, suitable for parts that must slide freely (e.g., pulley hubs on shafts, gearboxes with frequent dismantling).

> [!NOTE]
> The complete specification **50 H7/g6** is a **recommended tolerance** in the strict DFMA sense — the designer did not invent any value; every number came from ISO 286.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Recommended Tolerance for Common Processes

| Manufacturing Process | Achievable IT Grade | Typical Recommended IT | Relative Cost Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting | IT14 – IT16 | IT14 | 1× (baseline) |
| Die Casting | IT11 – IT13 | IT12 | 1.5× |
| Investment Casting | IT10 – IT13 | IT11 | 2× |
| Cold Rolling / Drawing | IT9 – IT11 | IT10 | 2.5× |
| Powder Metallurgy | IT9 – IT11 | IT10 | 2.5× |
| Stamping / Blanking | IT10 – IT13 | IT11 | 2× |
| Injection Moulding | IT10 – IT13 | IT11 | 2× |
| Conventional Turning | IT10 – IT12 | IT11 | 2× |
| CNC Milling / Turning | IT7 – IT10 | IT9 | 5× |
| Broaching | IT7 – IT9 | IT8 | 7× |
| Cylindrical Grinding | IT5 – IT7 | IT6 | 12× |
| Honing / Lapping | IT3 – IT6 | IT5 | 25× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFMA Rule:** *Always select the recommended IT grade that matches your chosen manufacturing process, not the tightest grade you can imagine. The cost penalty for overspecifying tolerance is exponential.*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Fit Class

| Fit Type (Hole-Basis) | Application Example | Recommended Class |
|---|---|---|
| **H11/c11** | Loose, large clearances, agricultural machinery | Easy sliding |
| **H9/d9** | Loose running, chain drives | Free running |
| **H8/f7** | General running fit, bearings | Normal running |
| **H7/g6** | Easy sliding, gear hubs | Sliding fit |
| **H7/h6** | Precision location, jigs | Locational clearance |
| **H7/k6** | Requires accurate location, can be dismantled | Locational transition |
| **H7/n6** | Permanent location with disassembly possible | Locational interference |
| **H7/p6** | Press fit, semi-permanent assembly | Light press fit |
| **H7/s6** | Permanent assembly, requires force | Medium press fit |
| **H7/u7** | Very permanent, requires heating/cooling | Heavy press fit |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO Fit System Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Recommended Tolerance Selection] --> B{Is the fit a mating or non-mating feature?}
    B -- Mating --> C{Mating Type?}
    B -- Non-Mating --> D[Use IT12 to IT14 Loose Tolerance]

    C -- Rotating / Sliding --> E[Clearance Fit Class]
    C -- Accurate Location --> F[Transition Fit Class]
    C -- Permanent Join --> G[Interference Fit Class]

    E --> E1[H11/c11 to H7/g6]
    F --> F1[H7/h6 to H7/k6 to H7/n6]
    G --> G1[H7/p6 to H7/s6 to H7/u7]

    E1 --> H[Consult ISO 286 Tables]
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H

    H --> I{Process Available?}
    I -- Sand Casting --> J1[Apply IT14]
    I -- CNC Machining --> J2[Apply IT9]
    I -- Grinding --> J3[Apply IT6]
    I -- Injection Moulding --> J4[Apply IT11]

    J1 --> K[Write Final Spec e.g. 50 H11/c11]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
```

## 4.2 Tolerance Zone Position Diagram (Hole-Basis)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SHAFT[SHAFT g6]
        Smin["Smin = 49.972"]
        Smax["Smax = 49.988"]
    end
    subgraph HOLE[HOLE H7]
        Hmin["Hmin = 50.000"]
        Hmax["Hmax = 50.025"]
    end
    BSIZE[Basic Size 50.000 mm]
    SHAFT --> HOLE
    BSIZE --- SHAFT
    BSIZE --- HOLE
```

## 4.3 DFMA Tolerance Decision Flowchart (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[New Component Design] --> B[Identify Functional Requirements]
    B --> C[Identify Mating Features]
    C --> D{Must Move Relative to Mating Part?}
    D -- YES --> E[Choose Clearance Fit]
    D -- NO --> E2{Must be Dismantled Often?}
    E2 -- YES --> F[Choose Transition Fit]
    E2 -- NO --> G[Choose Interference Fit]

    E --> H[Select IT Grade from Process Capability]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I[Look up ISO 286 Tables]
    I --> J[Verify Cpk greater than 1.33]
    J --> K[Finalize Recommended Tolerance Spec]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "Recommended Tolerance" as used in Design for Manufacturing and Assembly. Why are standardized tolerances preferred over designer-chosen tolerances?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Recommended Tolerance** is a tolerance value chosen from a standard table (such as ISO 286) that simultaneously satisfies the **functional requirement**, the **manufacturing process capability**, and the **assemblability** of a component. It is "recommended" because it has been pre-validated by international engineering bodies as the optimal compromise.

Standardized tolerances are preferred because:
1. **Interchangeability** — parts from different suppliers fit together.
2. **Reduced design time** — no need to derive tolerance from first principles.
3. **Cost optimization** — IT grades are tied to empirical process capability.
4. **Global communication** — a single symbol (e.g., 50 H7/g6) is understood worldwide. **(1 mark per point, total 3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**State the ISO formula for the tolerance unit $i$ and explain why it is a cube-root function of size rather than linear.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\mu\text{m, for } D \le 500 \text{ mm}) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$

The cube-root dependence arises from **error-propagation theory**: dimensional errors in manufacturing scale with the **cube root of the linear size**, not the size itself, because errors are proportional to the boundary of a feature, while size scales with the volume/cross-section. Empirical observations across thousands of measurements confirmed this relationship. Additionally, a cube-root function gives **bounded, realistic growth** of tolerance with size (linear would over-tolerate large parts, square-root would under-tolerate them). **(2 marks for the explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme Part B pattern, students answer **one of two choices**. Both choices are presented below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **ISO system of limits and fits**, clearly distinguishing between **basic size, deviations, tolerance, fit, and clearance**. **  (7 marks)**

**(b)** A hole and shaft assembly is designated as **40 H8/f7**. Determine: **(i)** the tolerance values for the hole and shaft, **(ii)** the maximum and minimum clearance, and **(iii)** classify the type of fit. State **two real-world applications** for this class of fit. **  (7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **ISO system of limits and fits (ISO 286)** is the international standard for specifying tolerances on mating parts. Its key elements are:

1. **Basic Size ($D$ or $d$)**: The reference size in millimetres, common to both hole and shaft. **(1 mark)**
2. **Actual Size**: The measured size of a manufactured part, which always lies within the limits.
3. **Deviations**: The algebraic difference between actual size and basic size. **Upper deviation** is for the largest size, **lower deviation** is for the smallest size. **(1 mark)**
4. **Tolerance**: The difference between maximum and minimum sizes (i.e., $ES - EI$ for a hole, $es - ei$ for a shaft). The width of the tolerance zone is determined by the **IT grade**. **(1 mark)**
5. **Fit**: The degree of tightness/looseness between mating parts. Three classes exist:
   - **Clearance fit** (always gap, e.g., H/g).
   - **Transition fit** (either gap or interference, e.g., H/k).
   - **Interference fit** (always interference, e.g., H/s). **(2 marks)**
6. **Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis**: In the hole-basis, the lower deviation of the hole is zero (H); in the shaft-basis, the upper deviation of the shaft is zero (h). Hole-basis is **recommended** as it reduces tooling cost. **(1 mark)**
7. **Sketch description** (verbal, since ASCII is impractical for a multi-line tolerance diagram): *The diagram should show the basic size as a vertical reference line, with a hole tolerance zone on the right (e.g., H7) straddling or above the line, and a shaft tolerance zone on the left (e.g., g6) below the line, with $C_{max}$ and $C_{min}$ marked as the gaps between the zones.* **(1 mark for diagram)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Fit = **40 H8/f7**

**Step 1 — IT Grade Values (from ISO 286 for $D = 40$ mm, step 30–50 mm)**

$$ \text{IT8} = 39 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.039 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ \text{IT7} = 25 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.025 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Hole H8 Deviations**

$$ EI = 0 \quad (\text{Hole H convention}) $$

$$ ES = EI + \text{IT8} = 0 + 0.039 = +0.039 \ \text{mm} $$

**Hole limits:**

$$ H_{max} = 40.039 \ \text{mm}, \quad H_{min} = 40.000 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Shaft f7 Deviations** (from ISO 286 table for 30–50 mm step)

$$ es = -0.025 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ ei = es - \text{IT7} = -0.025 - 0.025 = -0.050 \ \text{mm} $$

**Shaft limits:**

$$ S_{max} = 39.975 \ \text{mm}, \quad S_{min} = 39.950 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Clearances**

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min} = 40.039 - 39.950 = +0.089 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max} = 40.000 - 39.975 = +0.025 \ \text{mm} $$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Fit Classification**

Since both clearances are **positive** ($C_{min} > 0$), this is a **clearance fit**. Specifically, **H8/f7** corresponds to a **Normal Sliding/Loose Running Fit** suitable for high-speed rotating parts.

**Two real-world applications:** **(1 mark)**
1. **Bearing housings** where the inner ring must slide onto a shaft with minimal force.
2. **Centrifugal pump impellers** on shafts, where frequent dismantling is needed for maintenance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks on this type of question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\mu$m to mm** (e.g., writing 39 instead of 0.039) — loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Using wrong sign for $es$ of f-shaft** — 'f' is a negative deviation; many students write it positive.
> 3. **Not distinguishing clearance vs interference** explicitly — must state the type and the sign.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** in part (a) — KTU awards 1 full mark for a clear tolerance zone diagram.
> 5. **Wrong IT table value** — the IT grade changes with size range; for $D = 40$ mm, use the **30–50 mm** step, NOT the 18–30 mm step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the philosophy of **Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis** systems. State **three reasons** why the **Hole-Basis system is recommended** in the majority of engineering design situations. **  (7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC-turned shaft must mate with a bearing bore. The fit specified is **30 H7/k6**. Calculate: **(i)** the tolerance for the hole and shaft separately, **(ii)** the maximum clearance and maximum interference, and **(iii)** state whether this is a clearance, transition, or interference fit. Justify the choice of fit for a **bearing application**. **  (7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

In the **Hole-Basis system**, the hole is always the H-grade (lower deviation = 0), and the shaft tolerance zone is shifted to achieve the desired fit. In the **Shaft-Basis system**, the shaft is always h-grade (upper deviation = 0), and the hole is varied. **(2 marks)**

**Three reasons why Hole-Basis is recommended:**

1. **Tooling economy**: Standard cutting tools (drills, reamers, broaches) are manufactured in fixed sizes; producing a fixed-size hole is cheaper and easier than producing a fixed-size shaft with a high precision across the entire length. **(2 marks)**
2. **Manufacturing convenience**: Holes are usually produced by tooling (drilling, boring, broaching) which is naturally standardized, while shafts are typically turned from bar stock of standard size. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Historical and global standardization**: ISO 286, ANSI, and DIN all default to hole-basis; using shaft-basis requires a special justification. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Fit = **30 H7/k6**, $D = 30$ mm (step 18–30 mm)

**Step 1 — IT Grade Values**

$$ \text{IT7} = 21 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.021 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ \text{IT6} = 13 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.013 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Hole H7**

$$ EI = 0, \quad ES = 0 + 0.021 = +0.021 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ H_{max} = 30.021, \quad H_{min} = 30.000 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Shaft k6**

For shaft 'k' in 18–30 mm step, $es = +0.002$ mm.

$$ ei = es - \text{IT6} = +0.002 - 0.013 = -0.011 \ \text{mm} $$

$$ S_{max} = 30.002, \quad S_{min} = 29.989 \ \text{mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Clearance / Interference**

$$ C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min} = 30.021 - 29.989 = +0.032 \ \text{mm} \quad \text{(Max clearance)} $$

$$ I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min} = 30.002 - 30.000 = +0.002 \ \text{mm} \quad \text{(Max interference)} $$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Fit Classification**

Since the result can be **either clearance or interference** depending on the actual manufactured sizes, this is a **Transition Fit**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Justification for Bearing**

Bearings require **accurate location** (no relative movement between inner ring and shaft under load) but must still be **dismountable for replacement** (bearings are wear items). The H7/k6 transition fit gives a slight interference (positive location) when parts are at the tight end, and a small clearance when at the loose end — perfect for **press-fitting a bearing onto a shaft** using a mounting tool or hydraulic press. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Alternative Choice B — Common Errors to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing 'k' with 'h'**: 'k' has a *positive* upper deviation, not zero.
> 2. **Misclassifying the fit**: Students often call a transition fit a clearance fit if they see a positive $C_{max}$ and ignore the $I_{max}$.
> 3. **Justification must mention BOTH location AND dismountability** — a common answer missing one of these loses 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Recommended Tolerances**

- **Recommended tolerance** = a pre-validated tolerance from ISO 286 that meets function, process, and assembly needs simultaneously.
- The **ISO system** rests on **basic size + IT grade + fundamental deviation** — three pieces of information are sufficient.
- **IT grade** controls **width** of the tolerance zone; **letter (H/h, etc.)** controls **position**.
- Standard **IT grades in general use**: IT6 (grinding), IT7 (locational/precision fits), IT8–IT9 (general CNC), IT10–IT11 (castings, moulding), IT12–IT14 (rough castings, sheet metal).
- **Tolerance unit formula**:
$$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\mu\text{m}) $$
- **Hole 'H'** always has $EI = 0$; **Shaft 'h'** always has $es = 0$.
- **Hole-Basis is recommended by default** (90% of all fits) for tooling economy and global standardization.
- For a fit **X Hn / y m**:
  - Hole limits: $H_{min} = D$, $H_{max} = D + \text{IT}n$.
  - Shaft limits: depends on letter.
  - Clearance: $C = H - S$; **positive = clearance, negative = interference, mixed = transition**.
- **Cost grows exponentially** as IT grade tightens; **always choose the loosest grade that meets function**.
- **Process capability rule**: $C_p = \dfrac{\text{Tolerance}}{6\sigma} \ge 1.33$ for a capable process.
- **Fit categories** (hole-basis examples):
  - Clearance: H/c, H/d, H/e, H/f, H/g, H/h.
  - Transition: H/js, H/k, H/m, H/n.
  - Interference: H/p, H/r, H/s, H/t, H/u, H/x, H/z.
- **Common exam short-cuts**:
  - For 50 mm: IT7 = 25 $\mu$m, IT6 = 16 $\mu$m, IT8 = 39 $\mu$m, IT9 = 62 $\mu$m.
  - For 30 mm: IT7 = 21 $\mu$m, IT6 = 13 $\mu$m, IT8 = 33 $\mu$m.
  - For 25 mm: IT7 = 21 $\mu$m, IT6 = 13 $\mu$m.
- **Mnemonic for fit class**: *"Cats, Dogs, Eat, Furry, Grey, Hairy, Just, Keep, Mating, Nightly, Properly, Running, Strong, Together"* — corresponds to the alphabetical progression from C (loose clearance) → ZC (heavy interference).
- **DFMA mantra**: *"Specify the loosest recommended tolerance that satisfies the function; your design is only as good as your supplier's process capability."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parts Produced by Milling - Process Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parts Produced by Milling - Process Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Milling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which a **rotating multi-point cutting tool** (having one or more teeth) progressively removes material from a stationary or slowly moving workpiece to produce a desired geometry, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy. The cutting velocity is generated primarily by the **rotation of the cutter**, while the feed motion is provided either by the movement of the cutter or the workpiece depending on the machine configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **DFMA Perspective (KTU PEPET755 Context):**
> In the context of *Design for Manufacturing and Assembly*, milling is categorized as a **secondary/subtractive process** that generates complex prismatic features (slots, pockets, contours, holes, profiles) on parts. The DFMA goal is to redesign parts so that milling operations are **minimized, simplified, or combined**, thereby reducing setup time, fixturing cost, and part-to-part variation.

Mathematically, the basic process kinematics are defined by three independent motions:

$$\text{Cutting Speed} \quad V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]$$

$$\text{Feed Rate} \quad f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

$$\text{Material Removal Rate} \quad \text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

where **D** is the cutter diameter (mm), **N** is spindle speed (rev/min), **f_z** is feed per tooth (mm/tooth), **z** is number of effective teeth, **w** is width of cut (mm), and **d** is depth of cut (mm).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a hand carpenter's **wood plane** gliding across a wooden plank to shave off thin layers. Now, instead of pushing the blade, imagine the blade itself **spinning rapidly** like a fan, and the plank is fed into the spinning blades in small, controlled increments.

- The **rotating spinning blades** = the milling cutter with its multiple teeth.
- The **wood plank being fed** = the workpiece clamped on the machine table.
- Each **shaving action per blade passage** = one tooth engagement, removing a small chip.

The beauty of milling is that **many teeth share the cutting load**, which:
1. Distributes heat over time,
2. Allows higher productivity than single-point turning, and
3. Permits creation of complex 2D and 3D contours via simultaneous multi-axis movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Milling Matters in DFMA:**
> Up to **40–60% of machined features** in a typical mechanical assembly (housings, brackets, flanges, manifolds) are produced by milling. Designers must therefore understand milling's geometric capabilities, tolerances (~±0.025 mm achievable), surface finish (Ra 0.8–3.2 µm), and cost drivers so that part designs exploit the process efficiently.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

| Metric | Typical Range | Industrial Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Tolerance (CNC Milling)** | ±0.025 mm to ±0.125 mm | Precision grade achievable on 3-axis VMC |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | 0.4 µm to 3.2 µm | Depends on feed per tooth & cutter geometry |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | 100 to 40,000 RPM | High-speed machining (HSM) extends upper limit |
| **Material Removal Rate** | 1 to 500 cm³/min | Function of MRR = w × d × f_r |
| **Specific Cutting Energy (K_s)** | 0.4 to 4.5 J/mm³ | Steels: ~2.0; Al alloys: ~0.8 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Milling Operations

Milling is broadly classified by the **orientation of the cutter axis** relative to the workpiece surface and by the **geometry of the feature** being produced.

### 2.1.1 Based on Cutter–Workpiece Geometry

1. **Peripheral (Slab) Milling** — Cutter axis is parallel to the workpiece surface. The cylindrical periphery of the cutter does the cutting. Used for producing flat surfaces, deep slots, and contours.
2. **Face Milling** — Cutter axis is perpendicular to the workpiece surface. The flat face (bottom) of the cutter removes material. Used for producing large flat areas with high productivity.
3. **End Milling** — Versatile operation using end-mill cutters. Can produce slots, pockets, profiles, contours, and holes. Most common DFMA feature-generation process.
4. **Gang Milling** — Multiple cutters mounted on a common arbor to perform several operations simultaneously (e.g., slot + side finishing in one pass).
5. **Straddle Milling** — Two or more side-milling cutters spaced apart to produce parallel flat surfaces on both sides of the workpiece simultaneously.
6. **Form Milling** — Cutters shaped to the inverse profile of the desired feature. Used for complex contours (e.g., gear teeth, splines, T-slots).
7. **Profile Milling** — Reproducing an outline (2D or 3D) by following a programmed tool path on a CNC machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these distinctions matter for the designer:**
> Each operation dictates a **specific feature geometry**, **preferred tolerance stack-up**, and **fixturing strategy**. A DFMA-conscious designer will pick features that align naturally with one of these standard operations to avoid costly custom tooling.

## 2.2 Up Milling vs Down Milling — Critical Design Consideration

The **direction of feed relative to cutter rotation** defines two fundamental engagement modes:

- **Conventional (Up) Milling**: The cutter rotates against the feed direction. The chip thickness starts at zero and increases. The cutter tends to *lift* the workpiece.
- **Climb (Down) Milling**: The cutter rotates in the same direction as the feed. The chip thickness starts at a maximum and decreases. The cutter tends to *pull* the workpiece into the cut.

| Parameter | Up Milling (Conventional) | Down Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| Chip thickness | Starts at 0, increases | Starts at max, decreases |
| Cutter force on workpiece | Tends to lift | Tends to pull down |
| Tool life | Lower (rubbing at start) | Higher |
| Surface finish | Poorer | Better |
| Fixturing requirement | Must resist lifting force | Must resist pulling force |
| Use case | Roughing, irregular surfaces | Finishing, CNC machines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> Modern CNC milling almost universally employs **climb milling** because backlash in the lead-screw is eliminated (cutting force pushes the table against the backlash-preload direction). Always specify climb milling in DFMA design notes unless the workpiece has a hardened skin or scale that would damage the cutter.

## 2.3 Types of Milling Machines (Designer-Relevant)

| Machine Type | Axes | Typical Use | DFMA Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Knee-and-column (horizontal/vertical) | 3 manual | Job shop, low-volume | High setup time |
| Bed-type mill | 3 manual | Heavy, rigid parts | Limited part variety |
| Plano mill / Gantry | 3–5 | Very large parts | Aerospace, tooling |
| Vertical Machining Center (VMC) | 3 + ATC | General prismatic parts | Most common modern platform |
| Horizontal Machining Center (HMC) | 4 + APC | High-volume cube parts | Pallet changer boosts uptime |
| 5-axis Machining Center | 5 simultaneous | Complex contours, impellers | Highest feature integration |

**ATC** = Automatic Tool Changer; **APC** = Automatic Pallet Changer.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of all KTU numerical problems in this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D}$ | rev/min | Inverse of (1) |
| 3 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | mm/tooth | Look up from tool/material chart |
| 4 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | Key driver of MRR |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | $w$ = width, $d$ = depth |
| 6 | Specific Cutting Energy | $K_s$ | N·m/mm³ (J/mm³) | Material-dependent |
| 7 | Power at Cutter (gross) | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | SI: convert MRR (mm³/min) |
| 8 | Spindle Motor Power | $P_m = \dfrac{P_c}{\eta}$ | kW | $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (0.75–0.90) |
| 9 | Cutting Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | $L$ = length of cut |
| 10 | Volumetric Metal Removal | $V = w \cdot d \cdot L$ | mm³ | Used in $T_m$ simplification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Validity:**
> - Formula (1)–(2) assume **sharp tools** and **steady-state engagement**. At very low speeds, built-up edge (BUE) invalidates the equation empirically.
> - Formula (5) is exact only for **rectangular cuts**. For circular/curved cuts (e.g., circular interpolation), an effective engagement angle correction factor $C_e$ must be applied: $\text{MRR}_{eff} = \text{MRR} \cdot C_e$ where $C_e \le 1$.
> - Formula (7) requires $K_s$ corresponding to the **undeformed chip thickness** $h_1 = f_z \sin(\kappa)$ where $\kappa$ is the entering angle. In KTU problems, $K_s$ is generally given directly.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Milling Equations

1. **Process Planning**: Spindle speed (Eq. 2) sets the CNC program parameter; feed rate (Eq. 4) sets productivity.
2. **Cost Estimation**: $\text{Cost} = (T_m \cdot \text{Machine Rate}) + (\text{Tool Cost})$. Accurate $T_m$ (Eq. 9) drives cost models.
3. **Machine Selection**: $P_m$ (Eq. 8) determines whether a given CNC has sufficient spindle power.
4. **Design for Manufacturability**: Setting maximum $w$, $d$ via Eq. (5)–(7) ensures the chosen machine can handle the feature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material Removal Rate (MRR) Equation

The MRR for a **straight peripheral milling cut** is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the instantaneous chip geometry.**
As the cutter of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ rpm, each tooth engages the workpiece over a sweep angle. The unrolled chip has:
- **Width** = axial depth of cut, $d$ (mm)
- **Length** = width of cut, $w$ (mm)

**Step 2 — Determine feed per revolution.**
With $f_z$ mm/tooth and $z$ teeth cutting simultaneously, the table advances by:
$$f = f_z \cdot z \quad [\text{mm/rev}]$$

**Step 3 — Compute volume swept per revolution.**
Each revolution, the cutter sweeps a slab of width $w$, height $d$, and thickness equal to the table feed per revolution:
$$V_{\text{rev}} = w \cdot d \cdot f \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{rev}]$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by revolutions per minute.**
Since the spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute:
$$\text{MRR} = V_{\text{rev}} \cdot N = w \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N = w \cdot d \cdot (f_z \cdot z) \cdot N$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ is the table feed rate.**
Substituting yields the canonical equation:
$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on sign convention:** MRR is always a **positive** scalar quantity representing the volume removed per unit time, never a vector. KTU examiners expect a positive numerical answer.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Spindle Power Equation

**Step 1 — Energy per unit volume removed.**
Define the specific cutting energy $K_s$ as the energy required to remove a unit volume of material:
$$K_s = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} \quad [\text{N·m/mm}^3]$$

**Step 2 — Total power at cutter teeth.**
Power is energy per unit time:
$$P_c = \text{Energy Rate} = K_s \cdot (\text{Volume Rate}) = K_s \cdot \text{MRR}$$

**Step 3 — Unit consistency.**
MRR is in $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$; convert to $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ by dividing by 60. Power in Watts (J/s) requires:
$$P_c \, [\text{W}] = \frac{K_s \, [\text{N·m/mm}^3] \cdot \text{MRR} \, [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilowatts.**
$$\boxed{P_c \, [\text{kW}] = \frac{K_s \cdot \text{MRR}}{60 \times 10^6}}$$

(The $10^6$ factor converts $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ to a compatible SI base; for millimetre units we use $K_s$ in standard SI notation and treat MRR as $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$.)

**Step 5 — Account for mechanical losses.**
The motor must supply higher power:
$$P_m = \frac{P_c}{\eta} \quad \text{where} \quad 0.75 \le \eta \le 0.90$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A face mill of diameter $D = 100$ mm with $z = 8$ teeth is used on mild steel ($K_s = 2.0$ N·m/mm³). Cutting speed $V_c = 200$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth, axial depth of cut $d = 4$ mm, width of cut $w = 80$ mm. Machine efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Compute spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, cutting power, and motor power.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1: Spindle speed**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \cdot 200}{\pi \cdot 100} = \frac{200000}{314.159} \approx 636.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Feed rate**
$$f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.15 \cdot 8 \cdot 636.6 = 763.96 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3: Material removal rate**
$$\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r = 80 \cdot 4 \cdot 763.96 = 244467.2 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4: Cutting power**
$$P_c = \frac{K_s \cdot \text{MRR}}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.0 \cdot 244467.2}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{488934.4}{60 \times 10^6} \approx 8.15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Motor power**
$$P_m = \frac{P_c}{\eta} = \frac{8.15}{0.80} \approx 10.19 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | 636.6 | rpm |
| Feed Rate | $f_r$ | 763.96 | mm/min |
| Material Removal Rate | MRR | 244467.2 | mm³/min |
| Cutting Power | $P_c$ | 8.15 | kW |
| Motor Power | $P_m$ | 10.19 | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validity Check:** A safe working rule is that **motor power should not exceed 80% of the machine's rated continuous spindle power**. If $P_m > 0.8 \cdot P_{rated}$, the cutting parameters (lower $f_z$, smaller $d$, or $w$) must be revised — this is itself a DFMA design constraint on the part!

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications of Parts Produced on Milling Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
