
### 4.4 The DFMA Feedback Loop (Module 4 Anchor Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Concept Design] --> S2[DFMA Analysis]
    S2 --> S3{Need Part\nReduction?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Consolidate\nParts]
    S3 -->|No| S5[Ease of\nAssembly Review]
    S4 --> S6[Revised\nDesign]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Prototype\nBuild]
    S7 --> S8[Assembly\nTime Test]
    S8 --> S9{Cost\nTarget Met?}
    S9 -->|No| S2
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Production\nRelease]

    style S2 fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:3px
    style S9 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style S10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Assembly"** as applied in DFMA. How does it differ from manufacturing?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Assembly is the process of **joining two or more manufactured components** using fastening, bonding, or forming techniques to form a complete, functional product. It differs from manufacturing as follows:

| Aspect | Manufacturing | Assembly |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Raw material (sheet, bar, billet) | Discrete manufactured parts |
| Process | Shaping, cutting, forming | Joining, mating, fastening |
| Output | Individual components | Sub-assemblies or final product |
| Example | Turning a shaft | Mounting the shaft into a gearbox housing |

Assembly is the **integration phase**; manufacturing is the **creation phase**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **three assembly process metrics** used in DFMA evaluation with one-line definitions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Assembly Efficiency (η)** = Ratio of theoretical minimum parts to actual parts × 100%.
2. **Assembly Cost (C_a)** = Total labor time × wage rate + overheads.
3. **Assembly Defect Rate (D)** = Fraction of products failing final inspection (defects per million opportunities).
4. **Annual Savings from DFA** = (Cost_before − Cost_after) × Annual volume.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Module-based Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Standard split: **Part (a) = 7 marks**, **Part (b) = 7 marks**.

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Assembly Process Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **six sequential stages of the assembly process** from component presentation to final verification. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The six stages of the assembly process are:

1. **Part Presentation (Feeding):** Components are delivered to the workstation in an oriented state using hoppers, bowl feeders, or trays.
2. **Grasping / Pickup:** The component is held by a gripper, vacuum chuck, or hand.
3. **Transportation:** The held part is moved to the mating station along a controlled path.
4. **Location / Alignment:** Fixtures, pins, or vision systems ensure precise spatial alignment.
5. **Joining (Mating):** Insertion, snap-fit, screwing, welding, or riveting occurs.
6. **Verification:** Torque check, vision inspection, leak test, or functional test confirms quality.

*Refer to the Functional Architecture Diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete block-level flow.*

**(b)** A product consists of **15 parts**. The ideal minimum-part design requires only **6 parts**. Calculate the **assembly efficiency**. If the average handling time per part is **4 seconds**, insertion time is **6 seconds**, and the wage rate is **₹0.4 per second**, calculate the **assembly cost per unit** (assume overhead = ₹50). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b)-i: Assembly Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{assembly} &= \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{6}{15} \times 100\% \\
&= 40\%
\end{aligned}$$

This is **below acceptable** (KTU benchmark ≥ 60%) — design needs simplification.

**Part (b)-ii: Assembly Cost per Unit**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{i,\text{total}} &= h_i + t_i = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ seconds} \\
T_{assembly} &= N_{actual} \times T_{i,\text{total}} \\
&= 15 \times 10 \\
&= 150 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{assembly} &= T_{assembly} \times C_w + C_{overhead} \\
&= 150 \times 0.4 + 50 \\
&= 60 + 50 \\
&= \text{₹110 per unit}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_{assembly} = 40\%$, $C_{assembly} = ₹110/\text{unit}$.

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Assembly System Selection & Cost Optimization*

**(a)** Compare **Manual, Semi-Automatic, Automatic, and Robotic Assembly systems** in terms of production volume, flexibility, defect rate, and investment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Manual | Semi-Automatic | Automatic | Robotic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Production Volume | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium–High |
| Product Variety (Flexibility) | Very High | Medium | Low | Very High |
| Defect Rate (%) | 1–3% | 0.5–1% | 0.05–0.2% | 0.1–0.5% |
| Initial Investment | Very Low | Moderate | Very High | High |
| Labor Cost / Unit | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| Best Application | Prototypes | Mixed batch | Mass production | Mixed-model lines |

**Conclusion:** Robotic assembly offers the **best balance of flexibility and quality** for modern Industry 4.0 factories; manual is reserved for prototypes.

**(b)** A company produces **20,000 units/year**. Current assembly cost is **₹150/unit**. After applying DFA principles, the new design reduces the part count and assembly cost to **₹105/unit**. Calculate the **annual savings**. If the design change required a one-time tooling investment of **₹4,00,000**, determine the **payback period** in months. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b)-i: Annual Savings**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{\text{per unit}} &= 150 - 105 = \text{₹45} \\
\Delta C_{\text{annual}} &= 45 \times 20000 \\
&= \text{₹9,00,000 per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b)-ii: Payback Period**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback (months)} &= \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Monthly Savings}} \\
&= \frac{400000}{900000 / 12} \\
&= \frac{400000}{75000} \\
&= 5.33 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Annual savings = **₹9,00,000**; Payback period ≈ **5.33 months** (excellent ROI).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "assembly" with "manufacturing"** — they are NOT synonyms; assembly ALWAYS starts AFTER parts are made.
> 2. **Forgetting overhead in cost calculations** — many students compute only labor cost and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing η_assembly > 100%** — this is mathematically impossible; check your $N_{min}$ and $N_{actual}$.
> 4. **Skipping units in final answer** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing ₹/s, seconds, units/year.
> 5. **Not drawing the flow diagram** — in 14-mark questions, a missing block diagram costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential points to remember before the KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Assembly = Joining pre-manufactured parts**, NOT making them.
- ✅ The **6-stage assembly process** = Presentation → Grasping → Transport → Align → Join → Verify.
- ✅ **Assembly Efficiency** $\eta = (N_{min} / N_{actual}) \times 100\%$; target ≥ 60%.
- ✅ **Four assembly system classes** = Manual, Semi-Auto, Automatic, Robotic.
- ✅ **Assembly cost** = (Total assembly time × Wage rate) + Overhead.
- ✅ **Annual savings from DFA** = (Cost_before − Cost_after) × Annual volume.
- ✅ **Payback period** = Investment / (Annual savings / 12) in months.
- ✅ Assembly typically consumes **30–50% of total labor** and accounts for **~70% of defects**.
- ✅ The Boothroyd–Dewhurst minimum-part principle is the **golden rule of DFA**.
- ✅ Always convert assembly time to **consistent SI units** (seconds) before computing cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Characteristics and Applications" in DFMA?

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA) — Module 4**, the phrase *"Characteristics and Applications"* refers to the systematic study of the **engineering attributes (features, behaviors, performance metrics)** of various **fastening, joining, and assembly methods** used in product design, and the **industrial contexts (applications)** in which each method is best deployed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *"Characteristics and Applications"* in DFMA Module 4 is the study of the distinctive geometric, mechanical, economic, and process-related features of standard assembly techniques (such as threaded fasteners, snap-fits, rivets, clips, adhesives, welds, and press-fits) along with the engineering rationale for selecting each technique for specific product applications in automotive, aerospace, electronics, and consumer goods industries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Construction Toolbox" Intuition

Imagine you are **building a toy model airplane** for the first time.

- You have a **box of tools**: glue, screws, snap-pieces, velcro strips, rivets, and clips.
- Each tool has its own **"personality"** — glue is permanent but slow, snap-fits are fast but weak, screws are strong but need a screwdriver.
- Choosing the right tool depends on **what you are joining** (plastic wing vs. metal fuselage), **how often** you need to take it apart, and **how strong** the joint must be.

This is exactly what an engineer does in DFMA — they pick the **"right tool"** (joining method) based on the **"job description"** (application requirement). The **characteristics** are the "personality" of each tool, and the **applications** are the "jobs" they are hired for.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Aspect | Relevance to KTU Exam |
|---|---|
| **Design Selection** | Tests the engineer's ability to justify method choice |
| **CO Mapping** | Aligns with CO4: *Apply DFMA principles in real product design* |
| **Industrial Context** | Bridges academic design with industry practice |
| **Cost & Time** | Highlights DFA Boothroyd principle: *minimize part count* |

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used (Standard DFMA Terminology)

The following standard metrics govern the choice of assembly method:

- **Assembly Time (tₐ):** Time taken to add one part to the assembly, measured in **seconds**.
- **Insertion Force (Fᵢ):** Force required to position and align a part, measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Retention Force (Fᵣ):** Force resisting dislodgement after assembly, measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Number of Parts (Nₚ):** Total discrete components (Boothroyd metric).
- **Cost per Assembly Operation (Cₐ):** Measured in **₹ or $ per operation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 emphasizes that the *characteristics* of a joining method must be studied **before** deciding its *application*. Students must memorize at least **4-5 standard methods** with their characteristics and 2-3 real-world applications each.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Assembly Methods

The major assembly methods studied under DFMA Module 4 can be classified into four broad families:

1. **Mechanical Fasteners (Permanent)** — Rivets, nails, staples, crimped joints
2. **Mechanical Fasteners (Removable)** — Screws, bolts, nuts, threaded inserts
3. **Formed Features** — Snap-fits, clips, latches, hinges
4. **Material Joining** — Adhesives, welding (spot, arc, ultrasonic), brazing, soldering
5. **Press-fits and Interference Fits** — Press-fit pins, heat-shrunk assemblies

## 2.2 Characteristics of Major Assembly Methods

### 2.2.1 Threaded Fasteners (Screws and Bolts)

- **Mechanical Principle:** Convert rotational motion (torque **T**) into axial clamping force (**F_clamp**) via helix angle **λ**.
- **Holding Power:** Very high tensile and shear strength; preferred for **critical joints** in engines, machinery, and structural frames.
- **Reversibility:** Fully reversible — can be disassembled multiple times without damage.
- **Cost Consideration:** High cost per fastener and high assembly time (multiple operations: align, insert, drive, tighten).
- **Standardization:** Available in metric (ISO 261) and unified (ANSI B1.1) thread standards.

### 2.2.2 Snap-Fits

- **Mechanical Principle:** Use cantilever beam deflection to create temporary elastic deformation, then locking via an undercut or shoulder.
- **Holding Power:** Moderate; sufficient for plastic enclosures, battery covers, and consumer electronics.
- **Reversibility:** Some are irreversible (single snap), some are reversible (with a release tab).
- **Assembly Time:** Among the lowest — typically **1–2 seconds** per snap.
- **Cost Consideration:** No additional hardware required — feature is molded into the part itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boothroyd's DFA Insight:** Snap-fits are the *gold standard* for reducing part count and assembly time. A typical electronic enclosure uses 6–8 snap-fits to replace 6–8 screws.

### 2.2.3 Rivets

- **Mechanical Principle:** Plastic deformation of a ductile shank forms a second head, locking two or more sheets together.
- **Holding Power:** High shear strength, moderate tensile strength.
- **Reversibility:** **Permanent** — cannot be removed without destruction.
- **Application Niche:** Aerospace skins, structural bridges, sheet metal enclosures.
- **Variants:** Solid rivets, blind rivets ("pop rivets"), tubular rivets, semi-tubular rivets.

### 2.2.4 Adhesive Bonding

- **Mechanical Principle:** Chemical adhesion (primary bonds) + mechanical interlocking (secondary bonds) at the molecular level.
- **Holding Power:** Excellent for shear and tension when surface prep is correct; **weak in peel and cleavage**.
- **Surface Requirement:** Requires clean, degreased, sometimes roughened surfaces.
- **Cure Time:** Can range from seconds (cyanoacrylate / "super glue") to hours (epoxy).
- **Reversibility:** Generally permanent; some adhesives allow heat-based debonding.

### 2.2.5 Welding (Spot, Arc, Ultrasonic)

- **Mechanical Principle:** Localized fusion of base materials (metals) or localized melting (plastics via ultrasonic vibration).
- **Holding Power:** Equal to or greater than base material strength.
- **Reversibility:** **Permanent** — joint cannot be separated.
- **Application Niche:** Automotive body-in-white (spot welding of 4000+ points per car), plastic toys, sheet metal.

### 2.2.6 Press-Fits and Interference Fits

- **Mechanical Principle:** Elastic and plastic deformation generates a radial interference pressure **p** at the interface.
- **Holding Power:** Very high for circular shafts; depends on diametrical interference.
- **Reversibility:** Semi-permanent; can be removed with force or hydraulic press.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = K \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot d$ | Torque-clamp force relationship for a bolt | $T$ in N·m, $d$ = nominal diameter (m), $K$ = nut factor (≈ 0.20 for steel-on-steel) |
| 2 | $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{T}{K \cdot d}$ | Clamp force from applied torque | **Newtons (N)** |
| 3 | $\sigma_{bolt} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_s}$ | Tensile stress in bolt shank | $A_s$ = stress area (m²) |
| 4 | $F_{snap} = \dfrac{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \delta}{L^3}$ | Cantilever snap-fit deflection force (linear approx.) | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $I$ = second moment of area, $L$ = beam length, $\delta$ = deflection |
| 5 | $p = \dfrac{\delta}{d} \cdot \dfrac{E}{2}$ (approx. for hub-shaft) | Interference contact pressure | $p$ in Pa, $\delta$ = diametral interference, $d$ = nominal diameter |
| 6 | $t_a = \sum t_{i} + \sum t_{h}$ | Boothroyd total assembly time | $t_i$ = insertion times, $t_h$ = handling times (seconds) |
| 7 | $C_a = C_m \cdot t_a \cdot R_w$ | Cost of one assembly operation | $C_m$ = labor rate (₹/s), $R_w$ = wage rate factor |
| 8 | $\lambda = \arctan\left(\dfrac{L}{2\pi r}\right)$ | Helix angle of a thread | $L$ = lead, $r$ = mean thread radius |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** All absolute value bars and divisions are written using `\vert` or `\mid` style syntax to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. Inline equations are wrapped in `$...$` and display equations in `$$...$$`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A modern car uses **~3000+ spot welds** in its body-in-white, **~150+ threaded fasteners** in the engine, and **~50+ snap-fits** in the interior. Choosing the right method determines **manufacturing cost, serviceability, and NVH performance**.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones use **snap-fits, ultrasonic welding, and adhesive bonding** — *not a single screw* in many designs. This is a deliberate DFMA optimization to reduce part count and improve aesthetics.
- **Aerospace:** Rivets dominate wing-skin assembly because they offer high strength-to-weight ratio and proven reliability under cyclic loading (riveted joints show better fatigue life than adhesive joints in some studies).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Snap-Fit Deflection Force

The snap-fit is modeled as a **cantilever beam** of rectangular cross-section, fixed at one end and deflected by an amount $\delta$ at the free end. We want to derive the lateral force $F_{snap}$ required to produce this deflection.

### Step 1 — Define the Beam Geometry

Let the snap-fit cantilever have:
- Length = $L$ (m)
- Width = $b$ (m)
- Thickness = $t$ (m)
- Young's modulus = $E$ (Pa)

The second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section about the neutral axis is:

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Cantilever Beam Equation

For a cantilever beam with a point load $F$ at the free end, the tip deflection is given by:

$$
\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

### Step 3 — Invert to Express Force in Terms of Deflection

Solving the above for $F$:

$$
F = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \delta}{L^3}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $I$ for a Rectangular Cross-Section

Substituting $I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$ into the force equation:

$$
F_{snap} = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}\right) \cdot \delta}{L^3}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{snap} &= \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{12 \cdot L^3} \\
F_{snap} &= \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Working Equation

$$
F_{snap} = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^3}
$$

This is the **linear elastic** approximation, valid for small deflections where $\delta / L < 0.05$. For larger deflections, a correction factor (typically 1.3 to 1.5) is applied to account for non-linear geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** From the equation, $F_{snap} \propto t^3$ — the **thickness** of the snap arm is the most sensitive parameter. A 10% increase in thickness produces a **~33% increase** in force. This is why DFMA designers carefully size snap-arm thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque-Preload Relationship for a Threaded Bolt

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $T$ = applied tightening torque (N·m)
- $F_{clamp}$ = resulting clamp (preload) force in the bolt (N)
- $d$ = nominal bolt diameter (m)
- $K$ = nut factor (dimensionless, also called the "K-factor")

### Step 2 — Standard Empirical Relationship (from Shigley's / Bhandari)

The torque required to produce a given preload in a bolted joint is:

$$
T = K \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot d
$$

### Step 3 — Invert to Find Preload from Torque

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{T}{K \cdot d}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Resulting Bolt Stress

The tensile stress developed in the bolt (using the stress area $A_s$) is:

$$
\sigma_{bolt} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_s} = \frac{T}{K \cdot d \cdot A_s}
$$

### Step 5 — Yield Check (Safety Verification)

The bolt is safe against yielding if:

$$
\sigma_{bolt} \le \frac{S_{yt}}{n}
$$

where $S_{yt}$ is the yield strength of the bolt material and $n$ is the factor of safety (typically 1.5–2.5 for static loading).

### Step 6 — Substitute Numeric Example

For an M10 bolt ($d = 0.010$ m, $A_s \approx 58 \text{ mm}^2 = 58 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$) tightened to $T = 30$ N·m with $K = 0.20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{30}{0.20 \times 0.010} = 15{,}000 \text{ N} \\
\sigma_{bolt} &= \frac{15{,}000}{58 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 258.6 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a Class 8.8 bolt ($S_{yt} = 640$ MPa), the factor of safety is:

$$
n = \frac{640}{258.6} \approx 2.47
$$

This is a safe design for static loading.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Method vs. Application

| Assembly Method | Reversibility | Holding Power | Assembly Time | Cost/Part | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Threaded Bolt** | Removable | Very High | High (~10–30 s) | High | Engine assembly, structural frames |
| **Snap-Fit** | Irreversible (mostly) | Moderate | Very Low (~1–2 s) | Very Low (zero) | Plastic enclosures, electronic housings |
| **Rivet** | Permanent | High (shear) | Medium (~3–5 s) | Low | Aerospace skins, sheet-metal joints |
| **Adhesive** | Permanent | High (shear/tension) | Low (apply) + Cure Time | Low–Medium | Composite panels, glass-metal joints |
| **Spot Weld** | Permanent | Very High | Very Low (~0.5 s/point) | Low (in-line) | Automotive body-in-white |
| **Press-Fit** | Semi-permanent | Very High | Low | Zero extra part | Shaft-hub assemblies, bearings |
| **Clip (Sheet Metal)** | Often removable | Low–Moderate | Low (~1 s) | Zero extra part | Automotive trim panels, dashboards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (Boothroyd):** If the assembly can be done in less than **3 seconds per part** with **no additional hardware** (i.e., using formed features like snap-fits), it scores the maximum DFA index. Screws typically score the lowest because of the multi-step operation and additional hardware.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Selecting an Assembly Method

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **DFMA decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting an assembly method based on product requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Joining Requirement] --> B{Is Disassembly Needed?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Repeated Access Required?}
    B -- No --> D{Is High Strength Critical?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Select Threaded Fastener or Bolt]
    C -- No --> F[Select Clip or Quarter-Turn Fastener]
    D -- Yes --> G{Is It a Sheet Metal or Skin Joint?}
    D -- No --> H{Is It a Polymer or Composite?}
    G -- Yes --> I[Select Rivet or Spot Weld]
    G -- No --> J[Select Arc Weld or Adhesive]
    H -- Yes --> K[Select Adhesive or Ultrasonic Weld]
    H -- No --> L[Select Press-Fit]
    I --> M[End: Method Selected]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    E --> M
    F --> M
    stepA[Step A: Define Joint Requirements] --> A
    stepB[Step B: Apply Selection Matrix] --> M
    stepC[Step C: Verify with DFA Index] --> M
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Start at node `A` with the design problem.
- Branch `B` checks whether the joint must be **serviceable** (removable).
- Subsequent nodes progressively narrow down the choice based on **strength**, **material**, and **joint type**.
- End at node `M` with the **selected assembly method**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Snap-Fit Assembly Sequence

The schematic below illustrates the **mechanical engagement sequence** of a typical cantilever snap-fit during assembly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Part A: Cantilever Arm] --> B1[Approach: Part B with Undercut]
    B1 --> C1[Deflection: Arm Bends Outward]
    C1 --> D1[Engagement: Arm Snaps Over Shoulder]
    D1 --> E1[Lock: Arm Returns to Neutral Position]
    E1 --> F1[Final Assembly: Locked Joint]
    F1 --> G1[End: Snap-Fit Complete]
    stepX1[Step 1: Initial Position] --> A1
    stepX2[Step 2: Elastic Deformation] --> C1
    stepX3[Step 3: Plastic Recovery and Lock] --> E1
```

**Interpretation:**
- The cantilever arm starts straight (`A1`).
- As the mating part approaches, the arm deflects elastically (`C1`).
- After clearing the shoulder, it returns to its original shape, locking the joint (`E1`).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Interference Between Characteristics and Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Characteristics: Mechanical, Economic, Process] --> B2{Considered by Engineer}
    C2[Applications: Industry, Product, Function] --> B2
    B2 --> D2[DFMA Method Selection]
    D2 --> E2[Optimized Product Design]
    stepA1[Step Alpha: Identify Characteristics] --> A2
    stepB1[Step Beta: Map Applications] --> C2
    stepG1[Step Gamma: Select Best Method] --> D2
    stepD1[Step Delta: Verify via Testing] --> E2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:** All node IDs (`A1`, `B1`, etc.) are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. All labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown bold/italic formatting inside the quotes, ensuring clean Mermaid rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, 3 Marks]**
**Q: List any three characteristics of snap-fits that make them suitable for high-volume consumer electronics assembly.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Zero additional hardware** — the locking feature is molded into the part itself, eliminating the need for a separate fastener and reducing part count.
2. **Very low assembly time** (~1–2 seconds per snap) — enables fast, automated assembly, ideal for high-volume production lines.
3. **Self-aligning geometry** — the snap arm guides the mating part into position, reducing operator skill requirements.
4. (Optional) **Cost-effective** — no inventory of separate fasteners, no feeding mechanism required in the assembly station.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern, 3 Marks]**
**Q: Differentiate between a reversible and an irreversible assembly method with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Reversible Method | Irreversible Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Disassembly** | Possible without damage | Not possible without destruction |
| **Serviceability** | Suitable for serviceable joints | Suitable for permanent joints |
| **Example** | Threaded bolt, quarter-turn clip | Rivet, spot weld, adhesive |
| **Reusability** | Fastener can be reused | Joint must be replaced if opened |

A **reversible** example is a **M6 hex bolt** in a gearbox casing. An **irreversible** example is a **blind rivet** joining aluminium sheets in a window frame.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the characteristics of **three** major assembly methods used in automotive body-in-white (BIW) manufacturing, with reference to holding power, reversibility, and assembly time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cantilever snap-fit is to be designed for a polypropylene enclosure. The arm has the following dimensions: length $L = 15$ mm, width $b = 6$ mm, thickness $t = 1.2$ mm, and is to be deflected by $\delta = 1.0$ mm during engagement. The Young's modulus of polypropylene is $E = 1.4$ GPa. Calculate the engagement force $F_{snap}$. Is this a feasible design? Comment. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The three major assembly methods in BIW are:

1. **Spot Welding (Resistance Welding)**
   - *Holding Power:* Very high — weld nugget strength typically 4–6 kN per spot.
   - *Reversibility:* Permanent — joint cannot be separated without cutting.
   - *Assembly Time:* Very low — ~0.5 s per spot (in robotic production lines).
   - *Application:* Joining roof, floor pans, side panels. A typical sedan uses 4000–5000 spot welds.

2. **Adhesive Bonding (Structural Epoxy)**
   - *Holding Power:* High in shear, low in peel.
   - *Reversibility:* Permanent.
   - *Assembly Time:* Application is fast (~1 s per bead) but **cure time is 30 min to 1 hr** in an oven.
   - *Application:* Hem-flange joints, composite-aluminium hybrid joints.

3. **Mechanical Fasteners (Bolt and Nut)**
   - *Holding Power:* Very high (clamp force ~15 kN for M10 grade 8.8 bolt).
   - *Reversibility:* Fully reversible — designed for subassemblies (engine, suspension, doors).
   - *Assembly Time:* High (~20–30 s per bolt in manual assembly).
   - *Application:* Engine cradle, suspension towers, door hinges.

**Conclusion:** Spot welding dominates BIW because of speed and automation; adhesives complement welds for stiffness and NVH; bolts are reserved for serviceable joints.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $L = 15$ mm $= 15 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $b = 6$ mm $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $t = 1.2$ mm $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\delta = 1.0$ mm $= 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $E = 1.4 \text{ GPa} = 1.4 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1 — Calculate Second Moment of Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} \\
  &= \frac{(6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^3}{12} \\
  &= \frac{(6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1.728 \times 10^{-9})}{12} \\
  &= \frac{1.0368 \times 10^{-11}}{12} \\
  &= 8.64 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Engagement Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{snap} &= \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^3} \\
         &= \frac{(1.4 \times 10^9) \cdot (6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1.728 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-3})}{4 \cdot (15 \times 10^{-3})^3} \\
         &= \frac{1.4515 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \cdot (3.375 \times 10^{-6})} \\
         &= \frac{1.4515 \times 10^{-5}}{1.35 \times 10^{-5}} \\
         &\approx 1.075 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility Check:**

- The engagement force of **~1.075 N** is well within the human-assembly comfort range (typically 5–20 N is acceptable; below 1 N is borderline for tactile feedback).
- The **deflection ratio** $\delta / L = 1.0 / 15 = 0.067$, which is **slightly above** the linear-elastic limit (0.05). A non-linear correction factor of ~1.4 should be applied, giving effective force ≈ 1.5 N.
- This is feasible for a hand-assembled plastic enclosure.

**Conclusion:** The design is acceptable but at the lower limit of tactile feedback. Increasing thickness to $t = 1.5$ mm would raise the force to ~2.1 N (linear model) for better snap action.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **adhesive bonding** and **spot welding** for joining two steel sheets of 1.2 mm thickness. Discuss holding power, surface preparation, assembly time, reversibility, and typical industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An M8 bolt of grade 8.8 (yield strength $S_{yt} = 660$ MPa, stress area $A_s = 36.6$ mm²) is tightened to a torque of 18 N·m. The nut factor is $K = 0.20$. Calculate (i) the clamp force and (ii) the factor of safety against yield. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

| Criterion | Adhesive Bonding | Spot Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Holding Power** | High in shear; weak in peel and cleavage | Very high in both shear and tension |
| **Surface Preparation** | Requires degreasing, abrasion, and sometimes primers | Requires clean metal but minimal prep |
| **Assembly Time** | Quick application (~1 s), but **cure time of 30–60 min** | Instantaneous (~0.5 s per spot) |
| **Reversibility** | Permanent | Permanent |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low (dispensing gun + oven) | High (welding transformer + electrodes) |
| **Industrial Application** | Hem-flange joints in car doors, composite panels | Body panels, structural frames, exhaust systems |

**Conclusion:** Spot welding is preferred for high-speed, structural BIW joints. Adhesives complement welds where sealing and NVH damping are also needed.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Bolt: M8, $d = 0.008$ m
- $S_{yt} = 660$ MPa $= 660 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $A_s = 36.6 \text{ mm}^2 = 36.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $T = 18$ N·m
- $K = 0.20$

**Step (i) — Clamp Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{T}{K \cdot d} \\
          &= \frac{18}{0.20 \times 0.008} \\
          &= \frac{18}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} \\
          &= 11{,}250 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Factor of Safety:**

First, calculate the bolt stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bolt} &= \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_s} \\
              &= \frac{11{,}250}{36.6 \times 10^{-6}} \\
              &= 3.0738 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \\
              &\approx 307.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, the factor of safety:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_{bolt}} \\
  &= \frac{660}{307.4} \\
  &\approx 2.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The factor of safety of **2.15** is acceptable for static loading in general machinery. For dynamic or fatigue loading, this margin may be insufficient and a higher grade bolt (e.g., 10.9) or a larger size should be considered.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Characteristics" with "Advantages"** — Characteristics are factual attributes (e.g., "permanent", "reversible"); advantages are subjective benefits (e.g., "low cost", "easy to use"). Examiners deduct marks if these are mixed.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Final Answers** — Numerical answers without units (e.g., writing "1.075" instead of "1.075 N") typically lose **0.5 to 1 mark** per sub-question.
> 3. **Not Stating Assumptions** — In snap-fit and press-fit derivations, students must explicitly state the **linear-elastic assumption** or the **thin-beam assumption** to get full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Feasibility Comment"** — In KTU 2024 pattern, the 7-mark numerical sub-question often carries a 1-mark component for "engineering interpretation" or "feasibility comment". Do not skip this.
> 5. **Using the wrong stress area ($A_s$)** — For bolts, the **stress area** is NOT the same as the nominal cross-sectional area. Use standard tables (e.g., $A_s = 36.6$ mm² for M8, not $A = \pi/4 \times 8^2 = 50.3$ mm²).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Characteristics of an assembly method** include: reversibility, holding power, assembly time, cost, surface preparation, equipment requirement, and standardization.
- **Applications** are the product/industry contexts where a method is best deployed (e.g., snap-fits → consumer electronics, rivets → aerospace, spot welds → automotive BIW).
- **Boothroyd's DFA principle:** Minimize part count, maximize the use of **self-locking features** (snap-fits, clips) over **additional fasteners** (screws, bolts).
- **Key formulas to memorize for KTU exam:**
  - $F_{snap} = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^3}$
  - $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{T}{K \cdot d}$
  - $t_a = \sum t_i + \sum t_h$
  - $C_a = C_m \cdot t_a \cdot R_w$
- **Rule of thumb:** Snap-fits are best for low-to-moderate strength plastic joints; threaded fasteners are best for high-strength, serviceable joints; welds are best for permanent high-speed metallic joints.
- **Always check units** — examiners in KTU 2024 scheme place heavy emphasis on unit consistency in numerical problems.
- **Mention feasibility / industrial relevance** in every long-answer — this often fetches 1 bonus mark per sub-question.
- **Snap-fit design tip:** Force scales as $t^3$ — small thickness changes have large force effects.
- **Bolt design tip:** A 10% increase in tightening torque produces a 10% increase in clamp force (linear relation).
- **Memorize the K-factor:** $K = 0.20$ for steel-on-steel, $K = 0.10$ for lubricated threads, $K = 0.30$ for plain (unlubricated) threads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples of Common Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, a **Common Assembly** refers to a frequently recurring, standardized joining configuration between two or more components that is widely used across industrial product families and which can be classified using established assembly evaluation principles (such as **Boothroyd's** handling and insertion taxonomy). Common assemblies form the building blocks of virtually every mass-produced engineering product — from consumer electronics to automotive sub-systems and aerospace structures.

A common assembly is characterized by three essential features:

1. **Repetitive Application** — Used in high volumes across multiple products.
2. **Standardized Joining Principle** — Based on a single dominant fastening/joining mechanism (threaded, snap, press, weld, rivet, adhesive, solder, stake, fold, coin).
3. **Quantifiable Insertion Behaviour** — Each joining operation can be evaluated against handling time, insertion time, and tooling cost metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Common assemblies are studied as **case-study exemplars** to apply the **Minimum Number of Parts** concept, the **DFA Index**, and **Assembly Cost Models** learned earlier in the syllabus. They are *not* a study of fasteners in isolation, but of how fasteners govern **total assembly efficiency**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a meal at a self-service counter. You have many possible "joining operations" — you can *stack* ingredients (press-fit), you can *wrap* them (snap-fit/enclosure), you can *stitch* them with a toothpick (rivet/pin), you can *fuse* them with heat (weld/solder), or you can *tie* them (bolt-fastener). Each method takes a different amount of time, requires different "tools" (your fingers vs. tongs), and offers different "strength" of the final plate.

A **DFMA engineer** is essentially the chef who decides which plate can be assembled fastest, cheapest, and most reliably for a million meals per day. **Common assemblies** are the chef's *reusable recipes* — the standardized operations that keep being repeated across the menu.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Common Assemblies

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assembly Efficiency Index | $\eta_{A}$ | 0–1 (dimensionless) | Fraction of ideal assembly time achieved |
| Minimum Theoretical Parts | $N_{m}$ | Integer ≥ 1 | Lower bound on part count |
| Actual Part Count | $N_{a}$ | Integer ≥ $N_{m}$ | Realized part count in the design |
| DFA Index | $I_{DFA}$ | 0–100 (%) | Ratio of ideal to actual part count |
| Assembly Cost per Part | $C_{i}$ | **₹0.5 – ₹50** (depending on automation) | Per-part insertion/handling cost |
| Insertion Time per Part | $t_{i}$ | **1.5 s – 6.0 s** (manual), **0.3 s – 1.0 s** (auto) | Operation duration metric |
| Annual Volume Threshold | $V_{a}$ | **10⁴ – 10⁷** units | Decides manual vs. automated assembly |
| Assembly Direction Score | $k_{d}$ | 1, 1.5, 2, 3 (discrete) | Difficulty penalty multiplier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hand-coding convention used by Boothroyd:** A part coded **0** in a column means it is "ideal" for assembly (no rotation, no symmetry issues, single-direction insertion). A code of **3** is the worst case (large parts requiring multi-axis insertion).

---

## 1.4 The Seven Canonical Common Assembly Families

For KTU Module 4 evaluation purposes, all common assemblies reduce to **seven canonical families**:

1. **Threaded (Bolt–Nut / Screw)** — *Reversible, high-strength*
2. **Snap-Fit** — *Permanent plastic deformation, low-cost*
3. **Press-Fit / Interference Fit** — *Friction-locked, often sub-micron tolerance*
4. **Riveted** — *Permanent deformation, sheet-metal legacy*
5. **Welded** — *Atomic-level joining, fusion*
6. **Adhesive Bonded** — *Surface-area dependent, chemical cure*
7. **Soldered / Brazed** — *Metallurgical, thermal-capillary joining*

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner expects students to be able to **name, sketch, and classify** each of these families along with a representative product example (e.g., *bolt–nut → automotive chassis*; *snap-fit → mobile phone housing*; *press-fit → bearing in a gearbox hub*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boothroyd's Three-Criteria Minimum-Parts Filter (The Foundation of DFA)

Every part in a product is interrogated against three binary criteria. If **all three answers are NO**, the part is a candidate for **elimination** (it can be merged into an adjacent part without functional penalty).

1. **Movement Criterion** — Does the part move relative to all other parts during intended operation?
2. **Material Criterion** — Must the part be made of a different material than adjacent parts?
3. **Separation Criterion** — Must the part be separable from adjacent parts for assembly, servicing, or recycling?

The resulting minimum part count is:

$$N_{m} \; = \; \sum_{i=1}^{N_{a}} \delta_{i}$$

where the indicator function $\delta_{i}$ is defined as:

$$\delta_{i} \; = \; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if part } i \text{ passes at least one criterion (must exist)} \\ 0 & \text{if part } i \text{ fails all three (can be merged)} \end{cases}$$

---

## 2.2 The DFA Index (Boothroyd–Dewhurst)

The DFA Index is the single most-asked quantitative measure in the KTU 2024 ESE for DFMA. It compares the **ideal** (minimum) part count against the **actual** part count:

$$I_{DFA} \; = \; \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100 \; \text{(\%)}$$

Interpretation:
- $I_{DFA} = 100\%$ → Perfect design (no excess parts).
- $I_{DFA} < 30\%$ → Poor DFA; expect heavy mark loss in design efficiency.
- $I_{DFA} > 60\%$ → Good industrial design.

---

## 2.3 Manual vs. Automated Assembly Time Model

The base insertion time for a single part depends on:

- The **handling code** $h_{i}$ (0 = trivial, 1 = asymmetric, 2 = symmetric-with-lineup, 3 = tangled).
- The **insertion code** $k_{i}$ (0 = downward line-of-sight, 1 = obstructed, 2 = reorientation, 3 = far-target).
- The **size** of the part (no separate cost below a threshold).

The empirical Boothroyd time is:

$$t_{i} \; = \; t_{h}(h_{i}) \; + \; t_{i}^{ins}(k_{i})$$

Typical numerical values used in industry:

| Code | Handling Time $t_h$ (s) | Insertion Time $t_i^{ins}$ (s) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 1 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 3.0 |

For automated assembly, every time value above is scaled down by an automation factor $\alpha_{auto} \approx \mathbf{0.1 \; to \; 0.3}$.

---

## 2.4 Total Assembly Cost

The total cost of an assembly line is the sum of all per-part operations multiplied by the production volume and the labour/overhead rate:

$$C_{assembly} \; = \; V_{a} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_{a}} \left( t_{i} \cdot r_{L} + \frac{C_{tool}}{V_{tool}} \right)$$

where:

- $V_{a}$ = **annual production volume**
- $r_{L}$ = **loaded labour rate** (₹/s) including overheads
- $C_{tool}$ = **tooling amortized cost** (₹)
- $V_{tool}$ = **tooling lifetime in parts**

---

## 2.5 Snap-Fit Cantilever Beam Theory (Deflection)

Snap-fits are central to common plastic assemblies. The cantilever deflection under a mating force $F$ is:

$$\delta_{snap} \; = \; \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where the second moment of area for a rectangular cantilever is:

$$I \; = \; \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$$

The maximum bending stress at the root of the cantilever is:

$$\sigma_{max} \; = \; \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}}$$

Material yield check:

$$\sigma_{max} \; \leq \; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$$

where $n$ = **factor of safety** (typically **1.5 – 2.5** for plastic snap-fits).

---

## 2.6 Press-Fit / Interference Fit Stress

For a cylindrical shaft of outer diameter $d_{s}$ pressed into a hub of inner diameter $d_{h}$ with diametrical interference $\Delta = d_{s} - d_{h}$:

$$p_{contact} \; = \; \frac{\Delta}{2 \left( \dfrac{d_{s}}{E_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{d_{h}^{2} + d_{s}^{2}}{d_{s}^{2} - d_{h}^{2}} + \dfrac{d_{h}}{E_{h}} \cdot \dfrac{d_{o}^{2} + d_{h}^{2}}{d_{o}^{2} - d_{h}^{2}} \right)}$$

where $d_{o}$ is the **outer diameter of the hub**, $E_{s}$ and $E_{h}$ are the Young's moduli of shaft and hub.

The axial holding force is:

$$F_{hold} \; = \; p_{contact} \cdot \pi \cdot d_{s} \cdot L_{contact} \cdot \mu$$

with $\mu$ = **coefficient of friction** (typically **0.08 – 0.15** for steel-on-steel, **0.20 – 0.35** for steel-on-aluminium).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Single-Block Quick Revision)

| Formula | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| DFA Index | $I_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100$ | $N_m, N_a$ dimensionless |
| Min-Parts Filter | $\delta_i = 1 \text{ if any criterion = YES}$ | Boolean indicator |
| Total Assembly Time | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_i$ | Seconds |
| Total Assembly Cost | $C_{assy} = V_a \sum_{i} (t_i r_L + C_{tool}/V_{tool})$ | ₹ |
| Snap-fit Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$ | m |
| Cantilever Second Moment | $I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | m⁴ |
| Snap-fit Max Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{b h^{2}}$ | Pa |
| Press-fit Contact Pressure | $p = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\left[\dfrac{d_s}{E_s} \cdot \dfrac{d_h^{2}+d_s^{2}}{d_s^{2}-d_h^{2}} + \dfrac{d_h}{E_h} \cdot \dfrac{d_o^{2}+d_h^{2}}{d_o^{2}-d_h^{2}}\right]}$ | Pa |
| Press-fit Axial Holding Force | $F_{hold} = p \pi d_s L_{contact} \mu$ | N |
| Welding Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/mm |
| Rivet Shear Capacity | $F_{R} = 0.75 \cdot A_{R} \cdot \tau_{ult}$ | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are the **daily tools** of a DFMA engineer at Tata Motors, Mahindra, Bosch India, or HAL. The DFA Index is quoted in every design review to justify part-combination decisions; the snap-fit deflection formula governs the choice of wall-thickness in every consumer-durable enclosure; the press-fit pressure equation is checked against spindle-bearing pull-off loads in every gearbox.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — DFA Index for a Simple Bracket Assembly

**Problem Statement:** A bracket assembly has **9 parts** (1 base plate, 2 gussets, 1 vertical pillar, 1 flange, 2 nuts, 1 bolt, 1 washer). Apply Boothroyd's three-criteria test to determine the minimum number of parts and the DFA Index.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify each part and apply the three criteria.

| # | Part Name | Movement? | Different Material? | Separation? | Retain? |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Base Plate | No (fixed) | No (steel) | No (could merge) | **No** |
| 2 | Gusset-L | No (fixed) | No (steel) | No (could merge) | **No** |
| 3 | Gusset-R | No (fixed) | No (steel) | No (could merge) | **No** |
| 4 | Vertical Pillar | Yes (rotates) | Yes (steel) | Yes (must articulate) | **Yes** |
| 5 | Flange | No (fixed) | No (steel) | No (could merge) | **No** |
| 6 | Bolt | Yes (slips) | Yes (high-tensile steel) | Yes (must clamp) | **Yes** |
| 7 | Washer | No | No (steel) | No (could merge with bolt head) | **No** |
| 8 | Nut-Locking | Yes (threads) | Yes | Yes (must clamp) | **Yes** |
| 9 | Plain Nut | Yes (threads) | Yes | Yes (must clamp) | **Yes** |

**Step 2:** Count the retained parts.

$$N_{m} \; = \; 1_{pillar} + 1_{bolt} + 1_{nut-lock} + 1_{nut-plain} \; = \; 4$$

**Step 3:** Compute the DFA Index.

$$I_{DFA} \; = \; \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100 \; = \; \frac{4}{9} \times 100 \; = \; 44.4 \;\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An $I_{DFA}$ of **44.4%** indicates **moderate design waste**. The bracket is *over-parted* by ~5 components. The engineer should explore *cast/forge* consolidation of the plate–gusset–flange sub-group into a single near-net-shape.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Assembly Cost for a Threaded Fastener Sub-Assembly

**Problem Statement:** A sub-assembly uses **6 M6 bolts** with **6 hex nuts** and **6 washers**. Manual labour rate is $r_L = \mathbf{0.50 \; ₹/s}$. Bolt insertion time is **6.0 s**, nut pickup is **2.0 s**, washer placement is **1.5 s**, final torque is **4.5 s**. Compute the *per-station* and *per-product* assembly cost for an annual volume of **$V_a = 50{,}000$ units**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute per-fastener time.

$$t_{bolt} + t_{washer} + t_{nut} + t_{torque} \; = \; 6.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 4.5 \; = \; 14.0 \; \text{s per fastener}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by the count per product.

$$t_{product} \; = \; 6 \times 14.0 \; = \; 84.0 \; \text{s per product}$$

**Step 3:** Compute per-product cost.

$$C_{product} \; = \; t_{product} \cdot r_{L} \; = \; 84.0 \times 0.50 \; = \; \mathbf{42.0 \; ₹}$$

**Step 4:** Compute annual cost.

$$C_{annual} \; = \; V_{a} \cdot C_{product} \; = \; 50{,}000 \times 42.0 \; = \; \mathbf{21{,}00{,}000 \; ₹}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with automated alternative ($r_L^{auto} = 0.10$ ₹/s, automation factor $\alpha = 0.25$).

$$t_{auto}^{bolt} \; = \; 14.0 \times 0.25 \; = \; 3.5 \; \text{s}$$
$$C_{auto}^{product} \; = \; 6 \times 3.5 \times 0.10 \; = \; 2.10 \; \text{₹}$$
$$C_{auto}^{annual} \; = \; 50{,}000 \times 2.10 \; = \; \mathbf{1{,}05{,}000 \; ₹}$$

**Step 6:** Capital break-even for automation (assume $C_{tool}^{auto} = \mathbf{1.5 \; crore}$).

$$\text{Payback} \; = \; \frac{1.5 \times 10^{7}}{(21{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}05{,}000)} \; \approx \; \mathbf{7.5 \; months}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At 50,000 units/year the **automation payback is under a year** — a textbook trigger to migrate this common assembly from manual to robotic workstations.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Snap-Fit Deflection & Stress Check

**Problem Statement:** A polypropylene (PP) snap-fit cantilever has length **L = 12 mm**, width **b = 4 mm**, thickness **h = 1.5 mm**. During engagement the mating part pushes with **F = 5 N**. Compute deflection and root stress.

Material: $E_{PP} = \mathbf{1.5 \; GPa}$, $\sigma_y = \mathbf{30 \; MPa}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert units to SI.

$$L \; = \; 12 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}, \quad b \; = \; 4 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}, \quad h \; = \; 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute second moment of area.

$$I \; = \; \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12} \; = \; \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I \; &= \; \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9}}{12} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{13.5 \times 10^{-12}}{12} \\[4pt]
&= \; 1.125 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m}^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute cantilever deflection.

$$\delta_{snap} \; = \; \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{snap} \; &= \; \frac{5 \times (12 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{3 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.125 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{5 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0625 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{8.64 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0625 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \; 1.707 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} \\[4pt]
&\approx \; \mathbf{1.71 \; mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute maximum stress at the root.

$$\sigma_{max} \; = \; \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} \; &= \; \frac{6 \times 5 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{0.36}{9.0 \times 10^{-9}} \\[4pt]
&= \; 4.0 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \\[4pt]
&= \; \mathbf{40 \; MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Apply yield-check.

$$\sigma_{max} = 40 \; \text{MPa} \; > \; \sigma_y = 30 \; \text{MPa} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{YIELD EXCEEDED}$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **The cantilever WILL plastically deform on first engagement.** The designer must either (a) increase thickness to **h = 1.75 mm**, (b) reduce length to **L = 10 mm**, or (c) switch material to **ABS (E = 2.0 GPa, σ_y = 45 MPa)**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Press-Fit Holding Force

**Problem Statement:** A steel shaft (diameter **$d_s = 30$ mm**) is pressed into an aluminium hub (inner diameter **$d_h = 30.00$ mm**, outer diameter **$d_o = 60$ mm**, contact length **$L_c = 25$ mm**). Diametrical interference **$\Delta = 0.04$ mm**. Compute contact pressure and axial pull-off force.

Material: $E_s = \mathbf{210 \; GPa}$, $E_h = \mathbf{70 \; GPa}$, $\mu = \mathbf{0.20}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the contact pressure denominator.

$$\begin{aligned}
D \; &= \; 2 \left[ \frac{d_s}{E_s} \cdot \frac{d_h^{2} + d_s^{2}}{d_s^{2} - d_h^{2}} + \frac{d_h}{E_h} \cdot \frac{d_o^{2} + d_h^{2}}{d_o^{2} - d_h^{2}} \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \left[ \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{210 \times 10^{9}} \cdot \frac{900 + 900}{900 - 900} \cdot (\text{undefined for rigid shaft}) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

For a **solid shaft** ($d_{s,inner} = 0$), the inner term simplifies using the classical Lamé solution:

$$\frac{d_h^{2} + d_s^{2}}{d_s^{2} - d_h^{2}} \;\rightarrow\; 1 \quad \text{(solid shaft limit)}$$

$$\frac{d_o^{2} + d_h^{2}}{d_o^{2} - d_h^{2}} \; = \; \frac{3600 + 900}{3600 - 900} \; = \; \frac{4500}{2700} \; = \; 1.667$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\begin{aligned}
D \; &= \; 2 \left[ \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{210 \times 10^{9}} \cdot 1 + \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{70 \times 10^{9}} \cdot 1.667 \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \left[ 1.429 \times 10^{-13} + 7.143 \times 10^{-13} \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \times 8.572 \times 10^{-13} \\[4pt]
&= \; 1.714 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute contact pressure.

$$p \; = \; \frac{\Delta}{D} \; = \; \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{1.714 \times 10^{-12}} \; = \; 2.33 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \; \approx \; \mathbf{23.3 \; MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Compute axial holding force.

$$F_{hold} \; = \; p \cdot \pi \cdot d_s \cdot L_c \cdot \mu$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{hold} \; &= \; 2.33 \times 10^{7} \times \pi \times 0.030 \times 0.025 \times 0.20 \\[4pt]
&= \; 2.33 \times 10^{7} \times 4.712 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= \; 1.097 \times 10^{4} \; \text{N} \\[4pt]
&\approx \; \mathbf{10.97 \; kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering comment.

> [!NOTE]
> The press-fit can transmit **~11 kN** axially before slip — a typical automotive wheel-hub class assembly. Designers add a **safety factor of 2 – 3** to accommodate fatigue micro-slip and thermal cycling.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Boothroyd DFA Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Product Concept]:::start
    stepB[Part List Generated]:::node
    stepC[Apply Movement Criterion]:::node
    stepD[Apply Material Criterion]:::node
    stepE[Apply Separation Criterion]:::node
    stepF[Compute Minimum Part Count Nm]:::node
    stepG[Compute DFA Index]:::node
    stepH[Index Less Than 60 Percent]:::gate
    stepI[Redesign for Part Reduction]:::node
    stepJ[Index At Least 60 Percent]:::endnode
    stepK[Final Validated Design]:::endnode

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI
    stepH -- No --> stepJ
    stepI --> stepB
    stepJ --> stepK

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef endnode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **iterative Boothroyd DFA loop**. The design is interrogated part-by-part, the index is computed, and if it falls below the **60% benchmark**, the design is sent back for *redesign*. The loop terminates only when the DFA index crosses the industrial acceptance threshold.


**Interpretation:** A common-assembly station is not just "fastener + wrench". It carries **five parallel cost streams** — material, handling, insertion, tooling, and inspection — that all sum to the *total assembly cost*. A DFMA engineer who ignores any one of these will under-quote the manufacturing cost and lose the order.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Conceptual Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any **three** common assembly families used in mass production and give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Assembly Family | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Threaded (Bolt–Nut) | Automotive wheel-to-hub fastening |
| 2 | Snap-fit | Mobile-phone battery cover |
| 3 | Press-fit | Ball-bearing on a gear-shaft |

*(1 mark per correct pair — award 3/3 only if all three are correctly paired with a real product.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the **DFA Index**. State the formula and explain the significance of a value below 30%.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The DFA Index is the ratio of minimum theoretical part count $N_m$ to actual part count $N_a$, expressed as a percentage.
- **Formula (1 mark):**
$$I_{DFA} \; = \; \frac{N_{m}}{N_{a}} \times 100 \; \text{(\%)}$$
- **Significance (1 mark):** A value below **30%** indicates **severe design over-partitioning** — at least 70% of parts are *candidates for consolidation*, leading to higher assembly time, higher tool costs, and reduced reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module internal choice means the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both questions must be attempted at revision; the exam-taker chooses one.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A bracket assembly has the following 10 parts:**

1. Top Plate (steel, fixed)
2. Bottom Plate (steel, fixed)
3. Left Web (steel, fixed)
4. Right Web (steel, fixed)
5. Central Pillar (steel, rotates ±15°)
6. Shaft (hardened steel, rotates continuously)
7. Bearing (separable, must be replaceable)
8. Bolt (must clamp the bearing)
9. Lock-nut (must clamp the bearing)
10. Cover Plate (sheet steel, must be removable for lubrication)

**(a)** Apply Boothroyd's three-criteria minimum-parts filter. Determine the **minimum number of parts** and the **DFA Index**. Comment on the design quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the assembly volume is **$V_a = 20{,}000$ units/year**, manual labour rate is **$r_L = 0.40$ ₹/s**, and the average insertion time per retained part is **$t_i = 5$ s**, compute the **per-product assembly cost** and the **annual assembly cost**. Suggest **two design modifications** to reduce cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Apply Boothroyd criteria (1 mark per correct evaluation).

| # | Part | Movement? | Material? | Separation? | Retain? |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Top Plate | No | No | No | **No** (merge) |
| 2 | Bottom Plate | No | No | No | **No** (merge) |
| 3 | Left Web | No | No | No | **No** (merge) |
| 4 | Right Web | No | No | No | **No** (merge) |
| 5 | Central Pillar | Yes (rotates) | No | No (rotation only) | **No** (merge with shaft) |
| 6 | Shaft | Yes (rotates) | Yes (hardened) | Yes (mating) | **Yes** |
| 7 | Bearing | No | Yes (lubricated) | Yes (replaceable) | **Yes** |
| 8 | Bolt | No | Yes (cl-tensile) | Yes (clamps) | **Yes** |
| 9 | Lock-nut | No | Yes (cl-tensile) | Yes (clamps) | **Yes** |
| 10 | Cover Plate | No | No (sheet steel) | Yes (lubrication access) | **Yes** |

**Step 2:** Count retained parts. **(2 marks)**

$$N_{m} \; = \; 1_{shaft} + 1_{bearing} + 1_{bolt} + 1_{locknut} + 1_{cover} \; = \; \mathbf{5 \; parts}$$

**Step 3:** Compute DFA Index. **(2 marks)**

$$I_{DFA} \; = \; \frac{5}{10} \times 100 \; = \; \mathbf{50 \; \%}$$

**Step 4:** Design quality comment. **(2 marks)**

> An $I_{DFA} = 50\%$ is **fair but not optimal**. The plate–web–pillar stack can be consolidated into a single cast/forge component. Post-modification, a target index of **80% or higher** is recommended.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Per-product time. **(2 marks)**

$$t_{product} \; = \; N_{m} \cdot t_{i} \; = \; 5 \times 5 \; = \; 25 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Per-product cost. **(2 marks)**

$$C_{product} \; = \; 25 \times 0.40 \; = \; \mathbf{10 \; ₹ \; per \; product}$$

**Step 3:** Annual cost. **(1 mark)**

$$C_{annual} \; = \; 20{,}000 \times 10 \; = \; \mathbf{2{,}00{,}000 \; ₹ \; per \; year}$$

**Step 4:** Two design modifications. **(2 marks — 1 each)**

1. **Consolidate plate + webs + pillar into a single casting**, reducing part count from 5 → 4 (saves ₹40,000/year).
2. **Replace bolt + lock-nut with a single self-locking nut**, reducing part count from 5 → 3 (saves additional ₹80,000/year).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A polypropylene snap-fit cantilever has **L = 10 mm, b = 3 mm, h = 1.2 mm**, and is loaded by **F = 4 N** during engagement. Material: **E = 1.5 GPa, σ_y = 30 MPa**. Calculate the **deflection**, **maximum root stress**, and verify whether the design is safe using a **factor of safety n = 1.5**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft of **$d_s = 25$ mm** is press-fit into an aluminium hub of **$d_h = 25.00$ mm, $d_o = 50$ mm, $L_c = 20$ mm**, with diametrical interference **$\Delta = 0.03$ mm**. Take **$E_s = 210$ GPa, $E_h = 70$ GPa, $\mu = 0.18$**. Compute the **contact pressure** and the **axial holding force**. Comment on the suitability for a transmission shaft application requiring **F_service = 6 kN**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Convert units. **(1 mark)**

$$L = 10 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}, \quad b = 3 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}, \quad h = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Second moment of area. **(1 mark)**

$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12} = 4.32 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 3:** Deflection. **(2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{snap} \; &= \; \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} \; = \; \frac{4 \times (10 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{3 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 4.32 \times 10^{-13}} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.944 \times 10^{-3}} \; = \; 2.058 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} \; \approx \; \mathbf{2.06 \; mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum root stress. **(2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} \; &= \; \frac{6 F L}{b h^{2}} \; = \; \frac{6 \times 4 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \; \frac{0.24}{4.32 \times 10^{-9}} \; = \; 5.556 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \; \approx \; \mathbf{55.6 \; MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Yield check with safety factor. **(1 mark)**

$$\sigma_{allow} \; = \; \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \; = \; \frac{30}{1.5} \; = \; 20 \; \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 55.6 \; \text{MPa} \; > \; \sigma_{allow} = 20 \; \text{MPa} \; \Rightarrow \; \textbf{UNSAFE}$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion. **(Bonus)**

> [!WARNING]
> The design **fails** by a factor of **2.78**. Recommended remedy — increase thickness to **h = 1.8 mm** (re-evaluates to **σ ≈ 24.7 MPa**, marginally safe) or reduce length to **L = 7 mm** (re-evaluates to **σ ≈ 19.5 MPa**, fully safe).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute the Lamé denominator (solid-shaft approximation). **(2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
D \; &= \; 2 \left[ \frac{d_s}{E_s} \cdot 1 + \frac{d_h}{E_h} \cdot \frac{d_o^{2} + d_h^{2}}{d_o^{2} - d_h^{2}} \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \left[ \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{210 \times 10^{9}} + \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{70 \times 10^{9}} \cdot \frac{2500 + 625}{2500 - 625} \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \left[ 1.190 \times 10^{-13} + 3.571 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 1.667 \right] \\[4pt]
&= \; 2 \left[ 1.190 \times 10^{-13} + 5.952 \times 10^{-13} \right] \; = \; 1.428 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Contact pressure. **(1 mark)**

$$p \; = \; \frac{\Delta}{D} \; = \; \frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{1.428 \times 10^{-12}} \; = \; 2.10 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \; \approx \; \mathbf{21.0 \; MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Axial holding force. **(2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{hold} \; &= \; p \cdot \pi \cdot d_s \cdot L_c \cdot \mu \\[4pt]
&= \; 2.10 \times 10^{7} \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 0.020 \times 0.18 \\[4pt]
&= \; 5.94 \times 10^{3} \; \text{N} \; \approx \; \mathbf{5.94 \; kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Service suitability. **(2 marks)**

$$F_{hold} = 5.94 \; \text{kN} \; < \; F_{service} = 6 \; \text{kN} \; \Rightarrow \; \textbf{MARGINALLY UNSAFE}$$

**Step 5:** Recommendation. **(Bonus)**

> [!WARNING]
> The joint can transmit only **5.94 kN**, short of the **6 kN** service demand. Increase interference to **Δ = 0.035 mm** (yields **F_hold ≈ 6.93 kN**, safe with **~15% margin**) OR extend contact length to **$L_c = 22$ mm** (yields **F_hold ≈ 6.53 kN**, marginally safe).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second-moment-of-area derivation** ($I = bh^3/12$) for snap-fits — at least **1 mark lost** if omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up $d_s$ and $d_h$ in the Lamé equation** for press-fits — at least **2 marks lost** plus incorrect answer.
> 3. **Not stating units explicitly** in DFA Index calculation — ½ mark deducted per missing unit.
> 4. **Skipping the design-comment portion** — examiners award up to **2 marks** for *engineering judgment* on whether the index is acceptable.
> 5. **Using $N_m / N_a$ instead of $N_m / N_a \times 100$** for the DFA Index — 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Bulletins for KTU 2024 ESE — Common Assemblies:**

- ✅ **Common Assembly** = a recurring, standardised joining operation classified by Boothroyd's handling/insertion taxonomy.
- ✅ **Seven canonical families**: Threaded, Snap-fit, Press-fit, Riveted, Welded, Adhesive, Soldered (plus Staking and Folding as sheet-metal variants).
- ✅ **Boothroyd's 3-criteria filter**: Movement, Material, Separation — parts failing all 3 are mergeable.
- ✅ **DFA Index formula** — must memorise $I_{DFA} = N_m / N_a \times 100$ in **percent**.
- ✅ **Acceptance benchmark** — $I_{DFA} \geq 60\%$ for industrial-grade DFMA; < 30% = poor.
- ✅ **Snap-fit theory** — $I = bh^3/12$, $\delta = FL^3 / (3EI)$, $\sigma = 6FL / bh^2$.
- ✅ **Press-fit Lamé equation** — applies only for *solid shaft* simplification when $d_{s,inner} = 0$.
- ✅ **DFA cost model** — sum of (handling + insertion) times per part, multiplied by labour rate and volume.
- ✅ **Automation trigger** — when annual labour saving exceeds amortised tooling cost within **12 months**, migrate to automation.
- ✅ **Reversible vs Permanent** — first binary classification expected by KTU examiners.
- ✅ **Selection flowchart** — disassembly need → material similarity → volume → sheet-metal? → thermal sensitivity → final selection.
- ✅ **Common mistakes** — forgetting units, misapplying Lamé, omitting the yield-check, neglecting factor-of-safety.
- ✅ **Real-world anchor examples** — Bolt-Nut (chassis), Snap-fit (phone cover), Press-fit (bearing), Rivet (aircraft skin), Weld (frame), Adhesive (composite panel), Solder (PCB).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Significance of Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Significance of Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Economic Significance of Assembly** refers to the quantitative and qualitative evaluation of how assembly-related decisions influence the total manufacturing cost, lead time, product quality, and overall life-cycle economics of a product. In the context of Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFM/DFA), it establishes that **assembly costs typically constitute 40% to 60% of total product cost** in discrete goods manufacturing, making it a primary driver of product profitability.

The economic analysis of assembly encompasses four pillars:

1. **Direct Assembly Cost (Ca)** – labor, machine time, consumables (fasteners, adhesives).
2. **Indirect Assembly Cost** – tooling amortization, fixturing, inspection, rework.
3. **Opportunity Cost** – throughput, inventory carrying, and time-to-market penalties.
4. **Life-Cycle Cost** – serviceability, disassembly for repair, and end-of-life recovery.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The IKEA Furniture Paradigm**
> Think of two identical chairs, both made of wood. *Chair A* requires 12 parts, 8 screws, 2 tools, and 25 minutes to assemble. *Chair B* uses 4 parts, no fasteners, and snaps together in 90 seconds.
> 
> - **The material cost** of Chair A and Chair B may differ by only ₹20.
> - **The assembly cost**, however, will differ by **₹200+** in labor alone at standard shop rates.
> - The **economic significance of assembly** is precisely this gap: a designer's choice of how parts fit together often saves more money than the choice of what raw material to use.

## 1.3 Standard Economic Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Assembly cost share (Ca / Ctotal) | **40%–60%** | Dominant cost contributor |
| Labor cost share of assembly | **50%–80%** | Manual labor intensive |
| Typical insertion time | **1.5 s to 10 s** | Per fastener operation |
| DFA Efficiency target | **≥ 70%** | Good design practice |
| Cost of a single screw insertion | **₹1.5 to ₹8** | Including tool + labor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The phrase "**assembly is the largest controllable cost in a product**" is a direct quote from the Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA methodology. KTU examiners award marks for using the exact terminology of this classical framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework – Why Assembly Economics Matters

The product's total cost can be expressed as:

$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{fabrication} + C_{assembly} + C_{overhead} + C_{rework} + C_{warranty}
$$

In high-volume discrete manufacturing (automotive, consumer electronics, appliances), the dominant variables are **C_assembly** and **C_warranty**. Design decisions made in the conceptual stage affect these costs disproportionately because of the **multiplier effect** of production volume.

### 2.1.1 The Economic Multiplier Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every **1 second** saved per assembly operation, when multiplied across **1 million units** at a shop rate of **₹2/second**, yields a saving of **₹2 crore (₹20 million)**. This is why assembly economic analysis is non-negotiable in mass production.

## 2.2 Core DFA Economic Formulas (KTU High-Yield Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} (t_{a,i} \cdot R + C_{fastener,i})$ | Total assembly cost = sum over all parts of (time × rate) + fastener cost |
| 2 | $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | DFA efficiency based on part count reduction |
| 3 | $\eta_{time} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}}{t_{ea}} \times 100\%$ | Time-based DFA efficiency |
| 4 | $DFA\ Deficit = t_{ea} - (N_{min} \cdot t_{ma})$ | Time wasted beyond theoretical minimum |
| 5 | $C_{saving} = (C_{old} - C_{new}) \cdot V - C_{tooling}$ | Net savings = (unit saving × volume) − tooling investment |
| 6 | $Payback\ Period = \dfrac{C_{tooling}}{(C_{old} - C_{new}) \cdot V}$ | Time (in batches) to recover tooling cost |
| 7 | $C_{per\ part} = C_{mat} + C_{fab} + C_{ass} + C_{OH}$ | Per-part landed cost |
| 8 | $V_{break\ even} = \dfrac{C_{tooling}}{(C_{old} - C_{new})}$ | Minimum volume to justify redesign |
| 9 | $Throughput = \dfrac{T_{available}}{t_{ea}}$ | Units assembled per unit time |
| 10 | $C_{quality\ loss} = p_r \cdot C_{rework} + (1-p_r)\cdot C_{failure}$ | Expected quality cost (p_r = rework probability) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key** – N_a: actual number of parts; N_min: theoretical minimum parts; t_ma: minimum time per part (Boothroyd standard ≈ 1.5 s); t_ea: estimated actual assembly time; R: shop rate (₹/sec); V: annual volume.

## 2.3 Why the Engineering Utility Matters

1. **Decision-making at design stage** – determines whether to invest in dedicated assembly fixtures or rely on self-locating features.
2. **Make-vs-buy analysis** – assembly cost differential dictates outsourcing decisions.
3. **DFM/DFA trade-off** – a part that is expensive to manufacture but cheap to assemble may still be economically superior if volume is high.
4. **Robotics justification** – high-volume, low-mix products justify automation only when assembly time savings × volume exceeds the capital cost.

## 2.4 The Three Economic Tests for Part Elimination (Boothroyd Criterion)

A part may be eliminated only if it fails **at least two** of these tests:

1. **Relative motion** – Does the part move relative to all other parts during operation? (If no → candidate for elimination.)
2. **Separate material** – Is the part made of a different material than all adjacent parts? (If no → candidate for elimination.)
3. **Disassembly requirement** – Must the part be separated for servicing? (If no → candidate for elimination.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU short-answer question** (3 marks). Memorize the exact phrasing: *"During operation, the part does not move, is not of different material, and does not need to be disassembled."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Assembly Cost (Ca) – From First Principles

**Problem Setup:**
A product has N_a parts. Each part i requires:
- Handling time t_h (≈ 0.5 s)
- Insertion time t_i
- Fastening time t_f (if applicable)
- Tool engagement time t_tool

**Step 1:** Express time per part.

$$
t_{a,i} = t_{h} + t_{i} + t_{f,i} + t_{tool,i}
$$

**Step 2:** Express cost per part using shop rate R (₹/s) and fastener cost C_f.

$$
c_{a,i} = t_{a,i} \cdot R + C_{f,i}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all parts.

$$
C_a = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} \left[ t_{a,i} \cdot R + C_{f,i} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Expand and group for analysis.

$$
C_a = R \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_{a,i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} C_{f,i}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The first term is *labor-driven* (reducible by DFA), while the second is *consumable-driven* (reducible by material standardization).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DFA Efficiency (Time-Based, Boothroyd)

**Step 1:** Define the theoretical minimum assembly time.

$$
t_{ma} = N_{min} \cdot t_{ideal}
$$

where t_ideal ≈ 1.5 s (Boothroyd empirical standard for a self-locating, self-fastening part).

**Step 2:** Define the actual estimated assembly time.

$$
t_{ea} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} t_{a,i}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio (efficiency).

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}}{t_{ea}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** Express the DFA Deficit (wasted time).

$$
\Delta t = t_{ea} - N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert time deficit to monetary deficit for annual volume V.

$$
C_{waste,annual} = \Delta t \cdot R \cdot V
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
A product has N_a = 24 parts, theoretical minimum N_min = 9, t_ideal = 1.8 s, actual t_ea = 72 s, R = ₹3/s, V = 5,00,000 units/yr.

Step A: $N_{min} \cdot t_{ma} = 9 \times 1.8 = 16.2\ \text{s}$

Step B: $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{16.2}{72} \times 100 = 22.5\%$ (poor design)

Step C: $\Delta t = 72 - 16.2 = 55.8\ \text{s}$

Step D: $C_{waste,annual} = 55.8 \times 3 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}8.37\ \text{crore}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often pose a question asking: *"Calculate the DFA deficit and the annual monetary loss."* The four-step derivation above is the expected model answer structure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Make-vs-Buy Break-Even Volume

**Step 1:** Define unit cost of in-house assembly (A_in) and outsourced cost (A_out).

$$
A_{in} = C_{mat} + C_{fab} + C_{ass,manual}
$$

$$
A_{out} = C_{mat} + C_{fab} + C_{ass,outsourced}
$$

**Step 2:** Define fixed cost of setting up in-house assembly line.

$$
C_{setup} = C_{tooling} + C_{fixture} + C_{training}
$$

**Step 3:** Total cost in-house = variable + fixed.

$$
C_{in,total} = A_{in} \cdot V + C_{setup}
$$

**Step 4:** Total cost outsourced (no fixed cost).

$$
C_{out,total} = A_{out} \cdot V
$$

**Step 5:** Break-even where the two are equal.

$$
A_{in} \cdot V_{be} + C_{setup} = A_{out} \cdot V_{be}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for V_be.

$$
V_{be} = \dfrac{C_{setup}}{A_{out} - A_{in}}
$$

**Interpretation:** If A_out < A_in, outsourcing is always cheaper. If A_out > A_in, in-house is justified only when projected volume exceeds V_be.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Economic Levers in Assembly Design

| Design Lever | Cost Affected | Typical Saving | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part consolidation | C_a, C_mat, C_fab | **20%–40%** | Reduced serviceability |
| Snap-fit vs screw | C_a, C_f | **50%–70%** | Lower joint strength |
| Modular sub-assembly | C_a, C_logistics | **15%–25%** | Increased part count |
| Self-locating features | t_i, C_rework | **30%–50%** | Tolerance stack-up |
| Standardized fasteners | C_inventory, C_tool | **10%–20%** | Limited joint types |
| Robotization | C_labor | **60%–80%** | High fixed cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Composition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Total Product Cost C_total"] --> B["Material Cost C_mat"]
    A --> C["Fabrication Cost C_fab"]
    A --> D["Assembly Cost C_a"]
    A --> E["Overhead C_oh"]
    A --> F["Quality and Warranty C_q"]
    D --> D1["Direct Labor t_a x R"]
    D --> D2["Fasteners and Consumables C_f"]
    D --> D3["Tooling Amortization C_tool"]
    D --> D4["Rework and Inspection C_rwk"]
    D1 --> D1A["Handling t_h"]
    D1 --> D1B["Insertion t_i"]
    D1 --> D1C["Fastening t_f"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 DFA Economic Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Start: New Product Concept"] --> P2["List all parts N_a"]
    P2 --> P3{"Apply 3 Boothroyd Tests"}
    P3 -- "Fails 2 of 3" --> P4["Candidate for elimination"]
    P3 -- "Passes 2 of 3" --> P5["Retain part"]
    P4 --> P6["Combine with adjacent part"]
    P5 --> P7["Compute t_ea for retained parts"]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8["Compute DFA Efficiency eta"]
    P8 --> P9{"eta is greater than 70 percent?"}
    P9 -- "Yes" --> P10["Acceptable DFA design"]
    P9 -- "No" --> P11["Redesign fastening method"]
    P11 --> P7
    P10 --> P12["Compute Annual Cost Saving"]
    P12 --> P13["Compare with Tooling Investment"]
    P13 --> P14{"Payback is less than 2 years?"}
    P14 -- "Yes" --> P15["Approve Design Change"]
    P14 -- "No" --> P16["Reject Change"]
    style P1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P10 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P15 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
