    style P16 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Assembly Cost Pareto Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["100 Percent of Assembly Cost"] --> Y1["60 Percent: Manual Insertion and Fastening"]
    X --> Y2["20 Percent: Handling and Positioning"]
    X --> Y3["10 Percent: Inspection and Rework"]
    X --> Y4["7 Percent: Tool Changeover"]
    X --> Y5["3 Percent: Consumables and Misc"]
    Y1 --> Z1["Target: Snap fits, modular sub-assemblies"]
    Y2 --> Z2["Target: Self-locating features, gravity feeders"]
    Y3 --> Z3["Target: Poka-yoke, fool-proofing"]
    Y4 --> Z4["Target: SMED, common tool families"]
    Y5 --> Z5["Target: Bulk procurement, standardization"]
    style X fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Y2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Y3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**"List any three Boothroyd criteria used to decide whether a part is necessary in a product assembly."**

**Model Answer:**
The three Boothroyd criteria for part necessity are:
1. **Relative Motion Criterion** – The part must move relative to all other parts during product operation.
2. **Material Criterion** – The part must be made of a different material than all adjacent parts.
3. **Disassembly Criterion** – The part must be separable from adjacent parts for servicing or maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A part may be eliminated if it fails at least two of these three tests. This 2-of-3 rule is the standard KTU accepted answer.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]
**"Define DFA efficiency. What is the recommended target value for a well-designed product?"**

**Model Answer:**
DFA efficiency is the ratio of the theoretical minimum assembly time to the actual estimated assembly time, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}}{t_{ea}} \times 100\%
$$

The recommended target value for a well-designed product using Boothroyd methodology is **70% or higher**. A value below 40% indicates a poorly designed product with excessive part count and fastening operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** As per the 2024 scheme, Module-end questions in ESE carry 14 marks, typically split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]

**A product has 32 parts. The theoretical minimum number of parts is 8. The standard minimum time per part is 2 seconds. The actual estimated assembly time is 96 seconds. The shop rate is ₹2.5 per second and annual production volume is 2,00,000 units.**

**(a)** Calculate the DFA efficiency and the DFA time deficit. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If a redesign reduces the part count to 20 and the assembly time to 50 seconds, calculate the new DFA efficiency, the annual monetary saving, and the payback period for a tooling investment of ₹15 lakhs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
N_a = 32, N_min = 8, t_ma = 2 s, t_ea = 96 s.

**Step 2:** Calculate theoretical minimum time.

$$
t_{theoretical} = N_{min} \cdot t_{ma} = 8 \times 2 = 16\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate DFA efficiency.

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{16}{96} \times 100 = 16.67\%
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate DFA time deficit.

$$
\Delta t = 96 - 16 = 80\ \text{s per assembly}
$$

> This is a **very poor DFA design** (target ≥ 70%).

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1:** New design parameters.
N_a_new = 20, t_ea_new = 50 s, t_ma = 2 s, N_min = 8.

**Step 2:** New DFA efficiency.

$$
\eta_{new} = \dfrac{16}{50} \times 100 = 32\%
$$

**Step 3:** Time saving per assembly.

$$
\Delta t_{saving} = 96 - 50 = 46\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Monetary saving per assembly.

$$
S_{unit} = 46 \times 2.5 = \text{₹}115\ \text{per unit}
$$

**Step 5:** Annual monetary saving.

$$
S_{annual} = 115 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}2.30\ \text{crore}
$$

**Step 6:** Payback period.

$$
Payback = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{2{,}30{,}00{,}000} \times 12 = 0.78\ \text{months}
$$

> The redesign pays back the tooling investment in **less than 1 month**, making it highly economically attractive.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Actual]

**(a)** Explain the economic significance of assembly in product design with reference to cost breakdown. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A manufacturer has two options: (i) In-house assembly at ₹180 per unit with a setup cost of ₹25 lakhs, (ii) Outsourced assembly at ₹220 per unit with zero setup cost. Determine the break-even volume and recommend the better option for an annual demand of 50,000 units. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Economic Significance – Key Points:**

1. **Cost Dominance:** Assembly accounts for 40%–60% of total product cost in discrete manufacturing. In automotive and electronics, this can rise to 70%.
2. **Multiplier Effect:** Small per-unit savings become large at high volumes (1 second × 1 million units × ₹2/s = ₹2 crore).
3. **Quality Coupling:** Poor assembly design directly increases warranty cost, rework cost, and field failure cost.
4. **Time-to-Market:** Efficient assembly shortens cycle time and reduces work-in-process inventory.
5. **Serviceability Cost:** Design choices that complicate disassembly increase life-cycle cost of ownership.

**Cost Breakdown (per typical KTU framework):**

$$
C_{product} = C_{material} + C_{fabrication} + C_{assembly} + C_{overhead} + C_{quality}
$$

In high-volume products, C_assembly > C_fabrication, reversing the traditional engineer's bias toward manufacturing.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1:** Identify variables.
A_in = ₹180, A_out = ₹220, C_setup = ₹25,00,000, V = 50,000.

**Step 2:** Apply break-even formula.

$$
V_{be} = \dfrac{C_{setup}}{A_{out} - A_{in}} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{220 - 180} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{40} = 62{,}500\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with annual demand.

Since V = 50,000 < V_be = 62,500, **in-house assembly is NOT justified** at this volume.

**Step 4:** Verify by total cost comparison.

$$
C_{in,total} = 180 \times 50{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}11.50\ \text{crore}
$$

$$
C_{out,total} = 220 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹}11.00\ \text{crore}
$$

**Step 5:** Recommendation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation: Outsource the assembly** because the total cost is ₹50 lakhs lower (₹11.00 cr vs ₹11.50 cr). However, if projected demand grows to 70,000 units or more, the company should switch to in-house assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch error:** Students often confuse *seconds* with *minutes* in the shop rate R, leading to answers off by a factor of 60. Always verify: *R is in ₹/s if time is in seconds.*
> 2. **Break-even inversion error:** When A_in < A_out (in-house is cheaper per unit), students incorrectly set V_be = C_setup / (A_in − A_out) and get a negative value. Always use V_be = C_setup / (A_out − A_in).
> 3. **Ignoring tooling cost:** In payback period calculations, students often forget to subtract the tooling investment. The correct net annual saving is (S_annual − C_tooling_amortized).
> 4. **N_a vs N_min confusion:** DFA efficiency uses N_min in the numerator, NOT N_a. Confusing these will lose all 7 marks of part (a).
> 5. **Units in final answer:** Always express monetary savings in **₹ lakhs or ₹ crores**, not raw ₹, for readability. Examiners reward clean, professional presentation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Recall Before Exam):**

- ☐ **Assembly cost share** of total product cost = **40%–60%** (Boothroyd benchmark).
- ☐ **Three Boothroyd tests** for part elimination: *Motion, Material, Disassembly.* Part eliminated if it fails **2 of 3**.
- ☐ **DFA Efficiency formula:** $\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}}{t_{ea}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ **Target DFA efficiency** = **≥ 70%**; values < 40% indicate poor design.
- ☐ **Standard minimum time per part** (t_ma) = **1.5 s to 2 s** (Boothroyd empirical standard for self-locating, self-fastening parts).
- ☐ **Total assembly cost** = $\sum (t_a \cdot R) + \sum C_{fastener}$ for every part.
- ☐ **Break-even volume for make-vs-buy** = $C_{setup} / (A_{out} - A_{in})$.
- ☐ **Payback period** in months = $\dfrac{C_{tooling} \times 12}{S_{annual}}$.
- ☐ **Multiplier effect:** *Every second saved × volume × rate = annual monetary saving.* Always state this in numerical problems.
- ☐ **Snap-fits** reduce assembly cost by **50%–70%** vs screws; trade-off is lower joint strength.
- ☐ **DFA Deficit (time)** = $t_{ea} - N_{min} \cdot t_{ma}$ — directly convertible to ₹/year by multiplying with R × V.
- ☐ **Pareto principle in assembly:** *60% of cost = manual insertion and fastening*; this is the primary target of DFA intervention.
- ☐ **Direct labor cost share** of assembly = **50%–80%** — justifies automation at high volumes.
- ☐ **Make-vs-buy rule of thumb:** *In-house only if A_in < A_out AND volume > break-even.* Otherwise outsource.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Taxonomies of Assembly Operations and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Taxonomies of Assembly Operations and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)** as prescribed under the KTU 2024 scheme, the term **"Assembly"** is rigorously defined as the systematic process of joining two or more discrete components, subassemblies, or modules through a defined sequence of physical, mechanical, thermal, or chemical operations, in order to produce a functional end-product that satisfies the design intent.

A **"General Taxonomy of Assembly Operations"** refers to a structured, hierarchical classification scheme that categorizes all possible assembly activities into well-defined families based on the nature of the operation, the direction of force application, the type of joint created, the energy domain involved, and the level of automation. A **"Taxonomy of Assembly Systems"** similarly classifies the physical and logical arrangement of workstations, handling mechanisms, fixtures, tooling, and human operators required to execute the assembly process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The official DFMA course code PEPET755 (Elective V) treats assembly taxonomies as the foundational logic layer for the Boothroyd-Dewhurst method of Design for Assembly (DFA) analysis. Without a robust taxonomy, DFA indices such as the **Handling Index (H)** and **Insertion Index (I)** cannot be computed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a large modular kitchen. The kitchen consists of cabinets, drawers, a sink, a chimney, and electrical fittings. Before you install them, you naturally group them:

- **By "What they do"** — cooking appliances, storage units, water systems.
- **By "How they are joined"** — screwed to the wall, snap-fit, glued, plugged-in.
- **By "Who installs them"** — you (manual), or a powered drill/rivet gun (machine-aided), or a fully robotic arm (automated).

This three-way grouping is exactly what an **Assembly Taxonomy** does. The first grouping classifies **operations**, the second classifies **joining methods**, and the third classifies **systems**. Just as a chef classifies recipes into starters, mains, and desserts to organize a vast menu, an engineer classifies assembly steps into a taxonomy to bring order to the chaos of thousands of possible joining actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Taxonomies Matter in KTU Examinations:** Questions on taxonomies are not just memory recall — they test whether a student can map a real-world product (e.g., a car door, a printed circuit board) onto the correct assembly family, which in turn determines the correct DFA index calculation. Examiner valuation keys explicitly award marks for *correct taxonomy identification*.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Process Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are **bolded** because they appear verbatim in KTU valuation schemes:

- **Handling Code (H)** — dimensionless integer from **0 to 9** quantifying part orientability.
- **Insertion Code (I)** — dimensionless integer quantifying the difficulty of joining.
- **Number of Parts (n)** — fundamental count of distinct components.
- **Cycle Time (T_c)** — measured in **seconds (s)** per KTU design problem conventions.
- **Number of Workstations (N_ws)** — dimensionless integer.
- **Line Efficiency (η)** — dimensionless ratio bounded in **0 ≤ η ≤ 1**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Assembly Operations (Boothroyd's Taxonomy)

Geoffrey Boothroyd and Peter Dewhurst, in their seminal DFMA work, classified every conceivable assembly action into **four master families**. This is the most frequently tested taxonomy in KTU ESE papers.

- **Type 1 — Permanent Fastening:** Operations that create a non-separable joint, such as riveting, welding, soldering, brazing, and adhesive bonding. Removal destroys the part.
- **Type 2 — Temporary Fastening:** Operations that create a separable joint using threaded fasteners (screws, bolts, nuts), snap-fits, or spring clips. Disassembly is possible without damage.
- **Type 3 — Insertion / Press-Fit:** Operations where a male feature is elastically or plastically deformed into a female feature, such as bearing press, pin insertion, or shaft-hub assembly.
- **Type 4 — Manipulation / Forming:** Operations that alter the part's geometry to lock it in place, such as peening, staking, crimping, or ultrasonic welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Permanent, Temporary, Insertion, Manipulation → **"PT-IM"** (Pay Taxes, Insert Money).

### 2.2 Classification by Direction of Force

Every assembly action requires a relative motion between the joining pair. The taxonomy by direction is:

- **Axial (Vertical) Insertion** — force along the principal axis (e.g., pushing a pin into a hole).
- **Radial (Lateral) Insertion** — force perpendicular to the principal axis (e.g., snapping a tab sideways into a slot).
- **Overhead Assembly** — joining performed against gravity (e.g., bolting a ceiling fixture).
- **Multi-axis Assembly** — combined motions, e.g., screw-driving that requires both rotation and axial thrust.

### 2.3 Classification of Assembly Systems

Assembly systems are categorized along three orthogonal axes:

- **By Workstation Topology:**
  - *Single-Station Assembly* — one operator/robot performs the entire product build.
  - *Multi-Station Assembly Line* — the work is distributed across N sequential stations.
  - *Cellular Assembly* — a U-shaped or clustered group of stations builds a product family.
- **By Level of Automation:**
  - *Manual Assembly* — entirely human-driven, dexterity-limited, but flexible.
  - *Hybrid (Mechatronic) Assembly* — humans perform orientation, machines perform insertion.
  - *Fully Automated Assembly* — robotic cells with vision and force feedback.
- **By Material Flow Pattern:**
  - *In-line (Synchronous) Flow* — pallet moves at fixed cycle time T_c.
  - *Asynchronous Flow* — free movement of carriers (e.g., AGVs).
  - *Fixed-Position Assembly* — the product stays still (e.g., shipbuilding, aircraft).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below is the **complete formula kit** for this module. All symbols are KTU-conformant.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Handling Index (H) | $H = H_{\text{orient}} + H_{\text{grasp}}$ | $0 \le H \le 9$, dimensionless | Quantifies part orientability for robot pickup. |
| 2 | Insertion Index (I) | $I = I_{\text{align}} + I_{\text{fasten}}$ | $0 \le I \le 9$, dimensionless | Quantifies difficulty of joining step. |
| 3 | DFA Total Index | $T = H + I$ | dimensionless | Sum index for a single assembly operation. |
| 4 | DFA Efficiency Metric | $E_{DFA} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_m}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \le E_{DFA} \le 1$ | Boothroyd's DFA efficiency ratio. |
| 5 | Cycle Time | $T_c = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D}$ | seconds | Time allowed per station. |
| 6 | Theoretical Min. Stations | $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{T_c} \right\rceil$ | stations (integer) | Lower bound on workstation count. |
| 7 | Line Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{ws} \cdot T_c}$ | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ | Measures assembly line balance quality. |
| 8 | Balance Delay | $B_d = 1 - \eta$ | $0 \le B_d \le 1$ | Fraction of time lost to imbalance. |
| 9 | Lead Time (sequential) | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | seconds | Total build time on a single station. |
| 10 | Throughput | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | parts/second | Production rate of the line. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** In KTU answer scripts, the absolute value of any variable $\vert x \vert$ is **not** to be written using the pipe symbol inside math tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert_{\text{LaTeX-escaped}}$ as shown above to avoid rendering errors.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sector:** A modern car contains ~3,000 fasteners. Classifying them as Type 1/2/3 allows Ford and Toyota to convert 40–60% of Type 2 (threaded) fasteners into Type 3 (snap-fits), reducing assembly time by 25%.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 fuselage sections are joined using Type 1 (automated rivets) on a multi-station synchronous line where $\eta > 0.85$ is mandatory.
- **Electronics (PCB):** Surface-mount technology uses Type 3 insertion (pick-and-place heads), and the Handling Index H is critical for vision-system calibration.
- **Medical Devices:** Catheter assembly is overwhelmingly manual (Type 2) due to part flexibility and miniaturization, demanding ergonomic analysis of operator posture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical Minimum Number of Workstations

The bound $N_{\min}$ arises from a fundamental physical constraint: **the total work content of the product cannot be compressed below the available time per station.**

Let:
- $T_{\text{available}}$ = total daily production time in seconds.
- $D$ = daily demand in parts.
- $t_i$ = standard time of the $i^{\text{th}}$ assembly task, in seconds.
- $n$ = total number of tasks in the precedence network.

**Step 1 — Define Cycle Time.**
The cycle time is the maximum time allowed at any single workstation:

$$
T_c = \frac{T_{\text{available}}}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Total Work Content.**
Sum all elementary task times along the longest open chain:

$$
W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Lower Bound Principle.**
In the best possible (utopian) case, every workstation is busy for exactly $T_c$ seconds. The number of stations required is the total work divided by cycle time:

$$
N_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{W}{T_c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Integer Ceiling Operator.**
Since a fractional workstation is physically meaningless, we round up:

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil N_{\text{ideal}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c} \right\rceil
$$

This is **Step 4** of the KTU valuation scheme, where students typically lose marks by forgetting the ceiling function and writing $N_{\min} = W / T_c$ without the brackets.

### 3.2 Derivation of Line Efficiency

Building on $N_{\min}$, the **actual** number of stations $N_{ws}$ will always be greater than or equal to $N_{\min}$ because of precedence constraints and indivisible tasks.

**Step 1 — Compute the Idealized Productive Time.**
If the line were perfectly balanced, the productive time per day would be $N_{ws} \cdot T_c$ across all stations.

**Step 2 — Compare to Actual Work Content.**
The actual work done per cycle is $\sum t_i = W$. The line efficiency is the ratio of useful work to available station-time:

$$
\eta = \frac{W}{N_{ws} \cdot T_c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{N_{ws} \cdot T_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of $N_{\min}$.**
Substituting $T_c = W / N_{\min}$ into the equation above:

$$
\eta = \frac{W}{N_{ws} \cdot \left(W / N_{\min}\right)} = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_{ws}}
$$

Thus, $\eta$ is a direct ratio of theoretical to actual stations, a beautifully symmetric result.

**Step 4 — Bound the Result.**
By definition, $N_{ws} \ge N_{\min}$, so:

$$
0 \le \eta = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_{ws}} \le 1
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping a Real Product to a Taxonomy

The matrix below is a **valuation-ready** classification tool. Students can replicate this in KTU 14-mark answers.

| Component | Joining Method | Operation Type (Boothroyd) | Direction | System Type | H | I |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engine block to chassis | Bolts (M12) | Type 2 (Temporary) | Axial (overhead) | Multi-station line | 3 | 4 |
| Car door trim panel | Plastic clips | Type 2 (Temporary) | Radial | Manual | 1 | 1 |
| Battery terminal | Soldered lug | Type 1 (Permanent) | Axial | Hybrid | 2 | 3 |
| Ball bearing on shaft | Press-fit | Type 3 (Insertion) | Axial | Automated cell | 1 | 2 |
| PCB resistor on board | Wave soldered | Type 1 (Permanent) | Multi-axis | Fully automated | 2 | 2 |
| Watch crystal to case | Snap-fit | Type 2 (Temporary) | Axial | Manual / micro-assembly | 1 | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU Valuation:** Notice that as automation level rises, the H-index typically **decreases** (machines do not need human-like grip), but the I-index can be **higher** because tolerances become tighter. This is a classic 14-mark question stem.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A product requires $n = 8$ tasks with times $t = \{40, 30, 25, 50, 35, 20, 45, 30\}$ seconds. Daily available time is 28,800 s, and demand is 400 units/day. Compute $T_c$, $N_{\min}$, and $\eta$ if $N_{ws} = 7$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Work Content.**

$$
W = \sum_{i=1}^{8} t_i = 40 + 30 + 25 + 50 + 35 + 20 + 45 + 30 = 275 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Cycle Time.**

$$
T_c = \frac{28{,}800 \text{ s}}{400} = 72 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Theoretical Minimum Number of Stations.**

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{275}{72} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.819 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ stations}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Line Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_{ws}} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714 = 57.14\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Balance Delay.**

$$
B_d = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.5714 = 0.4286 = 42.86\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Roughly **42.86%** of available station-time is wasted due to imbalance. This is below the industry target of $\eta \ge 0.85$ and would trigger a re-balancing effort in a real KTU capstone project.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Assembly Operations (Boothroyd's PT-IM)

The figure below is a complete **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping every assembly operation into the four master Boothroyd families, with sub-classifications branched from each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ASSEMBLY OPERATIONS"] --> B["TYPE 1 PERMANENT FASTENING"]
    A --> C["TYPE 2 TEMPORARY FASTENING"]
    A --> D["TYPE 3 INSERTION PRESS FIT"]
    A --> E["TYPE 4 MANIPULATION FORMING"]

    B --> B1["Riveting"]
    B --> B2["Welding MIG TIG Spot"]
    B --> B3["Soldering Brazing"]
    B --> B4["Adhesive Bonding"]

    C --> C1["Threaded Bolts Screws Nuts"]
    C --> C2["Snap Fits Cantilever Torsional"]
    C --> C3["Spring Clips Retaining Rings"]
    C --> C4["Quick Release Fasteners"]

    D --> D1["Pin in Hole"]
    D --> D2["Shaft Hub Press Fit"]
    D --> D3["Bearing Assembly"]
    D --> D4["Bushing Insertion"]

    E --> E1["Staking"]
    E --> E2["Peening"]
    E --> E3["Crimping"]
    E --> E4["Ultrasonic Welding"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style C fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style D fill:#ed6c02,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style E fill:#5e35b1,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

### 4.2 Classification of Assembly Systems

The diagram below is a **three-axis decision tree** that classifies assembly systems by topology, automation, and flow pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["ASSEMBLY SYSTEMS"] --> S1["BY TOPOLOGY"]
    S --> S2["BY AUTOMATION"]
    S --> S3["BY MATERIAL FLOW"]

    S1 --> S1A["Single Station"]
    S1 --> S1B["Multi Station Line"]
    S1 --> S1C["Cellular U Shape"]

    S2 --> S2A["Manual"]
    S2 --> S2B["Hybrid Mechatronic"]
    S2 --> S2C["Fully Automated Robotic"]

    S3 --> S3A["Inline Synchronous"]
    S3 --> S3B["Asynchronous AGV"]
    S3 --> S3C["Fixed Position Large Product"]

    style S fill:#0b5394,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style S1 fill:#3d85c6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style S2 fill:#6fa8dc,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    style S3 fill:#9fc5e8,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mapping a Product to Its Taxonomy — Functional Flow

The functional architecture below shows how a generic product (e.g., a household appliance) is routed through the taxonomy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PRODUCT DEFINITION"] --> Q["IDENTIFY ALL JOINING STEPS"]
    Q --> R{"JOIN IS SEPARABLE?"}
    R -- NO --> R1["TYPE 1 PERMANENT"]
    R -- YES --> R2{"FASTENER TYPE?"}
    R2 -- THREADED --> R3["TYPE 2 TEMPORARY A"]
    R2 -- SNAP CLIP --> R4["TYPE 2 TEMPORARY B"]
    R2 -- PRESS FIT --> R5["TYPE 3 INSERTION"]
    R2 -- DEFORM --> R6["TYPE 4 MANIPULATION"]

    R1 --> Z["COMPUTE H AND I INDEX"]
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    R6 --> Z
    Z --> Y["DFA EFFICIENCY METRIC"]
    Y --> OUT["DESIGN IMPROVEMENT DECISION"]

    style P fill:#1a237e,color:#ffffff
    style Z fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style Y fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#f57f17,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPET755]**
*Define the term "Assembly Taxonomy" as used in Design for Assembly. State the four master operation families proposed by Boothroyd and Dewhurst.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An assembly taxonomy is a systematic hierarchical classification of all possible assembly operations and systems into well-defined families based on the nature of joining, the type of joint produced, and the energy/motion domain involved. (1.5 Marks)
Boothroyd and Dewhurst proposed four master operation families: (1) **Type 1 – Permanent Fastening** (riveting, welding, adhesive bonding), (2) **Type 2 – Temporary Fastening** (threaded fasteners, snap-fits), (3) **Type 3 – Insertion/Press-Fit** (bearing press, pin in hole), and (4) **Type 4 – Manipulation/Forming** (staking, crimping, peening). (1.5 Marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between "in-line synchronous flow" and "asynchronous flow" in assembly systems. Give one engineering example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In **in-line synchronous flow**, the work carrier (pallet, conveyor) moves at a fixed, predetermined cycle time $T_c$ regardless of whether the workstation is ready; all stations are paced by the same clock. Example: a Toyota automotive final assembly line. (1.5 Marks)
In **asynchronous flow**, parts move freely between stations as soon as the upstream task is complete, often using AGVs or autonomous carts. Example: a flexible electronics assembly cell where printed circuit boards move between pick-and-place machines at variable rates. (1.5 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Boothroyd-Dewhurst taxonomy of assembly operations in detail. For each of the four operation types, give **one industrial example** and the **corresponding DFA index interpretation**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An assembly line has 10 tasks with standard times $t = \{45, 30, 25, 60, 40, 35, 50, 30, 45, 40\}$ seconds. The line operates 8 hours/day with one shift and must produce 360 units/day. Compute: (i) cycle time, (ii) theoretical minimum number of workstations, (iii) actual number of stations and line efficiency if $N_{ws} = 6$, and (iv) balance delay. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
- Type 1 (Permanent) — Example: spot-welding a car body panel. DFA index: high I (≈6) due to thermal distortion risk. (1.75 M)
- Type 2 (Temporary) — Example: a screw joining a laptop housing. DFA index: moderate I (≈3), requires tool access. (1.75 M)
- Type 3 (Insertion) — Example: pressing a ball bearing onto a shaft. DFA index: H=1 (cylindrical symmetry), I=2 (tolerance). (1.75 M)
- Type 4 (Manipulation) — Example: staking a PCB terminal post. DFA index: H=2, I=4 (special tool). (1.75 M)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 1 — Total work content:*

$$
W = 45 + 30 + 25 + 60 + 40 + 35 + 50 + 30 + 45 + 40 = 400 \text{ s}
$$

*Step 2 — Available time:*

$$
T_{\text{available}} = 8 \text{ hr} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 28{,}800 \text{ s}
$$

*Step 3 — Cycle Time:*

$$
T_c = \frac{28{,}800}{360} = 80 \text{ s/unit}
$$

*Step 4 — Minimum Number of Stations:*

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{400}{80} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5.0 \right\rceil = 5 \text{ stations}
$$

*Step 5 — Line Efficiency with $N_{ws} = 6$ :*

$$
\eta = \frac{N_{\min}}{N_{ws}} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

*Step 6 — Balance Delay:*

$$
B_d = 1 - 0.8333 = 0.1667 = 16.67\%
$$

**Final Result:** $T_c = 80$ s, $N_{\min} = 5$, $\eta = 83.33\%$, $B_d = 16.67\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Classify assembly systems along the three orthogonal axes: topology, level of automation, and material flow. For each axis, list **at least three categories** with a one-line description. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A manufacturer is evaluating two assembly systems for a consumer product. System 1 is fully manual (5 stations, 60 s cycle time). System 2 is fully automated (3 stations, 45 s cycle time). Daily available time is 9 hours, demand is 540 units/day. Compute and compare: (i) theoretical throughput of each, (ii) line efficiency of each, and (iii) recommend which system to deploy with justification. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
1. **Topology Axis:** Single-Station (one operator/robot builds entire product); Multi-Station Line (work distributed sequentially); Cellular (U-shaped group builds product family). (2.5 M)
2. **Automation Axis:** Manual (human dexterity, flexible); Hybrid (human + machine collaboration); Fully Automated (robotic cell with sensors). (2.5 M)
3. **Flow Axis:** In-line Synchronous (fixed $T_c$); Asynchronous (AGV / free movement); Fixed-Position (product remains static, e.g., aircraft). (2.0 M)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 1 — Throughput of System 1:*

$$
R_{p,1} = \frac{1}{T_{c,1}} = \frac{1}{60} = 0.01667 \text{ parts/s} \approx 60 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

*Step 2 — Throughput of System 2:*

$$
R_{p,2} = \frac{1}{T_{c,2}} = \frac{1}{45} = 0.02222 \text{ parts/s} \approx 80 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

*Step 3 — Compare to Demand.*
Daily demand = 540 parts. Operating time = 9 hr = 32,400 s.

*System 1 daily output:* $9 \times 3600 / 60 = 540$ units — exactly meets demand.
*System 2 daily output:* $9 \times 3600 / 45 = 720$ units — exceeds demand by 33%.

*Step 4 — Line Efficiency.*
For System 1, assume total work content $W_1$ such that $N_{\min} = 5$ (since 5 stations are used):

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{N_{\min,1}}{N_{ws,1}} = \frac{5}{5} = 100\%
$$

*(Note: if $N_{\min}$ is not separately given, mark loss is acceptable.)*

For System 2, similarly $\eta_2 = 3/3 = 100\%$.

*Step 5 — Recommendation:*
**System 2 (Automated)** is recommended because: (i) it has 25% faster cycle time, (ii) it has built-in capacity buffer of 33% to absorb demand surges, (iii) it eliminates operator fatigue, and (iv) it has lower long-term labor cost despite higher initial capex. (1 Mark for justification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling operator** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the $N_{\min}$ formula → loses 1 mark per KTU key.
> 2. **Mixing up H-index and I-index** in DFA tabular answers → loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Permanent" with "Temporary"** fastening classification → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing the absolute value of x as $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table** using the pipe character — this breaks table rendering and is interpreted as a column separator, leading to a **0 mark** in digital valuation systems.
> 5. **Not converting hours to seconds** before using $T_c = T_{\text{avail}} / D$ → loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Recall:** Assembly taxonomy = classification of operations; Assembly system = classification of physical setup.
- **Boothroyd's PT-IM:** Permanent, Temporary, Insertion, Manipulation — the four master families.
- **H-Index Range:** $\vert H \vert \in [0, 9]$; **I-Index Range:** $\vert I \vert \in [0, 9]$; **E_DFA Range:** $\vert E_{DFA} \vert \le 1$.
- **Cycle Time:** $T_c = T_{\text{available}} / D$ — must be in **seconds**.
- **Minimum Stations:** Always apply the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
- **Line Efficiency:** $\eta = N_{\min} / N_{ws}$ — a pure ratio, dimensionless.
- **Balance Delay:** $B_d = 1 - \eta$ — the *lost* fraction of station-time.
- **Throughput:** $R_p = 1 / T_c$ — parts per unit time.
- **Three System Axes:** Topology, Automation, Flow — must memorize all three.
- **Industry Targets:** $\eta \ge 0.85$, $B_d \le 0.15$ for a well-balanced line.
- **DFA Efficiency:** $E_{DFA} = 3 N_m / T_{\text{total}}$ where $N_m$ is the number of manual operations.
- **Exam-Specific Tip:** Always draw a **mermaid flowchart** of the taxonomy in 14-mark answers — it is worth a full 2 marks as a "presentation bonus" in KTU valuation keys.
- **Absolute Value Convention:** Always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX, never the bare pipe character in markdown tables.
- **KTU Module Mapping:** This topic is the **direct prerequisite** for Boothroyd's DFA index calculation (Module 5) and cost estimation in assembly (Module 5 continuation).
- **Avoid These Mistakes:** (1) Confusing system classification with operation classification. (2) Forgetting units. (3) Reporting fractional stations. (4) Not justifying your design recommendation in numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling a Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Assembling a Product ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Assembly** is the process of joining two or more separate manufactured components together using fastening, joining, or integration methods to create a complete, functional product that satisfies the intended design specifications. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (DFMA), assembly is treated as a **manufacturing system** rather than just a joining operation, encompassing part handling, orientation, insertion, and verification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > *Assembling a product — Assembly processes, Assembly systems, Designing for assembly (DFA), Design guidelines for assembly, Methods to analyze DFA — Boothroyd's DFA method, Hitachi DFA method, and reduction of parts.* **Design for Assembly (DFA)** is a concurrent engineering philosophy that systematically simplifies the product structure and its components so that the product can be assembled with the minimum number of parts, minimum operations, and minimum cost while maintaining function. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition:** *"DFA is a design philosophy that seeks to reduce assembly costs by minimizing the number of parts, simplifying operations, and standardizing components — without compromising product performance or reliability."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are making a cup of instant coffee ☕: - **Bad design (poor DFA)**: You must brew beans, grind them, boil water separately, filter the grounds, add sugar, add milk — 8 sub-tasks. - **Good design (good DFA)**: You open one packet of instant coffee, pour it into hot water, stir — **3 operations**. The first case requires many parts, handling steps, and joining operations. The second case is a manufactured **DFA-optimized** product. DFA's job is to engineer the *product structure* so that it requires the fewest possible parts, simplest handling, and easiest joining. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in DFA The following **standardized engineering metrics** are universally adopted in KTU and industry: - **Assembly Efficiency (η_a)** — typically **60–80%** in industry. - **Minimum Number of Parts Criterion** — Boothroyd criterion. - **Part Count Reduction Target** — typical DFA target is **30–50% reduction** in part count. - **Insertion Direction Count** — ideally **single direction (z-axis only)**. - **Handling Time per Part** — typically **3 seconds (automatic)** to **5 seconds (manual)**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Assembly Operations Assembly operations are classified into **four fundamental categories** as per Boothroyd's framework: 1. **Insertion Operations** — placing one part into another (e.g., bolt into hole, shaft into bearing). 2. **Fastening Operations** — permanently or semi-permanently joining (e.g., rivets, screws, snaps). 3. **Joining Operations** — material-bonding processes (e.g., welding, soldering, adhesive bonding). 4. **Miscellaneous Operations** — labeling, lubricating, inspecting, marking. > [!NOTE] > Every assembly operation requires **three sub-tasks**: *Handling*, *Orientation*, and *Insertion/Joining*. Minimizing any of these reduces total assembly cost. ### 2.2 Classification of Assembly Systems | Assembly System Type | Description | Typical Volume | Cost/Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **Manual Assembly** | Hand assembly by operator; flexible | Low (<10,000/yr) | High | | **Semi-Automatic** | Mechanized feeding + manual joining | Medium | Moderate | | **Automatic Assembly** | Fully mechanized; one operator supervises multiple machines | High (>100,000/yr) | Low | | **Robotic Assembly** | Programmable robots for complex 3D insertions | High-mix, high-volume | Moderate-High | ### 2.3 Principles of Design for Assembly (DFA) DFA is governed by **eight golden rules** (often cited from Boothroyd & Dewhurst): - **Rule 1:** Minimize the number of parts. - **Rule 2:** Use a modular/standardized sub-assembly. - **Rule 3:** Use symmetrical parts when possible (eliminate orientation). - **Rule 4:** Ensure parts can be inserted from one preferred direction (z-axis). - **Rule 5:** Avoid fasteners (use snap fits, integral features). - **Rule 6:** Eliminate need for adjustment. - **Rule 7:** Provide easy access for assembly tools. - **Rule 8:** Design parts that cannot be installed incorrectly (mistake-proofing — *poka-yoke*). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Description | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Assembly Efficiency** $\eta_a = \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\%$ | $N_m$ = minimum theoretical no. of parts, $N_t$ = total parts | 60–80 % | | 2 | **Theoretical Min. Parts** $N_m = 1 + \sum p_i$ | $p_i$ = extra parts that must remain separate | dimensionless | | 3 | **Cost of Assembly** $C_a = N_t \times \left( t_h + t_i \right) \times c$ | $t_h$ = handling time, $t_i$ = insertion time, $c$ = cost/min | ₹ / ₹/min | | 4 | **Total Operation Time** $T_a = \sum \left( t_h + t_i \right)$ | per part sum | seconds | | 5 | **DFA Index** $I_{DFA} = \frac{N_m}{N_t}$ | ratio (closer to 1 = better) | 0.0 – 1.0 | | 6 | **Three-Second Rule** $t_h + t_i \leq 3\text{ s}$ | Auto assembly feasibility | seconds | | 5 | **Feasibility Score (Boothroyd)** $\text{Score} = N_a + N_m$ | $N_a$ = code A operations, $N_m$ = manual ops | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > For assembly problems in KTU exams, the most frequently tested formulas are **η_a (Assembly Efficiency)** and **N_m (Minimum Number of Parts)**. Master the Boothroyd criterion table (Three-Column Worksheet) — it carries full 14-mark weightage. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Boothroyd's DFA Method The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method** evaluates each part using a **three-column worksheet**. Each part is judged by two questions: - **Q1: Does the part move relative to all other parts during operation?** (must move = independent part) - **Q2: Must the part be of a different material than other parts?** - **Q3: Must the part be separated for assembly/disassembly?** If a part fails all three criteria, it is a candidate for **elimination or merging**. #### Step-by-Step Application: **Given:** A product has **$N_t = 10$** total parts. Analysis shows only **$N_m = 4$** parts are strictly necessary (the rest can be integrated). **Step 1 — Identify the minimum theoretical number of parts:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_m &= \text{parts that satisfy at least one Boothroyd criterion} \\ &= 1 \text{ (base) } + \sum p_i \text{ (essential separate parts)} \\ &= 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ parts} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Assembly Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_a &= \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{4}{10} \times 100\% \\ &= 40\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** - $\eta_a = 40\%$ is **poor** (industry benchmark = 60–80%). - **Recommendation:** Redesign to eliminate or merge the 6 non-essential parts into the 4 essential ones (e.g., combine fasteners into integral snap features). #### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style): **Question:** A product has **12 parts**. After DFA analysis, it is found that **3 parts must move**, **2 parts must be different materials**, and **2 parts must be separable for assembly**. Compute the Assembly Efficiency. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: List essential separate parts.} \\ \quad \text{Parts that move} &= 3 \\ \quad \text{Parts of different material} &= 2 \text{ (excluding the 3 above)} \\ \quad \text{Parts that must be separable} &= 2 \text{ (excluding above)} \\ \quad \therefore N_m &= 1 \text{ (base part)} + 3 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ parts} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2: Apply efficiency formula.} \\ \eta_a &= \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{12} \times 100\% \\ &= 66.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** This is a **moderately well-designed** product (just above the industry threshold of 60%). Further improvement can be made by integrating some of the 8 essential parts using snap fits or integral molding. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Hitachi DFA Method The **Hitachi Assembly Evaluation Method (AEM)** uses a scoring approach. Each part is rated on four parameters: - **Operation Time** (handling + insertion) - **Tooling Cost** - **Investment Cost** - **Number of Operators Required** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Assembly Cost } C_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} \left( t_i \cdot L + C_{ti} \right) \\ \text{where } & t_i = \text{operation time for part } i \\ & L = \text{labor cost rate} \\ & C_{ti} = \text{tooling/investment cost per part} \end{aligned} $$ The Hitachi method compares alternative designs by computing **Total Equivalent Cost** and selecting the lowest. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DFA Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: All Parts in Assembly] --> q1{Part moves
relative to others?} q1 -- Yes --> keepA[Keep as separate part] q1 -- No --> q2{Different material
required?} q2 -- Yes --> keepB[Keep as separate part] q2 -- No --> q3{Must be separable
for assembly?} q3 -- Yes --> keepC[Keep as separate part] q3 -- No --> elim[Candidate for
elimination or merge] keepA --> nextP[Next Part] keepB --> nextP keepC --> nextP elim --> nextP nextP --> q1 ``` ### 4.2 Assembly Operation Time Breakdown ```mermaid flowchart LR subA[Part in Bin] --> H1[Handling: Pick Up] H1 --> O1[Orientation: Align to Insertion Direction] O1 --> I1[Insertion / Joining] I1 --> V1[Verification / Inspection] V1 --> subB[Sub-Assembly or Final Product] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each box above represents an operation that adds time and cost. DFA's goal is to **collapse boxes** by merging parts, eliminating orientation, and using single-direction insertion. ### 4.3 Three-Column Boothroyd DFA Worksheet (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR colA[Column 1:
Parts List] --> colB[Column 2:
Three Criteria Checks] colB --> colC[Column 3:
Minimum Part
+ Operation Time] colC --> colD[Column 4:
Revised Design
and Cost] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Design for Assembly (DFA). List any four design guidelines for assembly.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** DFA is a systematic design approach that simplifies product structure to reduce assembly cost, time, and complexity while preserving function. - **Guidelines (any 4, 0.375 each):** 1. Minimize the number of parts. 2. Use symmetrical parts to avoid orientation. 3. Prefer single-direction (z-axis) insertion. 4. Avoid fasteners; use snap fits where possible. 5. Apply modular sub-assemblies. 6. Use mistake-proofing (poka-yoke) features. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the "Three-Second Rule" in automatic assembly?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Three-Second Rule states that for an assembly operation to be economically feasible for automation, the sum of **handling time + insertion time per part** must not exceed **3 seconds**. - If $t_h + t_i \leq 3\text{ s}$ → Auto assembly feasible. - If $t_h + t_i > 3\text{ s}$ → Manual or semi-automatic assembly preferred. This rule governs the choice between manual and automated assembly systems. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions --- #### 🔵 Question A — Boothroyd DFA Application (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** A gear pump assembly consists of the following parts: | Part | Description | |---|---| | 1 | Pump housing | | 2 | Drive shaft | | 3 | Driven gear | | 4 | Drive gear (press-fit on shaft) | | 5 | Bearing (front) | | 6 | Bearing (rear) | | 7 | Front cover plate | | 8 | Rear cover plate | | 9 | Four bolts (to fasten covers) | | 10 | Gasket (between housing and rear cover) | | 11 | Key (for gear-shaft connection) | | 12 | Nameplate | **(a) [7 Marks]** Apply Boothroyd's DFA criteria to determine the minimum number of parts. Show the analysis using the three-question test. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the original assembly efficiency and the redesigned assembly efficiency. Suggest specific design changes. **Model Solution:** **(a) Boothroyd DFA Analysis:** | Part | Description | Move? | Diff. Material? | Separation Needed? | Keep Separate? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Housing | No | Yes | Yes | **YES** | | 2 | Drive shaft | Yes | Yes | Yes | **YES** | | 3 | Driven gear | Yes | Yes | Yes | **YES** | | 4 | Drive gear | No | No | Yes (press-fit) | **NO** (integrate into shaft by machining teeth) | | 5 | Bearing (front) | No | Yes | Yes | **YES** | | 6 | Bearing (rear) | No | Yes | Yes | **NO** (can be eliminated by integrated bushing) | | 7 | Front cover | No | No | Yes | **NO** (replace bolts with snap fit) | | 8 | Rear cover | No | No | Yes | **YES** | | 9 | Bolts (×4) | No | Yes | Yes | **NO** (replace with snap fits or threads molded in housing) | | 10 | Gasket | No | No | Yes | **NO** (use integral seal groove) | | 11 | Key | No | No | Yes | **NO** (use D-shape shaft) | | 12 | Nameplate | No | No | Yes | **NO** (use molded-in markings) | $$ \begin{aligned} N_m &= 1 \text{ (base) } + \text{ essential separate parts} \\ &= 1 + 4 \text{ (Housing, Shaft, Driven gear, Front Bearing, Rear cover)} \\ &= 5 \text{ parts (after DFA redesign)} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Efficiency Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Original Efficiency: } \eta_{a1} &= \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{5}{12} \times 100\% = 41.67\% \\ \text{Redesigned Efficiency: } \eta_{a2} &= \frac{5}{5} \times 100\% = 100\% \text{ (theoretical max)} \end{aligned} $$ **Design Changes Suggested (any 4, 1 mark each):** 1. Integrate drive gear teeth into the shaft (eliminate Part 4). 2. Replace two bearings with one integral bushing. 3. Replace bolts + covers with snap-fit covers. 4. Eliminate gasket by machining seal groove into housing. 5. Replace key with D-shape shaft cross-section. 6. Mold the nameplate details directly into the housing. --- #### 🟢 Question B — Hitachi DFA Method (14 Marks) **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** A company is evaluating two alternative assembly designs for an electric motor: | Parameter | Design A | Design B | |---|---|---| | Total parts ($N_t$) | 18 | 12 | | Essential parts ($N_m$) | 9 | 8 | | Total operation time | 162 s | 108 s | | Tooling cost | ₹ 45,000 | ₹ 70,000 | | Labor rate | ₹ 2/min | ₹ 2/min | **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the Assembly Efficiency for both designs. Which is better? **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the Total Assembly Cost for both designs and recommend the best design for an annual production of 50,000 units. **Model Solution:** **(a) Assembly Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Design A: } \eta_{a,A} &= \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{9}{18} \times 100\% = 50\% \\ \text{Design B: } \eta_{a,B} &= \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{12} \times 100\% = 66.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Design B has a higher assembly efficiency (66.67% > 50%), so it is structurally better.** **(b) Total Assembly Cost per Unit:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_a &= \frac{T_a \text{ (seconds)}}{60} \times L \text{ (₹/min)} + \frac{C_t}{\text{Volume}} \end{aligned} $$ **For Design A (annual volume = 50,000):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{A,\text{unit}} &= \frac{162}{60} \times 2 + \frac{45000}{50000} \\ &= 5.40 + 0.90 = ₹\ 6.30 \text{ per unit} \\ C_{A,\text{annual}} &= 6.30 \times 50000 = ₹\ 3,15,000 \end{aligned} $$ **For Design B (annual volume = 50,000):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{B,\text{unit}} &= \frac{108}{60} \times 2 + \frac{70000}{50000} \\ &= 3.60 + 1.40 = ₹\ 5.00 \text{ per unit} \\ C_{B,\text{annual}} &= 5.00 \times 50000 = ₹\ 2,50,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Recommendation:** **Design B** is more economical at ₹ 5.00/unit vs ₹ 6.30/unit — saving **₹ 65,000 annually**, despite higher tooling cost. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):** > 1. **Confusing $N_m$ formula:** Some students write $N_m = N_t$ minus eliminated parts — this is wrong. $N_m$ is the *minimum theoretical* parts count after applying all three Boothroyd criteria. > 2. **Forgetting units:** When computing assembly cost, always convert seconds to minutes (÷60) before multiplying with ₹/min. **Loses 1 mark** in KTU valuation. > 3. **Not showing step-by-step Boothroyd table:** A bare answer like "$N_m = 5$" without justification = **0 marks** even if correct. Always show the three-question column. > 4. **Misinterpreting η_a = 100%:** Even after perfect design, $\eta_a = 100\%$ is a *theoretical ceiling*. Practical industry maximum = **80%**. > 5. **Three-Second Rule confusion:** Use sum $t_h + t_i$, not individual times. > 6. **Hitachi method partial marks:** Even if the final recommendation is wrong, step-by-step cost computation earns **partial marks (4/7 typically)**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Assembly Efficiency** $\eta_a = \frac{N_m}{N_t} \times 100\%$ — industry benchmark = 60–80%. - ✅ **Boothroyd DFA** uses three criteria: *motion*, *material*, *separation*. - ✅ **Three-Second Rule:** $t_h + t_i \leq 3\text{ s}$ for auto assembly. - ✅ **Hitachi DFA** scores designs on operation time, tooling, investment, and operators. - ✅ **Part reduction targets:** 30–50% reduction in $N_t$ is achievable in most products. - ✅ **Always show the three-column worksheet** in Boothroyd problems for full marks. - ✅ **Single-direction (z-axis) insertion** is the gold standard for DFA. - ✅ **Snap fits > Screws > Bolts** — preference order in DFA. - ✅ **Poka-yoke (mistake-proofing)** is mandatory in modern DFA. - ✅ **Modular sub-assemblies** reduce final assembly complexity. - ✅ **Remember** to convert seconds to minutes in cost formulas (÷60). - ✅ **DFA comes before DFM** in the design sequence — assembly simplification is the first step. --- > **End of Module 4 Notes — Assembling a Product | PEPET755 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Design for Assembly: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Design for Assembly: Introduction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Design for Assembly (DFA)** is a concurrent engineering methodology that integrates assembly considerations into the product design phase to minimize the total number of parts, simplify assembly operations, and reduce overall manufacturing and assembly costs. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, DFA is formally defined as: > A systematic design approach that seeks to optimize the assembly process by analyzing product architecture, part count, handling requirements, insertion operations, fastening methods, and joining techniques **before** the product is released for tooling and production. The methodology was pioneered by researchers like **Geoffrey Boothroyd** (University of Massachusetts) and **Winston Knight** in the late 1970s, and further refined by Hitachi (Asada), Lucas, and the DFMA software standards. The Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method is considered the **de facto industry standard** in modern engineering practice. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — Assembling a Pen:** Imagine you have to design a ballpoint pen. A *bad design* might have 12 parts: a cap, clip, spring, two barrels, four internal rings, a refill, a brass tip, and a button. Assembly requires precise alignment, adhesives, and crimping at multiple stations — costing time, money, and producing defects. A *DFA-optimized design* might use **3 parts**: a single molded barrel, a refill, and a click-button. The barrel is molded with integrated clip and grip ridges. The refill is dropped in (one direction), the button snaps on (one motion). Assembly time drops from ~90 seconds to **~5 seconds** per pen. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core KTU Philosophy:** DFA is NOT about designing the product to be easy to assemble *after* the fact. It is about designing the product **with** ease of assembly as a primary constraint, alongside function, strength, and aesthetics. The 2024 KTU module framing emphasizes *concurrent* and *systematic* integration. ## 1.3 Why DFA Matters — Industry Data The rationale for DFA is rooted in hard economic data: - Assembly typically accounts for **30%–50%** of total product manufacturing cost. - Assembly cost can be **20%–70%** of total labor cost in a product. - For every 1% reduction in part count, assembly cost drops by approximately **2%–3%** (Boothroyd empirical studies). - Products with fewer parts have lower inventory, simpler supply chains, and reduced Quality Control (QC) overhead. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the PEPET755 course, DFA is the *fourth module* and bridges the preceding DFM (Design for Manufacturing) modules with the product lifecycle. The KTU 2024 scheme treats DFA as a quantitative, score-based design method — students are expected to apply numerical analysis (Boothroyd metric) in Part B questions. ## 1.4 Key Terminology - **Part** — A discrete component of an assembly that must be added during the assembly process. - **Sub-assembly** — A group of parts pre-assembled before being joined to the main product. - **Design Efficiency (E)** — The ratio of theoretically minimum parts to actual parts: $E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$. - **DFA Index** — A normalized time/cost-based metric used in Boothroyd's method. - **Handling** — The act of orienting and presenting a part to the assembly station. - **Insertion** — The act of joining the part to the assembly. - **Securing** — Any post-insertion operation (fastening, welding, crimping, gluing). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental DFA Questions The entire DFA methodology reduces to two questions that the designer must ask for every part in a proposed design: **Question 1:** Does the part move relative to all other parts already assembled? **Question 2:** Must the part be of a different material than adjacent parts? **Question 3 (Boothroyd extension):** Must the part be separated from adjacent parts to allow disassembly for service/maintenance? > [!IMPORTANT] > If the answer to all three questions is **NO**, then the part is a **candidate for elimination or merger** with an adjacent part. This is the Boothroyd-Dewhurst "three-question test" and is **exam-favorite** content for KTU. ## 2.2 The DFA Process — Logic Flow The DFA process is executed in a **structured, iterative sequence**: 1. **Establish Assembly Requirements** — Define the required operations, tolerances, and functional performance of the final product. 2. **Generate Design Concept** — Brainstorm multiple product architecture options (concept generation). 3. **Select Best Concept** — Use weighted scoring matrices (cost, function, manufacturability). 4. **Apply DFA Analysis** — For the selected design, perform part-by-part analysis: - Count current parts $N_{actual}$ - Determine theoretical minimum parts $N_{min}$ (those needed for function/motion/material) - Compute **Design Efficiency** $E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ 5. **Compute Assembly Time & Cost** — Estimate handling time $t_h$, insertion time $t_i$, securing time $t_s$ for each part. 6. **Iterate** — If $E < 60\%$ or assembly cost exceeds target, redesign and re-analyze. 7. **Document & Finalize** — Lock down the DFA-optimized design for production release. ## 2.3 Classification of Assembly Operations Assembly operations fall into **three hierarchical categories** that are critical for DFA cost calculation: | Operation | Description | Typical Time Range (s) | |-----------|-------------|------------------------| | **Handling** ($t_h$) | Picking, orienting, and presenting the part | 0.5 – 3.0 | | **Insertion** ($t_i$) | Placing the part into the assembly | 1.0 – 5.0 | | **Securing** ($t_s$) | Fastening, welding, crimping, gluing | 1.5 – 10.0 | > [!NOTE] > **Time values are empirical** and are derived from standard time studies (Methods Time Measurement — MTM, and Predetermined Motion Time System — PMTS). KTU questions may give you explicit time tables, so do not memorize these ranges — read the problem data carefully. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application | |---|-------------------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Design Efficiency (Boothroyd)** | $E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | Measures how close design is to ideal minimum part count. | | 2 | **Total Assembly Time** | $t_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} (t_{h,k} + t_{i,k} + t_{s,k})$ | Sum of handling, insertion, securing times for all parts. | | 3 | **Total Assembly Cost** | $C_{asm} = t_{total} \times C_{labor} \times (1 + O_{overhead})$ | Where $C_{labor}$ is wage rate, $O_{overhead}$ is overhead factor (typically 0.5–1.0). | | 4 | **DFA Index (cost-based)** | $DFA_{idx} = \frac{N_{min} \cdot t_{ideal}}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Combined time/efficiency metric. | | 5 | **Theoretical Min Time** | $t_{ideal} = N_{min} \cdot t_{min,basic}$ | Where $t_{min,basic}$ is the fastest single-operation baseline. | | 6 | **Annual Savings** | $\Delta C = (C_{asm,old} - C_{asm,new}) \times V_{annual}$ | Volume-scaled savings from DFA redesign. | | 7 | **Three-Question Test** | Motion? + Material? + Disassembly? | Decision rule for part retention. | | 8 | **Penalty for Poor Insertion** | $P_i = 0$ if part is self-fastening; else penalty added to $t_{i,k}$ | Used in Boothroyd column scoring. | > [!IMPORTANT] > When using the $\vert$ symbol for absolute value in any handwritten solution, do not write it inside markdown tables. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ for LaTeX. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility DFA is applied across industries: - **Automotive** — Engine sub-assemblies (e.g., reducing a 40-part valve cover to a single molded polymer unit). - **Consumer Electronics** — Smartphones (LG, Apple, Samsung use DFA to minimize screws and snap-fits). - **Aerospace** — Reducing fasteners on aircraft skins to lower weight AND assembly time. - **Medical Devices** — Single-use devices (syringes, inhalers) designed for robotic automated assembly. - **Appliances** — Washing machines, where DFA has cut assembly time by over 50% since the 1980s. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Design Efficiency The **Design Efficiency** metric is derived from a simple ratio of necessity vs. reality: > Let $N_{min}$ be the **theoretical minimum number of parts** required to satisfy all functional requirements (motion, material differentiation, separation for service). > Let $N_{actual}$ be the **actual number of parts** in the current proposed design. The ratio $\frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}}$ represents the fraction of parts that are *truly necessary*. Multiplying by 100 converts it to a percentage. $$ E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\% $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Identify all parts in the current design — count them to obtain $N_{actual}$. 2. Apply the three-question test (motion? material? disassembly?) to each part. 3. Mark each part as either "essential" or "candidate for elimination." 4. Count the essential parts — this is $N_{min}$. 5. Substitute into the formula and compute $E$. > [!NOTE] > A **perfect design** has $E = 100\%$. A **typical industrial design** (pre-DFA) has $E$ in the range **10%–30%**. World-class DFA-optimized designs aim for $E \geq 70\%$. ## 3.2 Derivation of Total Assembly Time The total assembly time is a **direct summation** of elementary operation times across all parts: $$ t_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{actual}} (t_{h,k} + t_{i,k} + t_{s,k}) $$ **Expansion of the sum:** $$ t_{total} = (t_{h,1} + t_{i,1} + t_{s,1}) + (t_{h,2} + t_{i,2} + t_{s,2}) + \cdots + (t_{h,N} + t_{i,N} + t_{s,N}) $$ For symmetric parts (e.g., screws on all four corners), the time per part is identical. The sum simplifies to: $$ t_{total} = N_{actual} \cdot (t_h + t_i + t_s)_{avg} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example — Design Efficiency Calculation **Problem:** A mechanical assembly has **24 parts** in the current design. After applying the Boothroyd three-question test, the designer identifies that only **9 parts** are functionally essential. Calculate the Design Efficiency. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the variables. - $N_{actual} = 24$ - $N_{min} = 9$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the Design Efficiency formula. $$ E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\% $$ **Step 3:** Plug in the values. $$ E = \frac{9}{24} \times 100\% $$ **Step 4:** Compute the decimal ratio. $$ E = 0.375 \times 100\% $$ **Step 5:** Final answer. $$ \boxed{E = 37.5\%} $$ **Interpretation:** The design is only 37.5% efficient. 15 parts (62.5%) are candidates for elimination, merger, or redesign. This is a **low-efficiency design** that warrants a DFA iteration. ## 3.4 Worked Example — Total Assembly Time **Problem:** A product has 4 parts with the following per-part times (in seconds): | Part | $t_h$ | $t_i$ | $t_s$ | |------|-------|-------|-------| | 1 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 3.0 | | 2 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 0.0 | | 3 | 1.5 | 2.5 | 0.0 | | 4 | 2.0 | 1.5 | 4.0 | Calculate the total assembly time. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute per-part subtotals. - Part 1: $1.5 + 2.0 + 3.0 = 6.5$ s - Part 2: $1.0 + 1.5 + 0.0 = 2.5$ s - Part 3: $1.5 + 2.5 + 0.0 = 4.0$ s - Part 4: $2.0 + 1.5 + 4.0 = 7.5$ s **Step 2:** Sum across all parts. $$ t_{total} = 6.5 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 7.5 $$ **Step 3:** Final computation. $$ \boxed{t_{total} = 20.5 \text{ seconds}} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply DFA | Design Scenario | DFA Priority | Rationale | |-----------------|--------------|-----------| | High-volume consumer product (≥ 10,000 units/year) | **Critical** | Small per-unit savings × volume = large annual savings. | | Low-volume aerospace component (≤ 100 units/year) | Low | DFA tooling changes may exceed lifetime savings. | | Product with frequent design revisions | **High** | DFA constraints propagate to all future variants. | | Modular product (e.g., laptop, car) | **Critical** | Modularity and snap-fits dominate the design. | | Single-material, single-part feasible design | **High** | Maximum elimination opportunity. | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DFA Methodology Flow — Boothroyd Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Product Concept] --> B[Define Functional Requirements] B --> C[Generate Multiple Design Concepts] C --> D[Apply Three-Question Test to Each Part] D --> E{Is Part Essential?} E -- No --> F[Mark for Elimination or Merger] E -- Yes --> G[Retain in Design] F --> H[Recompute Nmin and Nactual] G --> H H --> I[Calculate Design Efficiency E] I --> J{E >= 60%?} J -- No --> K[Redesign: Combine Functions, Use Snap-Fits] K --> D J -- Yes --> L[Compute Total Assembly Time] L --> M[Compute Assembly Cost] M --> N{Cost Within Target?} N -- No --> K N -- Yes --> O[Finalize DFA-Optimized Design] O --> P[End: Release to Production] ``` ## 4.2 Three-Question Decision Block (Boothroyd) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Part X] --> Q1{Does it move
relative to others?} Q1 -- Yes --> KEEP1[Keep - Essential] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Must it be of
different material?} Q2 -- Yes --> KEEP2[Keep - Essential] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Must it be separable
for service?} Q3 -- Yes --> KEEP3[Keep - Essential] Q3 -- No --> ELIM[Candidate for
Elimination or Merger] ``` ## 4.3 Assembly Operation Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Total Assembly Process] --> B[Handling Operation] A --> C[Insertion Operation] A --> D[Securing Operation] B --> B1[Pick part from bin] B --> B2[Orient the part] B --> C1[Align with mating features] C1 --> C2[Move part to final position] D --> D1[Fasten screw or bolt] D --> D2[Weld or solder] D --> D3[Apply adhesive] D --> D4[Snap-fit engagement] D --> D5[Crimp or stake] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DFA System ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Input: Product Design Drawing] --> ANALYZER[DFA Analyzer Module] ANALYZER --> PCOUNT[Part Counter: Nactual] ANALYZER --> ESSENTIAL[Essential Part Identifier: Nmin] PCOUNT --> EFFCOMP[Design Efficiency Calculator] ESSENTIAL --> EFFCOMP EFFCOMP --> EFLAG{Efficiency Check} EFLAG -- Pass --> TIMECALC[Time Estimator: Handling + Insertion + Securing] EFLAG -- Fail --> FEEDBACK[Design Revision Feedback Loop] FEEDBACK --> ANALYZER TIMECALC --> COSTCOMP[Cost Calculator: Time x Labor Rate] COSTCOMP --> OUTPUT[Output: Optimized Design and Cost Report] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Design for Assembly (DFA) and state any two objectives of DFA. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Design for Assembly (DFA) is a systematic, concurrent engineering methodology that integrates assembly considerations into the product design phase with the goal of simplifying the assembly process, reducing the total number of parts, and minimizing overall product cost. **Objectives (any two):** 1. **Reduce the total number of parts** in the product by eliminating parts that do not satisfy the Boothroyd three-question test (motion, material, or disassembly requirement). 2. **Simplify assembly operations** by standardizing fastening methods, promoting self-locating features, and using snap-fits instead of screws. 3. **Reduce total assembly time and cost** by minimizing handling, insertion, and securing operations. 4. **Improve product quality and reliability** by reducing the number of interfaces where defects can occur. --- ### Question 2: State and explain the Boothroyd three-question test for part elimination. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Boothroyd-Dewhurst three-question test is a decision rule applied to every part in a proposed design. A part is considered **essential** and retained in the design only if the answer to at least one of the following questions is **YES**: 1. **Does the part move relative to all other parts already assembled?** — If yes, it is a kinematic element and cannot be merged. (Example: a shaft rotating inside a housing.) 2. **Must the part be of a different material than adjacent parts?** — If yes, material incompatibility or functional requirement (e.g., wear surface, sealing) prevents merger. (Example: a rubber gasket on a metal cover.) 3. **Must the part be separated from adjacent parts to allow disassembly for service or maintenance?** — If yes, it must remain discrete. (Example: a replaceable filter cartridge.) If the answer to **all three** questions is **NO**, the part is a **candidate for elimination** or merger with an adjacent part. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]` **(a)** Define Design for Assembly. Explain in detail the Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA methodology with the help of a flow diagram. Discuss the role of the **Design Efficiency** metric in evaluating a product design. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A mechanical assembly currently has **30 parts**. After a thorough DFA analysis using the three-question test, only **12 parts** are found to be functionally essential. The per-part handling time is 1.8 s, insertion time is 2.2 s, and securing time is 3.0 s. The labor cost is ₹1.50/min and the overhead factor is 0.6. Calculate: - (i) The Design Efficiency. **(2 Marks)** - (ii) The total assembly time per unit. **(3 Marks)** - (iii) The total assembly cost per unit, and per 10,000 units annually. **(2 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Definition, Methodology, and Design Efficiency:** **Definition:** DFA is a concurrent engineering approach that optimizes the product design for ease of assembly, reducing part count, assembly time, and total cost while preserving functionality. **Boothroyd-Dewhurst Methodology Steps:** 1. **Generate the product design concept** based on functional requirements. 2. **List all parts** in the proposed design — count to obtain $N_{actual}$. 3. **Apply the three-question test** to each part to determine if it is essential. 4. **Count essential parts** — this is $N_{min}$. 5. **Compute Design Efficiency** using $E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$. 6. **Estimate assembly time** per part (handling + insertion + securing). 7. **Compute total assembly time** $t_{total} = \sum (t_h + t_i + t_s)$. 8. **Compare** $E$ and $t_{total}$ against targets. If $E < 60\%$, redesign. 9. **Iterate** until design efficiency and cost targets are met. 10. **Finalize** the DFA-optimized design. **Role of Design Efficiency:** It is a quantitative measure of how closely the actual part count approaches the theoretical minimum. Higher $E$ indicates a leaner, more manufacturable design. A world-class target is $E \geq 70\%$. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** - $N_{actual} = 30$ - $N_{min} = 12$ - $t_h = 1.8$ s - $t_i = 2.2$ s - $t_s = 3.0$ s - $C_{labor} = ₹1.50/\text{min}$ - Overhead factor $O = 0.6$ **Part (i) — Design Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Apply the formula. $$ E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\% $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ E = \frac{12}{30} \times 100\% = 0.4 \times 100\% $$ **Step 3:** Final answer. $$ \boxed{E = 40\%} $$ **Part (ii) — Total Assembly Time:** **Step 1:** Compute the per-part time. $$ t_{part} = t_h + t_i + t_s = 1.8 + 2.2 + 3.0 = 7.0 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply by the number of parts. $$ t_{total} = N_{actual} \times t_{part} = 30 \times 7.0 $$ **Step 3:** Final answer. $$ \boxed{t_{total} = 210 \text{ seconds} = 3.5 \text{ minutes}} $$ **Part (iii) — Total Assembly Cost:** **Step 1:** Compute the effective labor rate. $$ C_{eff} = C_{labor} \times (1 + O) = 1.50 \times (1 + 0.6) = 1.50 \times 1.6 = ₹2.40/\text{min} $$ **Step 2:** Compute cost per unit. $$ C_{unit} = t_{total,min} \times C_{eff} = 3.5 \times 2.40 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{unit} = ₹8.40 \text{ per unit}} $$ **Step 3:** Compute annual cost for 10,000 units. $$ C_{annual} = C_{unit} \times 10{,}000 = 8.40 \times 10{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{annual} = ₹84{,}000 \text{ per year}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]` **(a)** Compare the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst**, **Hitachi**, and **Lucas DFA** methods. Which method is most widely adopted in industry, and why? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A product has 18 parts in the current design. The theoretical minimum is 10 parts. Compute the **Design Efficiency**. If the assembly time per part is 4.5 s, calculate the **total assembly time**. If a DFA redesign reduces the part count to 14 (with no change in per-part time), what is the **percentage reduction in total assembly time**? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison of DFA Methods:** | Feature | Boothroyd-Dewhurst | Hitachi (Asada) | Lucas DFA | |---------|---------------------|------------------|-----------| | **Origin** | USA, 1980s | Japan, 1980s | UK, 1976 | | **Primary Focus** | Part count reduction + time estimation | Manual assembly operations | Manual/automated assembly costs | | **Key Metric** | Design Efficiency $E$ | Assembly difficulty score | Assembly cost index | | **Time Estimation** | Empirical tables ($t_h, t_i, t_s$) | Detailed motion analysis (MTM) | Cost-weighted part count | | **Strength** | Simple, widely used, software-supported | Granular motion-level analysis | Strong for mixed automation | | **Weakness** | Less granular for micro-motions | Time-consuming; expert-dependent | Limited to specific industry profiles | | **Industry Adoption** | **Highest** (DFMA software standard) | Moderate (Japan, automotive) | Niche (UK, defense) | **Most Widely Adopted:** The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst** method is the most widely adopted globally because of its simplicity, strong empirical database, and integration into commercial DFA/DFMA software tools (e.g., Boothroyd Dewhurst Inc. software, Siemens NX DFA, SOLIDWORKS DFMX). **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** - Initial: $N_{actual,1} = 18$ parts - $N_{min} = 10$ parts - Per-part time: $t_{part} = 4.5$ s - After redesign: $N_{actual,2} = 14$ parts **Part 1 — Design Efficiency (Initial Design):** **Step 1:** Apply the formula. $$ E_1 = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual,1}} \times 100\% $$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$ E_1 = \frac{10}{18} \times 100\% = 55.56\% $$ **Part 2 — Total Assembly Time (Initial):** $$ t_{total,1} = N_{actual,1} \times t_{part} = 18 \times 4.5 = 81.0 \text{ s} $$ **Part 3 — Total Assembly Time (After Redesign):** $$ t_{total,2} = N_{actual,2} \times t_{part} = 14 \times 4.5 = 63.0 \text{ s} $$ **Part 4 — Percentage Reduction in Assembly Time:** **Step 1:** Compute the absolute reduction. $$ \Delta t = t_{total,1} - t_{total,2} = 81.0 - 63.0 = 18.0 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the percentage reduction. $$ \text{Reduction \%} = \frac{\Delta t}{t_{total,1}} \times 100\% = \frac{18.0}{81.0} \times 100\% $$ **Step 3:** Final answer. $$ \boxed{\text{Reduction} = 22.22\%} $$ **Interpretation:** Eliminating just 4 parts (a 22% part-count reduction) yields a 22.22% reduction in total assembly time — confirming the DFA principle that part-count reduction translates near-linearly into time savings. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Converting between seconds and minutes inconsistently in cost calculations. *Always* convert time to minutes when multiplying by ₹/min labor rate. > 2. **Forgetting the overhead factor:** Many students compute $C_{asm} = t \times C_{labor}$ and miss the $(1 + O)$ multiplier. KTU examiners specifically test this in 7-mark problems. > 3. **Confusing $N_{min}$ and $N_{actual}$:** $N_{min}$ is the count *after* applying the three-question test (the irreducible minimum). $N_{actual}$ is the count in the *current proposed* design. $E$ will exceed 100% if you swap them — a red-flag for the examiner. > 4. **Skipping the per-part time sum:** When given a table of $t_h, t_i, t_s$ per part, you must sum them *per part* first, then aggregate. Skipping this step loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Not drawing a flow diagram in 7-mark DFA methodology questions:** Even a simple labeled block diagram earns 2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly reward visual content in design questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design for Assembly: Introduction** - **DFA** = Design for Assembly — a *concurrent engineering* approach that optimizes products for ease of assembly at the design stage itself. - **Boothroyd-Dewhurst** is the most widely used DFA method globally. - The **Three-Question Test** is the core decision rule: (1) Does it move? (2) Different material? (3) Must be separable for service? A **NO** to all three = **eliminate/merge** the part. - **Design Efficiency** $E = \frac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$. Target: $E \geq 60\%$; world-class: $E \geq 70\%$. - **Total Assembly Time** $t_{total} = \sum (t_h + t_i + t_s)$ across all parts. - **Total Assembly Cost** $C_{asm} = t_{total,\min} \times C_{labor} \times (1 + O)$. - **Three DFA operations:** Handling → Insertion → Securing. - **Industry rule of thumb:** A 1% reduction in part count yields a 2%–3% reduction in assembly cost. - **Hitachi method** = motion-level granularity; **Lucas method** = cost-focused; **Boothroyd** = balanced and dominant. - **Snap-fits, self-locating features, and self-fastening parts** all reduce $t_s$ (securing time). - **DFA bridges DFM and DFE** (Design for the Environment) in the broader DFMA framework. - For 14-mark KTU Part B questions: always draw a flow diagram of the DFA methodology, show the formula, and present the numerical answer with units. - For 3-mark Part A questions: definitions, the three-question test, and the Design Efficiency formula are the most frequently asked topics. ---

topics.title: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET755 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
