# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fastener Selection Hierarchy Flowchart

The most important flowchart for this topic — it dictates the DFMA decision logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Need to Join Two Parts] --> B{Does Joint Need Disassembly for Service?}
    B -- NO --> C{Is Joint Permanent Acceptable?}
    C -- YES --> D[Use Weld or Adhesive Bond]
    C -- NO --> E[Use Snap-Fit or Integral Clip]
    B -- YES --> F{Is Disassembly Frequent or Rare?}
    F -- RARE --> G{Is Single-Sided Access OK?}
    G -- YES --> H[Use Blind Rivet or Self-Piercing Rivet]
    G -- NO --> I[Use Through-Bolt with Nut]
    F -- FREQUENT --> J{Need to Retain Fastener?}
    J -- YES --> K[Use Captive Fastener Cage Nut Stud]
    J -- NO --> L[Use Standard Bolt Screw]
    L --> M[Apply Standardization and Tool Access Rules]
    K --> M
    I --> M
    H --> M
    D --> N[Final Design with Eliminated Fasteners]
    E --> N
    M --> O[Final Design with Optimized Fasteners]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The terminal nodes (`N` and `O`) represent the optimized states.
- `N` is the **ideal DFMA outcome** (fastener eliminated).
- `O` represents the **acceptable compromise** (fastener retained but optimized).

---

## 4.2 Captive Fastener Types — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Because the captive fastener family is large, a physical drawing would be cluttered. We instead map the **functional architecture** showing how each type achieves retention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RetentionFamily[Captive Fastener Architecture]
        direction LR
        F1[Function: Retain Nut or Stud]
        F2[Function: Allow ±2 mm Float]
        F3[Function: Single-Sided Assembly]
    end

    F1 --> M1[Cage Nut: Spring Steel Cage Welded to Panel]
    F1 --> M2[Clinch Nut: Pressed into Stamped Hole]
    F1 --> M3[Weld Nut: Projection Welded to Panel]

    F2 --> M4[Floating Nut: Slides in Sheet Metal Slot]
    F2 --> M5[Spring Nut: Spring-Loaded Floating Element]

    F3 --> M6[Blind Rivet Nut: Single-Side Installation]
    F3 --> M7[Self-Tapping Screw: Forms Thread in Plastic or Sheet]

    M1 -.orientation: requires 2-sided.-> F3
    M2 -.orientation: requires 2-sided.-> F3
    M3 -.orientation: requires 2-sided.-> F3
    M4 -.provides ±1 to ±2 mm float.-> F2
    M5 -.provides ±2 to ±5 mm float.-> F2
    M6 -.single-sided suitable for closed sections.-> F3
    M7 -.single-sided no nut required.-> F3
```

**Functional Reading:** The matrix shows that each captive fastener type is optimized for **specific geometric constraints** (orientation, float, single-sided access). The designer selects based on the **most binding constraint**.

---

## 4.3 Tool Clearance Envelope Diagram

The clearance envelope around a bolt head dictates whether an assembly is feasible.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bolt Head on Panel] --> B[Inner Clearance: Wrench AF Width]
    B --> C[Outer Swing Radius: Wrench Sweep]
    C --> D[Ergonomic Hand Clearance: 5 to 10 mm]
    D --> E[Total Envelope: Defined by R_total]

    A -.vertical clearance required.-> F[Vertical Tool Clearance: 2x Wrench Length]
    F --> G[Check Adjacent Components for Interference]
    G --> H[Final Clearance Verified or Redesign Needed]

    E --> H
```

**Block-Level Functional Mapping of the Clearance Logic:**

| **Layer** | **Element** | **Geometric Constraint** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bolt head | $d_{head}$ per ISO 4014/4017 |
| 2 | Wrench across flats (A/F) | $A/F \approx 1.5 \cdot d_b$ |
| 3 | Wrench outside diameter | $\approx 2.5 \cdot d_b$ |
| 4 | Wrench swing radius (handle swept area) | $\approx 4$ to $5 \cdot d_b$ |
| 5 | Hand clearance | +5–10 mm |
| 6 | Vertical tool clearance | $\geq L_{wrench}$ |
| 7 | Adjacent part interference check | Must be **0 mm or positive clearance** |

---

## 4.4 Standardization Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Starts with N Fastener Locations] --> B{How Many Distinct Types and Sizes?}
    B -- 1 type --> C[Ideal: SI near 1.0]
    B -- 2 to 3 types --> D[Acceptable: Optimize with Captive Versions]
    B -- 4 or more types --> E[REDESIGN: Standardize]

    E --> F[Identify the Most Frequent Size X]
    F --> G[Replace Others with Size X]
    G --> H[Verify Strength at New Size]
    H --> I[Verify Tooling Single Size Capable]
    I --> J[Acceptable: Proceed]
    D --> J
    C --> J
```

**Numerical Mapping:**

Suppose current design has $k = 5$ types: M3 × 4, M4 × 8, M5 × 6, M6 × 3, M8 × 2 = 23 total.

$$SI = 1 - \frac{5-1}{k_{max,feasible}} = 1 - \frac{4}{5} = 0.20$$

After consolidating M3, M5, M8 (smaller counts) into M4 (most frequent):

- New: M4 × 18, M6 × 5 = 23 total, $k = 2$.

$$SI_{new} = 1 - \frac{2-1}{5} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

**SI improvement: 4× better.** This is a quantitative answer KTU rewards.

---

## 4.5 Bolt Assembly Time Breakdown Diagram

The figure shows how a single fastener's total insertion time is decomposed into Boothroyd's basic operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bin Retrieval] --> B[Orientation and Pose]
    B --> C[Alignment with Hole]
    C --> D[Insertion into Hole]
    D --> E[Thread Engagement Start]
    E --> F[Tightening or Torquing]
    F --> G[Tool Release and Reset]

    A -.2 to 3 seconds.-> H[Total: 12 to 25 s]
    B -.1 second.-> H
    C -.1 to 2 seconds.-> H
    D -.1 second.-> H
    E -.2 to 3 seconds.-> H
    F -.3 to 10 seconds.-> H
    G -.2 seconds.-> H
```

**Block Insight:** The **tightening step** dominates the time. DFMA recommendations target this step: power tools, pre-lubricated bolts, captive nuts reduce it dramatically.

---

## 4.6 Self-Locating and Chamfer Feature Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bolt Hole Without Chamfer] --> B[Manual Alignment Required]
    B --> C[Operator Tilts Bolt to Find Hole]
    C --> D[Time Penalty: +2 to +4 s]
    D --> E[Operator Fatigue and Error Risk]

    F[Bolt Hole With 30 deg to 45 deg Chamfer] --> G[Self-Centering Lead-In]
    G --> H[±3 deg Misalignment Tolerance]
    H --> I[Time Saved: 2 to 4 s per Fastener]
    I --> J[Lower Assembly Error Rate]

    A -.compare.-> F
```

**Quantitative Result:** For 20 fasteners, chamfering all holes saves **40–80 seconds** of assembly time — purely from a 30° chamfer costing essentially zero additional manufacturing cost.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]:**
**"List any three DFMA recommendations for fastener design with one-line justifications."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Standardize fastener sizes** — Reduces tool count, bin complexity, and operator confusion; improves assembly efficiency.
2. **Use captive fasteners (e.g., cage nuts, weld nuts)** — Eliminates separate bin handling of nuts, saving 4–6 seconds per fastener.
3. **Provide chamfered lead-ins on bolt holes** — Enables self-centering, reducing alignment time by 2–4 seconds per bolt.

*(Any three valid recommendations with one-line justifications — full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]:**
**"What is the theoretical minimum handling time per part in Boothroyd's DFA analysis? Why is this benchmark important for fastener analysis?"**

**Model Answer:**

The theoretical minimum handling time per part in Boothroyd's DFA methodology is **t_min = 3 seconds**. This benchmark is critical for fastener analysis because:

- It represents the **physiological lower limit** for an operator to grasp, orient, position, and release a part — including a fastener.
- It allows the calculation of the **Assembly Efficiency (η)** which quantifies how much "wasted" assembly time each fastener contributes.
- It enables comparison between fastener types (e.g., captive vs. loose) on an objective time scale.

*(2 marks for the value, 1 mark for the importance explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU Model – Module 4 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with sketches the **three types of captive fasteners** commonly used in sheet metal assembly. Discuss their DFMA advantages and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sub-assembly has **12 bolts** (M6) requiring an average of **18 seconds each** to insert. The same assembly, redesigned with **captive nuts and chamfered holes**, takes **8 seconds per fastener**. Calculate (i) the original and new total assembly time for fasteners, (ii) the assembly efficiency improvement, and (iii) the percentage time saving. Take $t_{min}$ = 3 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Captive Fastener Type 1: Cage Nut**

A cage nut is a sheet metal cage stamped from spring steel that holds a standard hex nut. The cage is **welded (resistance or projection-welded) or press-fit** to the mating panel. The nut is free to rotate within the cage but cannot fall out, even when the bolt is removed.

- **DFMA Advantage:** Allows ±1 mm positional tolerance between panels; nut is pre-positioned; saves bin retrieval and orientation time.
- **Application:** Automotive dashboard sub-assemblies, white goods chassis, electrical enclosures.

**Captive Fastener Type 2: Clinch Nut**

A clinch nut is a hex nut with **knurled or annular rings** on its body. It is pressed into a **pre-punched or stamped hexagonal hole** in the sheet metal. The material flows into the nut's annular grooves during pressing, creating a permanent mechanical lock.

- **DFMA Advantage:** Single-piece installation (no separate cage); high pull-out strength; nut cannot rotate.
- **Application:** High-volume sheet metal enclosures (e.g., server racks, control panels).

**Captive Fastener Type 3: Weld Nut**

A weld nut is a standard nut with **small embossed projections** on its base. It is positioned on the panel and **resistance projection-welded** in place (single-sided operation).

- **DFMA Advantage:** Permanent attachment suitable for high-stress joints; nut orientation cannot shift.
- **Application:** Automotive body-in-white, heavy machinery frames.

*(Any three types with advantages and applications — full 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $n_f = 12$ fasteners
- $t_{actual,old} = 18$ s/fastener
- $t_{actual,new} = 8$ s/fastener
- $t_{min} = 3$ s/fastener

**Step (i): Total Assembly Time**

Original total fastener time:

$$T_{old} = 12 \times 18 = 216 \; \text{seconds}$$

New total fastener time:

$$T_{new} = 12 \times 8 = 96 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Step (ii): Assembly Efficiency Improvement**

Original efficiency:

$$\eta_{old} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_{min}}{T_{ma}} = \frac{12 \times 3}{216} = \frac{36}{216} = 0.167 \; (16.7\%)$$

New efficiency:

$$\eta_{new} = \frac{12 \times 3}{96} = \frac{36}{96} = 0.375 \; (37.5\%)$$

Efficiency improvement:

$$\Delta \eta = 0.375 - 0.167 = 0.208 \; \text{(absolute)} \; \rightarrow \; \frac{0.208}{0.167} = 124\% \; \text{relative gain}$$

**Step (iii): Percentage Time Saving**

$$\% \; \text{Saving} = \frac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{216 - 96}{216} \times 100 = 55.56\%$$

**Answer:** 
- (i) $T_{old} = 216$ s, $T_{new} = 96$ s.
- (ii) Efficiency improved from 16.7% to 37.5% (a 2.25× increase).
- (iii) Time saving of **55.56%** (~2 minutes per assembly unit).

*(7 marks — awarded for correct values, formulas cited, and final percentage stated.)*

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU Model – Module 4 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd Bolalian Analysis** for fasteners. Describe the three screening questions and the time-penalty formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly has **20 fasteners** in an enclosed housing where **none of the three Bolalian criteria are satisfied** (all three answers are "No"). (i) Compute the actual assembly time per fastener using Boothroyd's formula. (ii) If the design is modified so that **all three criteria are satisfied**, calculate the new time. (iii) What is the cost impact if the company produces **50,000 units/year** and the labor cost is **₹2 per minute**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Bolalian Analysis Definition:**

The **Bolalian (Bolt + Analysis) Worksheet** is a Boothroyd DFA screening tool applied to every fastener in an assembly. It evaluates each fastener against three questions to determine whether the design supports efficient insertion.

**The Three Screening Questions:**

| **Question** | **Pass Condition** | **Why It Matters** |
|---|---|---|
| **Q1: Symmetry of insertion** | The fastener can be inserted from either end without preference | Reduces orientation time; one stroke, no flip |
| **Q2: One-sided accessibility** | The fastener can be inserted from the preferred side without obstruction | Avoids blind alignment; tool swings freely |
| **Q3: Self-alignment** | The fastener self-locates (chamfer, lead-in, captive feature) | Eliminates manual alignment effort |

**Time-Penalty Formula:**

Each "No" answer doubles the theoretical handling time:

$$\boxed{t_{actual} = t_{theoretical} \times 2^{N_{No}}}$$

Where:
- $t_{theoretical}$ = Boothroyd's minimum (3 s)
- $N_{No}$ = number of "No" answers (0, 1, 2, or 3)

**Implication:** A fastener that fails all three tests takes **8× longer** than a fully compliant one ($t = 24$ s vs. $t = 3$ s).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $n_f = 20$ fasteners
- $N_{No} = 3$ (original design)
- $N_{No} = 0$ (redesigned)
- $t_{min} = 3$ s
- Annual volume: 50,000 units
- Labor cost: ₹2 per minute

**Step (i): Original Time per Fastener**

$$t_{old} = 3 \times 2^3 = 3 \times 8 = 24 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Step (ii): Redesigned Time per Fastener**

$$t_{new} = 3 \times 2^0 = 3 \times 1 = 3 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Step (iii): Cost Impact**

Time saved per fastener:

$$\Delta t = 24 - 3 = 21 \; \text{seconds}$$

Time saved per assembly unit (20 fasteners):

$$\Delta T_{unit} = 20 \times 21 = 420 \; \text{seconds} = 7 \; \text{minutes}$$

Annual savings (50,000 units):

$$\Delta T_{annual} = 50{,}000 \times 7 = 350{,}000 \; \text{minutes}$$

Annual cost saving:

$$\text{Saving} = 350{,}000 \times ₹2 = ₹7{,}00{,}000 \; \text{(₹7 lakh per year)}$$

**Answer:**
- (i) $t_{old} = 24$ s
- (ii) $t_{new} = 3$ s
- (iii) **Annual cost saving = ₹7,00,000** (Rupees Seven Lakhs) — purely from DFMA redesign.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $N_{No}$ count.** Many students compute only one fastener and stop. **Always multiply by the number of fasteners in the assembly.**
> 2. **Using $t_{min} = 5$ s or $t_{min} = 1$ s.** KTU strictly expects **3 seconds** as the Boothroyd baseline. Using any other value costs marks.
> 3. **Unit confusion in cost calculations.** Convert seconds to minutes **before** multiplying by labor rate. A common error is multiplying seconds by ₹/min, which produces a wrong answer 60× off.
> 4. **Ignoring the "captive fastener" benefit in captive scenarios.** If the question says captive fasteners are used, the new time should be **less than the no-change case**, even if $N_{No}$ is unchanged.
> 5. **Skipping the percentage saving calculation.** Even if numerical time is computed, KTU Part B often demands **percentage saving or relative efficiency** as a separate answer.
> 6. **Omitting a sketch in Part (a) descriptive questions.** Even a hand-drawn schematic of cage nut or clinch nut earns extra credit. Always include a labeled diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read Twice Before Exam**

- ✅ **Boothroyd's minimum handling time per part** = **3 seconds** (always).
- ✅ **Total fastener time = sum** of handling + insertion + fastening + tool time for **each** fastener.
- ✅ **Assembly efficiency** $\eta = N_m \cdot t_{min} / T_{ma}$ — **closer to 1.0 is better**.
- ✅ **First DFMA priority = ELIMINATE** the fastener; second = INTEGRATE; third = REDUCE; fourth = STANDARDIZE.
- ✅ **Three Bolalian questions**: Symmetry, Access, Alignment. **Each "No" doubles** the handling time.
- ✅ **Time-penalty formula**: $t_{actual} = t_{theoretical} \cdot 2^{N_{No}}$.
- ✅ **Captive fasteners save** ~4–6 s per fastener by eliminating bin retrieval and orientation.
- ✅ **Standardize to ≤ 3 distinct fastener types** in any product (DFMA ideal).
- ✅ **Hole clearance**: ISO fits H11, H12, H13 — **know the values for M6, M8, M10** by heart.
- ✅ **Thread engagement length**: $1.5 d_b$ (steel), $2.0 d_b$ (cast iron), $2.5 d_b$ (aluminum).
- ✅ **Bolt tightening torque**: $T = K \cdot d_b \cdot F_{preload}$, with **K ≈ 0.20** for plain steel.
- ✅ **Wrench clearance**: Total swing radius ≈ **4 to 5 × bolt diameter**; vertical tool clearance ≥ **wrench length**.
- ✅ **Chamfer the bolt entry hole**: 30°–45° angle, depth = $0.5 \cdot d_{hole}$ for self-centering.
- ✅ **Avoid mixing fastener types and sizes** — it increases tooling cost and operator confusion.
- ✅ **Prefer screws going into tapped holes** over bolts + nuts — one fewer part to handle.
- ✅ **Avoid dissimilar metals** (galvanic corrosion risk) — use plated or insulated fasteners if unavoidable.
- ✅ **Captive fastener families to know for diagrams**: Cage nut, Clinch nut, Weld nut, Floating nut, Blind rivet nut, Self-tapping screw.
- ✅ **Fastener fraction target**: $n_f / N_{total} < 10\%$ — ideal DFA metric.
- ✅ **Use the hierarchy flowchart** in Section 4.1 as a decision-making template for any fastener question.
- ✅ **Always justify with η or % time saving** in numerical answers — KTU rewards quantitative reasoning.
